The Riemann tensor vanishes if and only if the manifold is flat (locally
isometric to Euclidean space). When it is nonzero, it encodes the
path-dependence of parallel transport: transporting a vector V around an
infinitesimal parallelogram spanned by X and Y yields a change
proportional to R(X, Y)V.

\textbf{Contractions of the Riemann tensor} yield lower-rank curvature
tensors: - The \emph{Ricci tensor}: R\_\{ij\} = R\^{}k\{\}\emph{\{ikj\}
(a symmetric (0, 2)-tensor) - The scalar curvature: R = g\^{}\{ij\}
R}\{ij\} (a single number summarizing overall curvature at each point)

Whether moral space has nonzero curvature is an empirical question ---
one of the open problems in Chapter 29. But the framework is equipped to
handle it if it does.

\subsection{4.7 Fiber Bundles}\label{fiber-bundles}

\subsubsection{Motivation}\label{motivation}

\textbar{} Agent Fiber Bundle

\subsubsection{Definition}\label{definition-2}

\textbf{Definition 4.6 (Fiber Bundle).} A \emph{fiber bundle} is a
triple (E, π, M) where: - E is a topological space called the
\emph{total space} - M is a topological space called the \emph{base
space} - π: E → M is a continuous surjection called the
\emph{projection} - For each p ∈ M, the \emph{fiber} F\_p = π⁻¹(p) is
homeomorphic to a fixed space F (the \emph{typical fiber}) - \emph{Local
triviality}: Each point p has a neighborhood U such that π⁻¹(U) ≅ U × F

The total space E looks locally like a product M × F, but may be
``twisted'' globally --- like a Möbius strip, which locally looks like a
strip of paper but has a global twist.

\textbf{Example (Tangent Bundle).} The tangent bundle TM is a fiber
bundle with base space M, total space TM, and fiber ℝⁿ (the tangent
space at each point).

\textbf{Example (Frame Bundle).} At each point p, the \emph{frame
bundle} consists of all ordered bases for T\_pM. The fiber is GL(n, ℝ)
--- the group of invertible n × n matrices.

\subsubsection{Sections}\label{sections}

A \emph{section} of a fiber bundle is a smooth map σ: M → E such that
π(σ(p)) = p for all p --- it selects one element from each fiber. A
vector field is a section of the tangent bundle. A metric tensor is a
section of the bundle of symmetric (0, 2)-tensors.

\textbf{Moral interpretation:} Consider a bundle over moral space M
whose fiber at each point p is the space of \emph{possible moral
character traits} available to an agent in situation p.~A \emph{virtue}
is then a section of this bundle: a consistent assignment of character
to each situation. The requirement that virtue be \emph{consistent} ---
that it not jump discontinuously --- is the requirement that the section
be smooth. Virtue ethics, in this language, is the study of sections of
the character fiber bundle.

\subsection{4.8 Stratified Spaces}\label{stratified-spaces}

\subsubsection{Beyond Smooth Manifolds}\label{beyond-smooth-manifolds}

Smooth manifolds are fundamental, but they are not sufficient for moral
space. Manifolds are, by definition, uniform in dimension: every point
has a neighborhood homeomorphic to ℝⁿ for the \emph{same} n.~But moral
space has regions of different effective dimensions (the interior of the
simplex has dimension 2; its edges have dimension 1; its vertices have
dimension 0), separated by boundaries where the smooth structure
changes.

For this, we need \emph{stratified spaces}.

\subsubsection{Definition}\label{definition-3}

\textbf{Definition 4.7 (Whitney Stratification).} A \emph{Whitney
stratified space} is a topological space X together with a locally
finite partition into smooth manifolds \{S\_α\}\_\{α ∈ A\} (called
\emph{strata}) such that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Frontier condition:} If S\_α ∩ cl(S\_β) ≠ ∅ and S\_α ≠ S\_β,
  then S\_α ⊂ cl(S\_β) and dim(S\_α) \textless{} dim(S\_β).
\item
  \textbf{Whitney's condition (B):} For strata S\_α ⊂ cl(S\_β), consider
  sequences \{x\_n\} ⊂ S\_β and \{y\_n\} ⊂ S\_α both converging to y ∈
  S\_α. If the secant lines ℓ\_n = x\_n y\_n converge to a line ℓ, and
  the tangent planes T\_\{x\_n\} S\_β converge to a plane τ, then ℓ ⊂ τ.
\end{enumerate}

Condition (1) says that lower-dimensional strata lie on the boundaries
of higher-dimensional strata --- they are genuinely ``boundary''
structures, not isolated points floating in space. Condition (2) is a
regularity condition ensuring that higher-dimensional strata approach
lower-dimensional strata in a controlled way --- no ``pinching'' or
pathological tangencies.

\subsubsection{Example: The Simplex}\label{example-the-simplex}

The 2-simplex Δ² has a natural Whitney stratification: -
\textbf{2-stratum} (interior): \{(p₁, p₂, p₃) : all pᵢ \textgreater{}
0\}, a 2-dimensional manifold. - \textbf{1-strata} (edges): Three edges,
each a 1-dimensional manifold. - \textbf{0-strata} (vertices): Three
vertices, each a 0-dimensional manifold (a point).

The frontier condition holds: each vertex is in the closure of each
adjacent edge, and each edge is in the closure of the interior.
Whitney's condition (B) holds because the simplex is a convex polytope.

\subsubsection{Moral Significance}\label{moral-significance}

Stratification is the geometric structure that represents moral
\emph{thresholds}, \emph{phase transitions}, and \emph{categorical
boundaries}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The transition from ``permissible'' to ``forbidden'' is a stratum
  boundary.
\item
  The transition from ``one duty applies'' to ``a different duty
  applies'' is a stratum boundary.
\item
  The point where ``consent'' shifts from insufficient to sufficient is
  a stratum boundary.
\item
  A genuine dilemma --- where two strata of different moral regimes meet
  at a singularity --- is a 0-stratum at the intersection of
  higher-dimensional boundaries.
\end{itemize}

Within each stratum, smooth calculus applies: we can take derivatives,
define gradients, and optimize. \emph{Across} stratum boundaries, we
need the machinery of stratified spaces to track how the structure
changes. The transition functions between strata encode the ``rules of
passage'' from one moral regime to another --- and these transitions may
be discontinuous.

\subsection{4.9 Gauge Theory: A Brief
Introduction}\label{gauge-theory-a-brief-introduction}

\subsubsection{Motivation}\label{motivation-1}

Gauge theory is the mathematical framework that describes how local
symmetries constrain global structure. In physics, it governs all four
fundamental forces. In this book, it provides the mathematical content
of the Bond Invariance Principle: the requirement that moral evaluations
be invariant under admissible re-descriptions.

\subsubsection{Gauge Transformations}\label{gauge-transformations}

Consider a fiber bundle (E, π, M) with structure group G --- a Lie group
that acts on each fiber. A \emph{gauge transformation} is a smooth map
g: M → G that acts on sections by:

\[\sigma(p) \mapsto g(p) \cdot \sigma(p)\]

In physics, gauge transformations change the ``internal'' description at
each point (e.g., the phase of a quantum field) without changing the
physics. In ethics, gauge transformations change the
\emph{representation} of a moral situation (the framing, the language,
the cultural context) without changing the moral content.

\textbf{Definition 4.8 (Gauge Invariance).} A quantity Q defined on
sections of a fiber bundle is \emph{gauge-invariant} if Q(g · σ) = Q(σ)
for all gauge transformations g.

The Bond Invariance Principle states that legitimate moral evaluations
must be gauge-invariant: if two descriptions are related by an
admissible re-description (a gauge transformation), they must receive
the same moral assessment.

\subsubsection{Connections on Fiber
Bundles}\label{connections-on-fiber-bundles}

A \emph{connection} on a fiber bundle generalizes the affine connection
of Section 4.6 to arbitrary fibers. It specifies how to
parallel-transport elements of the fiber along paths in the base space.

In coordinates, a connection is given by a \emph{gauge field} A --- a
1-form on M valued in the Lie algebra 𝔤 of the structure group G:

\[A = A_{\mu}^{a}T_{a}\, dx^{\mu}\]

where T\_a are generators of 𝔤. The gauge field tells us how the
``internal'' structure (the fiber) changes as we move through the base
space.

\subsubsection{Curvature of a
Connection}\label{curvature-of-a-connection}

The \emph{curvature} of a gauge connection is the 2-form:

\[F = dA + A \land A\]

In components:

\[F_{\mu\nu}^{a} = \partial_{\mu}A_{\nu}^{a} - \partial_{\nu}A_{\mu}^{a} + f_{bc}^{a}A_{\mu}^{b}A_{\nu}^{c}\]

where f\^{}a\{\}\_\{bc\} are the structure constants of the Lie algebra.
The curvature measures the failure of parallel transport to be
path-independent --- the holonomy of the connection.

If F = 0, the connection is \emph{flat} and parallel transport is
path-independent. If F ≠ 0, different paths between the same endpoints
yield different results.

\textbf{Moral interpretation:} Moral curvature F ≠ 0 means that
transporting a moral assessment along different paths through moral
space yields different results. The obligation you arrive at depends on
the route you took --- which intermediate contexts you passed through.
This is the geometric content of moral path-dependence, and it is
distinct from mere disagreement: it is a structural feature of the moral
landscape itself.

\subsubsection{Noether's Theorem
(Preview)}\label{noethers-theorem-preview}

A central result of gauge theory is \emph{Noether's theorem}: every
continuous symmetry of a system corresponds to a conserved quantity. The
connection to ethics is developed in Chapter 12, but the basic structure
is:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Symmetry:} Moral evaluation is invariant under re-description
  (gauge invariance).
\item
  \textbf{Conservation law:} Harm is conserved --- it cannot be created
  or destroyed by re-description, only generated by wrongdoing or
  repaired by restorative action.
\end{itemize}

The mathematical proof follows the same pattern as in physics: the
invariance of the action functional under infinitesimal gauge
transformations implies, via the Euler-Lagrange equations, the existence
of a conserved current.

\subsection{4.10 Summary: The Geometric
Toolkit}\label{summary-the-geometric-toolkit}

The table below summarizes the mathematical structures introduced in
this chapter and previews their moral applications (developed in
subsequent chapters).

\subsection{4.11 Bibliographic Notes}\label{bibliographic-notes}

The differential geometry in Sections 4.1--4.6 is standard and can be
found in Lee (2012), \emph{Introduction to Smooth Manifolds}, which is
the gentlest rigorous introduction, or do Carmo (1992), \emph{Riemannian
Geometry}, for the metric and curvature theory. Readers seeking physical
intuition may prefer Frankel (2011), \emph{The Geometry of Physics}.

For fiber bundles and gauge theory (Section 4.9), Nakahara (2003),
\emph{Geometry, Topology, and Physics}, provides an accessible treatment
aimed at physicists. The mathematical foundations are in Kobayashi and
Nomizu (1963), \emph{Foundations of Differential Geometry}, though this
is aimed at specialists.

For stratified spaces (Section 4.8), the original source is Whitney
(1965). An accessible modern treatment is Pflaum (2001), \emph{Analytic
and Geometric Study of Stratified Spaces}. The application to moral
space is, to our knowledge, original to this work and to the companion
paper on Stratified Geometric Ethics (Bond 2025).

Readers who wish to go deeper before proceeding should read Lee (2012),
Chapters 1--6, which covers manifolds, tangent spaces, and vector
bundles at the level assumed in subsequent chapters.

❖

\emph{The mathematics in this chapter is not new.}

\emph{It is the standard toolkit of modern geometry, developed over two
centuries by mathematicians working on problems in physics, topology,
and analysis.}

\emph{What is new is the application.}

\emph{In the next chapter, we build the moral manifold --- the specific
space on which geometric ethics takes shape.}

\section{Chapter 5: The Moral
Manifold}\label{chapter-5-the-moral-manifold}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya maps TrialMatch onto the 3×3 moral manifold. For the
BEACON-7 match, μ = 1 (Empirical--Deontic): HealthBridge has a
contractual duty to match using validated criteria---an observable fact.
μ = 4 (Normative--Deontic): the algorithm ought to give rural patients a
fair chance, but `fair' is undefined in the codebase. μ = 6
(Normative--Consequential): matching more rural patients should improve
trial generalizability. μ = 9 (Meta--Consequential): what theory of
value grounds the assumption that statistical power is good? She fills
in all nine cells and realizes TrialMatch uses at most three dimensions
and ignores the other six entirely. Six dimensions of moral reality,
invisible to her algorithm.}
\end{quote}

\subsection{5.1 The Question of Base
Space}\label{the-question-of-base-space}

Every tensor lives on a manifold. The stress tensor in materials science
lives on the manifold of spatial points within a body. The metric tensor
in general relativity lives on the manifold of spacetime events. The
electromagnetic field tensor lives on the four-dimensional manifold of
spacetime, and the gauge connection lives on a fiber bundle over it. The
question for geometric ethics is: \emph{what is the manifold on which
moral tensors are defined?}

This is not a technical detail. The choice of base space determines:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  What counts as a ``point'' in moral reasoning
\item
  What counts as a ``direction'' --- what the tangent vectors are
\item
  What transformations are admissible and which quantities are invariant
\item
  What it means for two moral situations to be ``nearby'' or ``far
  apart''
\item
  Where the smooth structure breaks down --- where discontinuities,
  boundaries, and singularities occur
\end{itemize}

This chapter develops the concept of the \emph{moral manifold} --- the
space of morally relevant configurations over which ethical tensors are
defined. We build on the mathematical toolkit of Chapter 4, but proceed
with philosophical care, distinguishing geometric structure from
metaphysical commitment.

\subsection{5.2 What Are the Points?}\label{what-are-the-points}

The first question is fundamental: what are the ``points'' of moral
space? Several candidates present themselves, each with distinctive
strengths and limitations.

\subsubsection{Candidate 1: Possible
Actions}\label{candidate-1-possible-actions}

On this view, the moral manifold M is the space of possible actions
available to an agent. Each point x ∈ M represents a complete
specification of what the agent does --- not just ``help the neighbor''
but a full description including manner, timing, motivation, and
foreseeable consequences.

\textbf{Strengths:} This connects directly to the practical question of
ethics (``what should I do?''). The gradient of the satisfaction
function, ∇S, points toward the best available action.

\textbf{Weaknesses:} Actions are agent-relative. My action space differs
from yours. This makes interpersonal comparison difficult and global
structure unclear. Moreover, the space may not be a manifold in any
natural sense --- discrete choices (accept or decline) do not admit
smooth interpolation.

\subsubsection{Candidate 2: States of
Affairs}\label{candidate-2-states-of-affairs}

On this view, points are possible states of the world --- complete
descriptions of how things are or could be. Actions are then paths or
vectors, not points: an action takes the world from one state to
another.

\textbf{Strengths:} This is agent-neutral. All agents evaluate the same
space. It connects to consequentialist intuitions: what matters is the
state of the world, not who brings it about.

\textbf{Weaknesses:} The space is enormous --- potentially
infinite-dimensional. And it privileges states over processes, which
deontologists and care ethicists will resist, since the moral
significance of \emph{how} one arrives at a state is lost.

\subsubsection{Candidate 3: Situations}\label{candidate-3-situations}

A \emph{situation} is richer than a state: it includes not just how
things are, but the relationships between agents, their histories, their
commitments, and the options available. A situation is something like:
``Alice has promised Bob X. Bob is in need of Y. Alice can provide Y but
only by breaking the promise to Carol. Dana is observing.''

\textbf{Strengths:} This captures the morally relevant features without
requiring a full specification of the universe. It is closer to how
moral reasoning actually works --- what matters is the structured
configuration of agents, stakes, and relationships.

\textbf{Weaknesses:} What counts as ``morally relevant'' is
theory-dependent. Different ethical theories may carve situation-space
differently. The category of ``morally relevant features'' requires
principled delimitation.

\subsubsection{Candidate 4: Agent-Situation
Pairs}\label{candidate-4-agent-situation-pairs}

On this view, a point is a pair (a, s): an agent a in a situation s.
This allows agent-relative evaluations while maintaining a common
framework.

\textbf{Strengths:} It captures both partiality and impartiality as
different operations on the space --- holding a fixed (what should
\emph{I} do in this situation?) vs.~varying a (what should \emph{anyone}
do?). It connects naturally to the agent-indexed tensors of Chapter 6
and to the fiber bundle structure where agent-perspectives form the
fiber over a base situation.

\textbf{Weaknesses:} More complex structure; requires specifying how the
agent index interacts with the situation index.

\subsubsection{Our Choice: Structured
Situations}\label{our-choice-structured-situations}

For the purposes of this book, we take the moral manifold M to be a
space of \emph{structured situations} --- specifications of:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The agents involved and their relationships
\item
  The options available to each agent
\item
  The morally relevant features of the context (needs, promises,
  histories, stakes)
\item
  The epistemic state (what is known, by whom)
\end{enumerate}

This is deliberately ecumenical. A consequentialist can project onto
states; a deontologist can focus on the structure of relationships and
commitments; a virtue ethicist can attend to the character of the
agents; a care ethicist can foreground the relational structure. The
manifold M is common ground; different theories correspond to different
tensors, metrics, and contractions \emph{on} M.

\textbf{Definition 5.1 (Moral Manifold, Informal). The moral manifold M
is a paracompact Hausdorff topological space (see Definition A.1 for the
formal construction) whose points represent structured moral situations
--- complete specifications of agents, relationships, options, and
morally relevant context.}

Agent-perspectives enter as additional structure \emph{over} M ---
specifically, as a fiber bundle whose base is the space of situations
and whose fiber at each point is the space of agent-perspectives
available in that situation. This separation of base from fiber allows
us to ask independently: ``What is the situation?'' (a question about M)
and ``Who is evaluating?'' (a question about the fiber). We develop this
in Section 5.4.

{[}Modeling choice.{]} The moral manifold M is a model, not a discovery.
Its justification is pragmatist: the nine-dimensional structure
converges across cultures and languages (the cross-lingual evidence of
§17.7 shows stable dimensional weights in 11 languages spanning 3,000
years), it arises from structural necessity (any framework satisfying
the three axioms of Chapter 9 produces an equivalent space), and it
generates testable predictions that scalar alternatives cannot (the
order-effect and semantic-gate results of §17.9--17.10). The
dimensionality --- nine, not seven or twelve --- is a modeling choice
whose adequacy is empirical. If future evidence reveals a moral
phenomenon that the nine dimensions cannot represent, the manifold must
be extended. If evidence shows that some dimensions are redundant, it
must be reduced. The modeling-choice label on Axiom 5.1 applies equally
here: the manifold is posited, not proved, and its warrant is that it
works.

\subsection{5.3 The Dimension of Moral
Space}\label{the-dimension-of-moral-space}

\subsubsection{The Dimensionality
Question}\label{the-dimensionality-question}

Having established what the points of M are, we must ask: what is the
\emph{dimension} of M? That is, how many independent directions of moral
variation exist at each point?

This is not a question the mathematics can answer by itself. It is a
substantive question about the structure of moral reasoning --- one that
admits of empirical investigation. But we can approach it
systematically.

\subsubsection{The 3 × 3 Derivation}\label{the-3-3-derivation}

A principled argument for the dimensionality of the moral manifold
proceeds from two independently motivated classifications.

\textbf{Three ethical scopes} capture whose interests are at stake:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Individual} --- the focal agent's own stakes, autonomy, and
  epistemic position
\item
  \textbf{Relational} --- the structured obligations, claims, and care
  relationships between specific parties
\item
  \textbf{Collective} --- the aggregate effects on groups, institutions,
  and the environment
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Three epistemic modes} capture what kind of moral question is
being asked:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{What Matters} --- the substantive values at stake (welfare,
  rights, justice)
\item
  \textbf{Who Decides} --- the procedural and agentive questions
  (autonomy, legitimacy, virtue)
\item
  \textbf{What We Know} --- the epistemic conditions (certainty,
  privacy, evidence)
\end{enumerate}

Crossing these two classifications yields a 3 × 3 matrix of nine cells:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1352}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2709}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2415}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3524}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What Matters}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Who Decides}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What We Know}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Individual} & Consequences \& Welfare & Autonomy \& Agency &
Epistemic Status \\
\textbf{Relational} & Rights \& Duties & Virtue \& Care & Privacy \&
Data Governance \\
\textbf{Collective} & Justice \& Fairness & Procedural Legitimacy &
Societal \& Environmental Impact \\
\end{longtable}
}

Each cell identifies an independent dimension of moral evaluation:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Consequences and Welfare} (Individual × What Matters): Who
  benefits, who is harmed, at what scale?
\item
  \textbf{Rights and Duties} (Relational × What Matters): What claims,
  obligations, and permissions exist?
\item
  \textbf{Justice and Fairness} (Collective × What Matters): Is the
  distribution of benefits and burdens equitable?
\item
  \textbf{Autonomy and Agency} (Individual × Who Decides): Are
  individuals free to make their own choices?
\item
  \textbf{Privacy and Data Governance} (Relational × What We Know): Is
  personal information appropriately protected?
\item
  \textbf{Societal and Environmental Impact} (Collective × What We
  Know): What are the aggregate and ecological effects?
\item
  \textbf{Virtue and Care} (Relational × Who Decides): What does this
  reflect about the agent's character and relationships?
\item
  \textbf{Procedural Legitimacy} (Collective × Who Decides): Was the
  process fair, transparent, and accountable?
\item
  \textbf{Epistemic Status} (Individual × What We Know): How confident
  are we? What don't we know?
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Arguments for Necessity and
Sufficiency}\label{arguments-for-necessity-and-sufficiency}

\textbf{Necessity.} Each cell of the matrix is logically independent of
the others. Consequences can be positive while rights are violated (a
beneficent paternalism). Rights can be respected while justice is denied
(each individual treated correctly, but the aggregate distribution is
skewed). Autonomy can be preserved while epistemic conditions are poor
(free choice based on bad information). No cell is reducible to a
combination of others.

\textbf{Sufficiency.} The claim is that these nine dimensions
\emph{subsume} existing ethical frameworks. The EU High-Level Expert
Group's seven requirements for trustworthy AI map onto the nine
dimensions without residue. The Markkula Center's five ethical lenses
map onto them. Beauchamp and Childress's four principles of biomedical
ethics map onto them. This subsumption is not proof of completeness, but
it provides strong evidence that the nine dimensions capture the space
of considerations that diverse ethical traditions have independently
identified as morally relevant.

\textbf{Falsifiability.} The completeness claim is falsifiable: produce
a genuine moral consideration that is (a) independent of all nine
dimensions and (b) not a combination of them. If such a consideration
exists, the framework must be extended. This is the appropriate
epistemic stance toward a structural claim: hold it provisionally, test
it against cases, and revise if necessary.

The status of the number nine. The nine dimensions are a canonical
engineering chart, derived from the 3x3 scope/mode grid for coverage and
auditability - not a discovered empirical dimensionality. We do not
claim nine is the unique or minimal basis: a learned latent
representation, or a factor analysis of moral judgments, may recover
fewer semi-independent factors, and we expect at least six. Recovering
fewer than six would falsify the coverage claim; but the specific count
of nine is a design choice, chosen so that each dimension maps to an
established tradition - consequences and welfare (consequentialism),
rights and duties (deontology and Hohfeldian jural relations), justice
and fairness (Rawls, Sen), autonomy and agency, privacy and data,
societal and environmental impact (capability and sustainability
theories), virtue and care (virtue and care ethics), procedural
legitimacy (institutional theory), and epistemic status. Its
justification is pragmatic coverage, not metaphysical necessity; where a
tradition emphasizes a distinction the chart blurs, that is a limitation
of the chart, not a claim about morality.

\subsubsection{Formal Statement}\label{formal-statement}

\textbf{Definition 5.2 (Dimension of the Moral Manifold). The moral
manifold M has nine independent dimensions at generic (top-stratum)
points; lower-dimensional strata may have fewer active dimensions. In
local coordinates, a point p ∈ M can be parameterized by}

\[p = (x^{1},x^{2},\ldots,x^{9})\]

\emph{where x\^{}μ (μ = 1, \ldots, 9) represents the state of the
situation along the μ-th moral dimension. The tangent space T\_pM at a
generic point is a nine-dimensional real vector space.}

We say ``at least'' because domain-specific refinements may introduce
additional dimensions (the medical context adds benefit scores, waiting
times, and clinical parameters that expand the local coordinate
description). And we say ``at generic points'' because at stratum
boundaries, the effective dimension may drop --- some directions of
variation cease to be available, as when an option becomes forbidden.

\subsubsection{The Varying Metric}\label{the-varying-metric}

A crucial consequence of the nine-dimensional structure: the metric on M
is not constant. Empirical data from the Dear Abby corpus (Chapter 17)
reveals that the \emph{relative weights} of dimensions shift by context:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  In \textbf{family} contexts, social bonds (Dimension 7) dominate: the
  metric assigns greater ``length'' to variations in relational care.
\item
  In \textbf{workplace} contexts, procedural legitimacy (Dimension 8)
  and fairness (Dimension 3) dominate.
\item
  In \textbf{neighbor} disputes, fairness (Dimension 3) and rights
  (Dimension 2) dominate.
\end{itemize}

This context-dependence of the metric is precisely what differential
geometry is designed to handle. The metric tensor g\_μν(p) is a
\emph{field} on M --- it varies from point to point. The fact that it
varies is not a complication but the central phenomenon: moral space is
curved, and the curvature encodes how the structure of moral evaluation
changes across contexts.

\subsection{5.4 The Differential
Structure}\label{the-differential-structure}

\subsubsection{Charts and Atlases on M}\label{charts-and-atlases-on-m}

Recall from Chapter 4 that a smooth manifold requires coordinate charts
that overlap smoothly. For the moral manifold, a chart assigns numerical
coordinates to a neighborhood of situations.

\textbf{Example.} In a neighborhood of a family dispute about holiday
hosting, local coordinates might be:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  x¹ = degree of welfare impact on the host (Dimension 1)
\item
  x² = strength of the host's duty to extended family (Dimension 2)
\item
  x³ = fairness of the hosting distribution over years (Dimension 3)
\item
  x⁴ = host's autonomy regarding their own time (Dimension 4)
\item
  x⁷ = quality of the care relationship with family members (Dimension
  7)
\end{itemize}

Not all nine dimensions need vary independently in every neighborhood.
Some may be locally constant (privacy may be irrelevant to this
particular dispute), reducing the effective local dimension. This is the
sense in which the moral manifold may have varying dimension across its
strata --- though within a single stratum, the dimension is constant.

\subsubsection{The Tangent Space of M}\label{the-tangent-space-of-m}

At each point p ∈ M, the tangent space T\_pM has basis vectors
\{∂/∂x\^{}μ\}\_μ=1,\ldots,n where n is the local dimension. Each basis
vector represents an independent direction of moral variation:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  ∂/∂x¹ = ``increase welfare impact, holding all else fixed''
\item
  ∂/∂x² = ``strengthen the duty, holding all else fixed''
\item
  ∂/∂x³ = ``make the distribution more equitable, holding all else
  fixed''
\end{itemize}

A moral vector --- an obligation, a gradient of satisfaction, a
direction of moral improvement --- is a linear combination of these
basis directions:

\[O = O^{\mu}\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\mu}}\]

The components O\^{}μ give the strength of the obligation \emph{in each
moral dimension}. This is the fundamental advantage over scalar ethics:
the obligation has direction, not just magnitude.

\subsubsection{The Agent Fiber Bundle}\label{the-agent-fiber-bundle}

Agent-perspectives enter as a fiber over M. At each situation p ∈ M, the
fiber F\_p consists of the available agent-perspectives in that
situation --- the physician, the patient, the family member, the
institutional review board.

\textbf{Definition 5.3 (Agent Bundle).} \emph{The agent bundle is a
fiber bundle (E, π, M) where the fiber F\_p = π\^{}\{-1\}(p) at each
point p ∈ M is the set of agent-perspectives available in situation p.}

Remark. The fiber F\_p varies with the point p: different moral
situations may involve different sets of available agent-perspectives.
Consequently, the agent bundle E is, in general, a fibered space rather
than a fiber bundle in the strict sense (the fibers need not be
diffeomorphic to a fixed model fiber F). The bundle structure is locally
trivial within each stratum --- situations in the same stratum have the
same structure of available perspectives --- but may change across
stratum boundaries.

A point of E is a pair (p, a) --- a situation and a perspective.
Agent-relative moral tensors are sections of tensor bundles constructed
from E. For instance, the agent-indexed evaluation tensor M\^{}μ\_a(p)
assigns, for each dimension μ and each agent a, an evaluation score at
situation p.

Perspective shifts (Type 2 transformations, in the language of Section
5.5) are transformations on the fiber that leave the base point fixed.
The correlative structure of Hohfeldian analysis --- that Morgan's
obligation implies Alex's claim --- is a constraint relating different
points on the same fiber: if M\^{}2\_Morgan = O (obligation), then
M\^{}2\_Alex = C (claim). This constraint is a \emph{structural
invariant} of the fiber, not a consequence of any particular evaluation.

\subsection{5.5 Coordinates and Admissible
Transformations}\label{coordinates-and-admissible-transformations}

\subsubsection{The Problem of Multiple
Descriptions}\label{the-problem-of-multiple-descriptions}

In physics, coordinate transformations have a precise meaning: a change
from one coordinate chart to another covering the same region of
manifold. The transformation laws for tensors (Chapter 4, Section 4.4)
specify exactly how components change.

In ethics, we speak loosely of ``different perspectives,'' ``different
framings,'' ``different descriptions.'' But not all of these are
coordinate transformations in the geometric sense. Conflating them
invites the criticism that geometric ethics is vacuous --- that by
choosing the right ``coordinate system,'' one can make any answer come
out.

This section introduces a discipline. We distinguish three types of
transformation, with progressively weaker geometric status.

\subsubsection{Type 1: Coordinate
Redescriptions}\label{type-1-coordinate-redescriptions}

A \emph{coordinate redescription} is a change in how we parameterize the
same underlying situation. The situation itself --- the point p ∈ M ---
is unchanged; only the coordinates (the labels, the representation)
differ.

\textbf{Example.} Describing a resource allocation in terms of ``amount
to Alice'' (x\_A) vs.~``amount to Bob'' (x\_B = T - x\_A), where the
total T is fixed. These are different coordinates on the same
one-dimensional manifold.

\textbf{Example.} Describing an action as ``withholding treatment''
vs.~``allowing natural death.'' If these are genuinely synonymous --- if
they pick out exactly the same action in all morally relevant respects
--- then they are coordinate redescriptions.

\textbf{The tensorial requirement.} Any moral quantity that is a genuine
feature of the situation must transform appropriately under coordinate
redescriptions. Scalars are invariant; vectors transform by the
Jacobian; rank-2 tensors transform by two copies of the Jacobian. This
is the mathematical content of the Bond Invariance Principle:

\textbf{Axiom 5.1 (Bond Invariance Principle). {[}Definition/Modeling
choice.{]} If two descriptions d and d' of a situation are related by an
admissible coordinate transformation, then any legitimate moral
evaluation must assign the same value to both: E(d) = E(d').
Equivalently, legitimate moral evaluations are gauge-invariant
quantities on the moral manifold.}

What this rules out: moral evaluations that depend on \emph{mere
labeling}. If ``allowing to die'' sounds better than ``withholding
treatment'' but they denote the same action, a proper moral evaluation
must not distinguish them. This is the ethical analogue of general
covariance in physics.

What this does \emph{not} rule out: evaluations that differ because the
descriptions are \emph{genuinely different} --- because they pick out
different features, different contexts, or different aspects of the
situation. The framework demands invariance under re-description, not
under re-situation.

In the language of group actions, BIP states that the moral evaluation
functional E is constant on orbits of the admissible re-description
group: E(g · d) = E(d) for all g ∈ G\_adm and all descriptions d of a
situation p ∈ M. Here g · d denotes the action of the re-description
group element g on the description d (the fiber over p in the
description bundle of §5.4), and G\_adm is the group of admissible
re-descriptions --- the subgroup of Diff(fiber) satisfying the five
admissibility conditions of §5.5. Chapter 12 will identify G\_adm = D₄ ×
U(1)\_H as the maximal such group consistent with axioms A1--A5. The BIP
is thus a precise orbit-invariance condition, and the tensor
transformation laws of §4.4 are the infinitesimal manifestation of this
group action on the moral evaluation algebra.

\subsubsection{Pre-flight Checklist: Bond Invariance
Principle}\label{pre-flight-checklist-bond-invariance-principle}

Before applying BIP, verify each item:

\textbf{Admissibility verified.} Both descriptions pass all five
conditions (§5.5): same physical grounding, same agent structure, same
stratum, reversible, no evaluative loading.

\textbf{Manifold located.} Situation has explicit coordinates on nine
dimensions and an explicit stratum.

\textbf{Tensor rank declared.} The moral quantity has a declared type;
BIP requires appropriate transformation.

\textbf{Gauge group scope.} Transformation is within G\_ethics = D₄ ×
U(1)\_H (Thm 12.3).

\textbf{Grounding adequate (AI).} Ψ satisfies six adequacy conditions
(Def. 18.1).

\subsubsection{Type 2: Perspective
Shifts}\label{type-2-perspective-shifts}

A \emph{perspective shift} changes the evaluating agent while holding
the situation fixed. This is not a coordinate change on M --- it is a
transformation on the fiber of the agent bundle (Section 5.4).

\textbf{Example.} Evaluating a kidney allocation from the physician's
perspective vs.~a patient's family's perspective. The situation (who the
patients are, what their conditions are) is the same. What changes is
\emph{who is evaluating}.

\textbf{Tensorial treatment.} Perspective shifts act on the agent index
of agent-relative tensors. The rank-2 tensor M\^{}μ\_a (evaluation of
dimension μ by agent a) transforms in the agent index a when we change
perspectives. The framework makes no general demand that all
perspectives agree --- that would be an implausibly strong objectivity
requirement. Instead, it demands that certain \emph{structural} features
are perspective-invariant:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The correlative structure: if one perspective assigns obligation, the
  correlative perspective must assign claim.
\item
  The constraint set: what is forbidden is forbidden from all
  perspectives.
\item
  The stratum structure: which moral regime applies is not
  perspective-dependent.
\end{itemize}

These invariants characterize the \emph{objective} structure of the
moral situation, while the \emph{perspectival} variation (which
dimensions each agent prioritizes, which trade-offs each is willing to
make) is the legitimate space of moral disagreement.

\subsubsection{Type 3: Theory Shifts}\label{type-3-theory-shifts}

A \emph{theory shift} changes the mathematical structure we impose on M:
the metric, the connection, the constraint set, the admissible
transformations themselves.

\textbf{Example.} Switching from a utilitarian metric (all dimensions
commensurable, with trade-off ratios specified by the metric's
off-diagonal components) to a lexicographic metric (Dimension 3 has
absolute priority; other dimensions matter only when Dimension 3 is
tied). This is not a coordinate change; it is a change in the
\emph{geometry} of moral space.

\textbf{Example.} Switching from a theory that treats agent identity as
morally irrelevant (strict impartialism) to one that gives weight to
special relationships (moderate partiality). This changes which
fiber-bundle structures are admitted.

\textbf{Tensorial treatment.} Theory shifts are \emph{not} symmetries
--- they change the structure. Different theories correspond to
different choices of metric g, connection ∇, constraint set C, and
admissibility conditions. The geometric framework does not adjudicate
between theories; it represents each theory precisely, making
commitments explicit and disagreements localizable.

\subsubsection{Summary: The Transformation
Hierarchy}\label{summary-the-transformation-hierarchy}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2076}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2552}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2053}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3319}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Type}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What Changes}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Geometric Status}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Invariance Requirement}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Coordinate redescription & Labels, parameterization & Gauge symmetry &
Tensors must transform appropriately \\
Perspective shift & Evaluating agent & Fiber transformation & Structural
invariants preserved \\
Theory shift & Metric, connection, constraints & Changes the geometry &
No invariance; theories are compared, not equated \\
\end{longtable}
}

\textbf{The discipline:} When we say ``moral claims should be invariant
under redescription,'' we mean Type 1 transformations. Types 2 and 3 are
not symmetries in this sense --- they are legitimate sources of
variation that the framework makes explicit rather than concealing.

\subsubsection{Admissibility in Practice: A
Checklist}\label{admissibility-in-practice-a-checklist}

The BIP's power rests on the boundary of ``admissible.'' A skeptic may
object: \emph{if you control the equivalence relation, invariance is
guaranteed trivially}. The objection is correct in principle---which is
why the boundary must be operationally defined and independently
testable. Given two descriptions \(d\) and \(d'\) of what is claimed to
be the ``same'' moral situation, the following checklist determines
whether \(d \rightarrow d'\) is an admissible (Type 1) transformation.
\emph{All} conditions must be met.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Same physical grounding.} Would a sensor suite measuring
  welfare indicators, bodily-autonomy indicators, resource
  distributions, and other morally relevant observables produce the
  \emph{same readings} under both descriptions? If not, the descriptions
  pick out different situations, and the transformation is not
  admissible.
\item
  \textbf{Same agent structure.} Do \(d\) and \(d'\) identify the same
  agents, the same relationships, and the same power asymmetries? If a
  description obscures an agent (``mistakes were made'' vs.~``the CEO
  fired the whistleblower''), the transformation changes the agent index
  and is Type 2, not Type 1.
\item
  \textbf{Same stratum.} Do \(d\) and \(d'\) place the situation in the
  same Hohfeldian stratum? If ``allowing to die'' evokes Liberty while
  ``withholding treatment'' evokes Obligation, the descriptions encode
  different moral regimes. This is detectable by checking whether the
  semantic-gate structure differs under the two descriptions.
\item
  \textbf{Reversibility.} Can a competent moral reasoner, given \(d'\),
  reconstruct \(d\) without additional information? If the
  transformation is lossy---if it obscures morally relevant
  features---it is a projection, not a coordinate change. Projections
  are not admissible.
\item
  \textbf{No evaluative loading.} Does the transformation change the
  evaluative valence? ``Freedom fighter'' vs.~``terrorist'' is not a
  coordinate change; it smuggles in an evaluation along the
  normative-mode dimensions. Admissible transformations change
  coordinate labels, not evaluative content.
\end{enumerate}

If all five conditions are met, the transformation is admissible (Type
1) and the BIP demands invariance. If any condition fails, the
transformation is Type 2 or Type 3, and variance between the two
evaluations is legitimate.

\subsubsection{Tempting but Invalid
Transformations}\label{tempting-but-invalid-transformations}

The following examples illustrate transformations that \emph{appear} to
be mere redescriptions but fail the admissibility checklist. Each is a
case where naive application of the BIP would be a mistake.

\textbf{1. ``Killing'' vs.~``allowing to die.''} These are often treated
as synonyms describing the same physical outcome (a death). But they
differ on the agent-structure criterion: \emph{killing} requires an
active causal agent; \emph{allowing to die} involves the absence of
intervention. They also frequently differ on stratum: killing may
trigger the absorbing stratum for direct harm (Chapter 8, Type III
boundary), while allowing to die may remain in the Liberty stratum.
\emph{Checklist verdict:} Fails criteria 2 and 3. Classification:
distinct situations. BIP does not apply.

\textbf{2. ``Taxation'' vs.~``theft.''} Both involve involuntary
transfer of resources, and a libertarian critic may claim they are
equivalent. But they differ on the procedural-legitimacy dimension
(\(x^{8}\))---taxation operates through institutional authority; theft
does not---and on evaluative loading: ``theft'' encodes a negative
judgment that ``taxation'' does not. \emph{Checklist verdict:} Fails
criteria 2 and 5. Classification: Type 3 (theory shift). BIP does not
apply.

\textbf{3. ``Surveillance'' vs.~``monitoring for safety.''} Both
describe the same physical activity (cameras recording people). The
physical grounding may be identical. But ``surveillance'' carries
negative evaluative loading (privacy invasion), while ``monitoring for
safety'' carries positive loading (protective care). The transformation
smuggles evaluation into the description. \emph{Checklist verdict:}
Fails criterion 5. Classification: Type 2 (perspective shift with
evaluative content). BIP does not apply; the variance between the two
framings is a legitimate signal of moral disagreement.

\textbf{4. ``He said / she said'' perspective swaps.} Describing a
dispute from either party's perspective may seem like a mere coordinate
change. But it shifts the evaluating agent (Type 2), not the coordinate
system: the power asymmetries, epistemic positions, and relational roles
are redistributed. \emph{Checklist verdict:} Fails criterion 2.
Classification: Type 2. Variance is expected and informative.

\textbf{5. ``Economic growth'' vs.~``ecological destruction.''} Both may
describe the same industrial activity. But they foreground different
dimensions of the moral manifold (welfare \(x^{1}\)
vs.~societal/environmental impact \(x^{6}\)), use different physical
grounding (GDP metrics vs.~ecosystem metrics), and carry evaluative
loading in opposite directions. \emph{Checklist verdict:} Fails criteria
1, 2, and 5. Classification: distinct framings invoking different
regions of the moral manifold. The BIP correctly predicts that these
should \emph{not} yield the same evaluation.

These examples demonstrate that the admissibility boundary is not a free
parameter that can be tuned to guarantee invariance. It is an
operationally testable criterion rooted in the five-condition checklist.
Failures of admissibility are \emph{detectable}---they show up as
violations of the agent-structure, stratum, or evaluative-loading
conditions---and they are \emph{informative}: they reveal that what
appeared to be a mere redescription is actually a substantive moral
reframing.

An Edge Case: Genuine Ambiguity

The five invalid transformations above were designed to fail clearly.
But the checklist's greatest value emerges when the verdict is
ambiguous---when reasonable people might disagree about whether a
transformation is admissible. Consider:

\textbf{``Euthanasia'' vs. ``mercy killing'' vs. ``assisted dying.''}
All three describe a physician administering a lethal dose to a
terminally ill, consenting patient.

1. Same physical grounding. Yes---the same patient, the same medication,
the same outcome. ✓

2. Same agent structure. Yes---physician, patient, and family occupy the
same roles. ✓

3. Same stratum. Ambiguous. ``Mercy killing'' may trigger the absorbing
stratum for direct killing (§8.5), while ``assisted dying'' may remain
in the obligation stratum (medical duty of compassion). ``Euthanasia''
is etymologically neutral (``good death'') but is culturally coded as a
boundary term. This is where the checklist escalates.

4. Reversibility. Marginal---a competent reasoner can reconstruct any of
the three from any other, but connotative weight is lost in translation.

5. No evaluative loading. ``Mercy killing'' loads positively (``mercy'')
and negatively (``killing'') simultaneously. ``Assisted dying'' loads
procedurally (``assisted''). ``Euthanasia'' is closest to neutral.

Checklist verdict. Criteria 3 and 5 fail for ``mercy killing'' → Type 2
or 3. Criterion 3 is ambiguous for ``euthanasia'' vs. ``assisted dying''
→ the transformation's admissibility depends on a governance decision
about stratum assignment (§9.1).

The lesson. When the checklist produces ambiguity rather than a clear
verdict, it has done its job. It has identified the precise locus of
genuine moral disagreement---the stratum boundary at which ``medical
compassion'' meets ``taking a life''---rather than papering over it with
a false equivalence. The equivalence relation ≈ is not subjective; it is
decidable up to a governance parameter (the stratum assignment), and the
checklist tells you exactly which parameter requires a decision.

\subsection{5.6 Stratification}\label{stratification}

\subsubsection{Why Stratification Is
Essential}\label{why-stratification-is-essential}

The most important structural feature of the moral manifold is not its
smooth geometry but its \emph{stratification}: the partition of M into
regions (strata) where different rules apply, joined along boundaries
where the rules change discontinuously.

Smooth manifolds, by definition, are uniform in dimension: every point
has a neighborhood homeomorphic to ℝⁿ for the same n.~But moral space is
not uniform. There are regions of smooth trade-offs (within a stratum),
hard boundaries (between strata), and singular points (where strata
intersect pathologically). The moral manifold is not a smooth manifold
but a \emph{stratified space} in the sense of Whitney (Chapter 4,
Section 4.8).

\subsubsection{Strata, Boundaries, and
Transitions}\label{strata-boundaries-and-transitions}

\textbf{Definition 5.4 (Moral Stratification).} *The moral manifold M
admits a Whitney stratification \{S\_α\}\_\{α ∈ A\} where: \emph{1.}
Each stratum S\_α is a smooth manifold (possibly of different
dimension). \emph{2.} Within each stratum, the moral evaluation function
S and the moral metric g are smooth. \emph{3.} At stratum boundaries, S
or g may be discontinuous, or the effective dimension may change.*

\textbf{Threshold effects.} Within the ``consent'' stratum, the degree
of informed consent varies smoothly --- a little more information, a
little more understanding. But at the boundary between ``sufficient
consent'' and ``insufficient consent,'' the moral evaluation jumps
discontinuously. Below the threshold, the action may be impermissible;
above it, permissible. This is a stratum boundary.

\textbf{Categorical distinctions.} The difference between a 17-year-old
and an 18-year-old, between killing and letting die, between lying and
remaining silent --- these are boundaries where legal and moral rules
change. Within each side of the boundary, smooth variation is possible.
Across the boundary, the applicable framework shifts.

\textbf{The forbidden region.} Certain options are not merely low-value
but \emph{excluded from consideration} --- moral non-starters regardless
of other considerations.

\textbf{Definition 5.5 (Constraint Set). *The constraint set C ⊂ M is a
closed subset such that every satisfaction function S compatible with
the stratification: M → ℝ ∪ \{-∞\} satisfies S\_C = -∞. The boundary ∂C
is a stratum boundary.*}

Notation remark. The symbol S is used in two distinct senses throughout
this work: (i) S: M → ℝ ∪ \{−∞\} denotes the satisfaction function (a
scalar field on M), and (ii) S\_α denotes strata of the Whitney
stratification (subsets of M). Context always disambiguates: S with a
subscript α ∈ A (the index set) refers to a stratum, while S alone or
S(p) refers to the satisfaction scalar. Where both appear in the same
expression, the satisfaction function is written S(p) or as the
contraction I\_μ O\^{}μ to avoid ambiguity.

The constraint set represents absolute prohibitions: actions involving
non-consensual harm to innocents, violations of inviolable rights, and
the like. The framework does not determine \emph{what} belongs in C ---
that is the work of normative ethics and democratic governance. But it
represents the \emph{structure} of constraints precisely: as a region
with hard boundaries, distinguished from regions of trade-offs.

\subsubsection{Nullifiers as Absorbing
States}\label{nullifiers-as-absorbing-states}

Empirical evidence (Chapter 17) reveals a special class of stratum
boundary: \emph{nullifiers} --- conditions that override all other
considerations and collapse obligations to zero.

The Dear Abby corpus (Bond 2026) identifies abuse as the primary
nullifier: across all domain contexts (family, workplace, friendship,
romance), the presence of abuse universally nullifies obligations (n =
582). Other nullifiers include danger (n = 218) and impossibility (n =
144, encoding the principle \emph{ought implies can}).

In stratified-space language, nullifiers define \emph{absorbing strata}
--- lower-dimensional strata from which there is no transition back.
Once a situation enters the ``abuse'' stratum, the normal obligation
structure collapses: the metric becomes degenerate along the obligation
dimensions, and the previously operative duties cease to apply.

\textbf{Definition 5.6 (Absorbing Stratum).} \emph{A stratum S\_0 ⊂ M is
absorbing if, for any path γ entering S\_0, the moral evaluation along γ
is determined by S\_0's rules thereafter, regardless of the prior
stratum's structure.}

\subsubsection{Semantic Gates as Transition
Functions}\label{semantic-gates-as-transition-functions}

The transitions between strata are not arbitrary. They are triggered by
specific features of the situation --- what we call \emph{semantic
gates}, borrowing the language of quantum normative dynamics.

\textbf{Example.} The phrase ``you promised'' triggers a transition from
the Liberty stratum to the Obligation stratum. The phrase ``only if
convenient'' triggers the reverse transition, from Obligation to
Liberty. These transitions are \emph{discrete} --- step functions, not
sigmoids --- and empirically measurable with high reliability (94\% for
``you promised,'' 89\% for ``only if convenient'').

In the geometry of M, a semantic gate is a codimension-1 submanifold (a
``wall'' between strata) equipped with a transition function specifying
how the moral evaluation changes upon crossing. The discreteness of
these transitions --- verified empirically --- supports the
stratified-space model over a purely smooth manifold.

\subsection{5.7 Singularities and
Dilemmas}\label{singularities-and-dilemmas}

A \emph{singularity} in M is a point where the normal geometric
structure breaks down. In general relativity, singularities are points
of infinite density or undefined curvature --- places where the theory
reaches its limits. In geometric ethics, they are \emph{genuine moral
dilemmas}.

\subsubsection{Definition and Examples}\label{definition-and-examples}

\textbf{Definition 5.7 (Moral Singularity).} \emph{A point p ∈ M is a
moral singularity if:} 1. \emph{The metric tensor g\_p is degenerate
(det(g\_p) = 0), so that some directions of moral variation have zero
``weight'' and comparisons break down; or} 2. \emph{The satisfaction
function S is not differentiable at p, so that the gradient ∇S --- the
``direction of moral improvement'' --- is undefined; or} 3.
\emph{Multiple constraint surfaces intersect at p, creating a cone of
forbidden directions that leaves no permissible path.}

The three singularity types are: (i) metric singularities, where the
moral metric degenerates and dimensional comparisons fail; (ii) gradient
singularities, where the satisfaction function loses differentiability
and the direction of moral improvement is undefined; and (iii)
constraint singularities, where multiple prohibition surfaces intersect
and the feasible region collapses. The singular set Σ ⊂ M is their
union: Σ = Σ\_metric ∪ Σ\_gradient ∪ Σ\_constraint. Each type has
distinct geometric character: metric singularities are intrinsic
(detectable from g alone), gradient singularities depend on S, and
constraint singularities depend on the constraint set C.

\textbf{Example (Sophie's Choice).} A mother must choose which of her
two children will be killed. There is no ``right'' answer. Any choice
involves irreducible moral loss. The situation is singular: the
satisfaction function S has no global maximum in the feasible region, or
has multiple local maxima that are genuinely incomparable --- the metric
between them is degenerate.

\textbf{Example (Competing absolute obligations).} A physician has
promised confidentiality to a patient who reveals an intent to harm a
third party. The obligation of confidentiality and the obligation to
prevent harm are both operative, both point in opposite directions, and
neither can be subordinated to the other without remainder. The gradient
∇S is undefined: there is no single ``direction of improvement.''

\subsubsection{Dilemmas as Features, Not
Bugs}\label{dilemmas-as-features-not-bugs}

Singularities are not failures of the framework. They are
\emph{features}. The framework \emph{represents} genuine dilemmas as
singular points, rather than forcing a false determinacy. This is an
advance over scalar approaches, which must either deliver an answer (by
arbitrary tie-breaking) or fall silent (by declaring the situation
beyond their scope).

The geometric representation is more precise: we can say \emph{why} the
dilemma is a dilemma --- which constraint surfaces intersect, which
metric components become degenerate, which dimensions of the moral
tensor conflict. We can characterize the \emph{type} of singularity
(metric degeneracy vs.~gradient failure vs.~constraint intersection) and
the \emph{moral residue} that any resolution leaves behind (Chapter 15).

\subsection{5.8 Topology of the Moral
Manifold}\label{topology-of-the-moral-manifold}

\subsubsection{Local and Global
Structure}\label{local-and-global-structure}

The differential structure developed so far is \emph{local}: it
describes the neighborhood of a point. The \emph{topology} of M concerns
its global properties --- properties that are invariant under continuous
deformation but may be invisible from any single neighborhood.

\textbf{Connectedness.} Can any two moral situations be reached from
each other by a continuous path through M? Or are there disconnected
components --- perhaps corresponding to genuinely incommensurable moral
frameworks, or to domains of life so different that no smooth
interpolation between them exists?

The evidence suggests that M is connected within a single
cultural-historical context: any two everyday moral situations can be
related by a sequence of small modifications. But whether M is connected
\emph{across} cultures and historical epochs is a substantive question.
The temporal stability data from the Dear Abby corpus (Chapter 17)
suggests that the core topology of M is stable over at least three
decades, though the metric varies.

\textbf{Compactness.} Is M compact (closed and bounded) or does it
extend indefinitely? The constraint set C introduces boundaries, but
does M have a ``ceiling'' --- a maximum possible stakes, a highest
conceivable obligation?

In practice, moral evaluation operates on a bounded region: the stakes
in any real situation are finite, the number of agents is finite, and
the morally relevant features take values in bounded ranges. We can
model the relevant portion of M as compact without great loss.

\textbf{Fundamental group.} Does M have ``holes'' --- non-contractible
loops? A nontrivial fundamental group π₁(M) would mean that certain
closed paths in moral space cannot be continuously shrunk to a point.
This would have implications for holonomy (Chapter 10): transporting a
moral evaluation around such a loop could yield a nontrivial change,
even though the loop returns to the same situation.

Whether M has nontrivial topology is among the most intriguing open
questions of the framework. The path-dependence of obligation (Chapter
10) provides \emph{indirect} evidence: if obligations change when
transported around loops, the manifold may have nontrivial holonomy,
which constrains the topology. But settling this definitively requires
the empirical program outlined in Chapter 29.

\subsection{5.9 Two Worked Examples}\label{two-worked-examples}

\subsubsection{Example 1: The Manifold of a Medical
Allocation}\label{example-1-the-manifold-of-a-medical-allocation}

\textbf{Situation.} A physician must allocate a scarce treatment among
three patients (A, B, C). Each patient has a medical benefit score β\_i
∈ {[}0, 1{]}, a waiting time w\_i ∈ {[}0, ∞), an age a\_i, and a number
of dependents d\_i.

\textbf{The manifold.} The space of possible allocations is the
2-simplex:

\[M = \Delta^{2} = \{(p_{A},p_{B},p_{C}):p_{i} \geq 0,\mspace{6mu}\sum_{i}^{}p_{i} = 1\}\]

where p\_i is the fraction of treatment allocated to patient i.

\textbf{Stratification:} - \emph{Interior} S₂ (dim 2): All patients have
positive probability. Smooth trade-offs possible. - \emph{Edges} S₁ (dim
1): Two patients share the treatment; one is excluded. Three edges. -
\emph{Vertices} S₀ (dim 0): Deterministic allocations. Three vertices.
These are the \emph{actual decisions}; the interior represents
deliberation space.

This is a Whitney-stratified space with three levels. The frontier
condition holds: each vertex is in the closure of each adjacent edge,
and each edge is in the closure of the interior.

\textbf{Constraint region.} Suppose Patient C's inclusion would
constitute discrimination (the decision is based on a protected
characteristic). Then the region p\_C \textgreater{} 0 is forbidden:

\[C = \{(p_{A},p_{B},p_{C}) \in \Delta^{2}:p_{C} > 0\}\]

The feasible region is the edge from vertex A to vertex B --- a
1-dimensional manifold.

\textbf{Metrics.} Different metrics on Δ² correspond to different
ethical theories: - \emph{Euclidean metric:} g = dp\_A ⊗ dp\_A + dp\_B ⊗
dp\_B. All movements equally costly. - \emph{Weighted metric:} g\_\{ij\}
= δ\_\{ij\}/β\_i. Movement toward sicker patients (higher β) is
``easier'' --- lower moral cost. - \emph{Lexicographic metric:} Not a
metric in the standard sense, but a limiting case: the allocation must
first maximize benefit to the worst-off, then consider secondary
criteria. This corresponds to a degenerate metric where the worst-off
dimension has infinite weight.

\textbf{Satisfaction function.} S: Δ² → ℝ assigns a moral score to each
allocation. On the interior, S is smooth. On the constraint boundary, S
= -∞. The gradient ∇S, computed via the metric, points toward the
morally preferred allocation.

\subsubsection{Example 2: The Promise
Landscape}\label{example-2-the-promise-landscape}

\textbf{Situation.} Morgan has promised to help Alex move on Saturday.
Various complications arise.

\textbf{The manifold.} The relevant region of M is parameterized by (at
least) three dimensions:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  x² = strength of Morgan's obligation (Dimension 2: Rights and Duties)
\item
  x⁴ = cost to Morgan's autonomy (Dimension 4: Autonomy)
\item
  x⁷ = quality of the Morgan-Alex relationship (Dimension 7: Care)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Strata.} The promise creates two primary strata:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Obligation stratum} S\_O: The region where Morgan has an
  obligation to help. The promise is binding, the cost is manageable,
  and no nullifiers apply.
\item
  \emph{Liberty stratum} S\_L: The region where Morgan is free to
  decline. Either the promise was conditional, or a nullifier applies,
  or the cost has become prohibitive.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Semantic gates.} The boundary between S\_O and S\_L is crossed
by specific triggers:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  ``Only if convenient'' → O → L transition. The obligation collapses.
  The Dear Abby corpus measures this gate at 89\% effectiveness.
\item
  ``You explicitly promised'' → L → O transition. The obligation
  crystallizes. Measured at 94\% effectiveness.
\item
  Abuse by Alex → O → L (nullifier). Universal across contexts. The
  stratum boundary is absorbing.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Path-dependence.} Consider two paths from an initial state
(Morgan made the promise) to a final state (Morgan is aware that Alex
was rude last week):

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Path α: Morgan learns Alex was rude, then recalls the explicit
  promise.
\item
  Path β: Morgan recalls the explicit promise, then learns Alex was
  rude.
\end{itemize}

If the final obligation differs depending on which information came
first, the moral manifold has \emph{curvature} in this region. The
non-commutativity of these information-updates --- learning about
rudeness and recalling the promise do not commute --- is precisely the
non-abelian structure formalized by the D₄ dihedral group (Chapter 12).

\subsection{5.10 The Manifold as Common
Ground}\label{the-manifold-as-common-ground}

The moral manifold M is the base space over which all ethical tensors
are defined. Its points are structured situations; its structure
includes a nine-dimensional tangent space (at generic points), a
context-varying metric, a Whitney stratification into moral regimes, and
singular points representing genuine dilemmas.

Different ethical theories correspond to different structures \emph{on}
M:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Different metrics (utilitarian vs.~egalitarian vs.~lexicographic)
\item
  Different constraint sets (what is absolutely forbidden)
\item
  Different contractions (how multi-dimensional evaluation reduces to
  action)
\item
  Different connections (how obligations are transported across
  contexts)
\end{itemize}

But all theories share M as common ground. This makes disagreement
\emph{tractable}: we can ask whether two theories differ in their
metrics, their constraints, or their contractions. We can identify where
they agree (perhaps on the constraint set --- both agree that torture is
forbidden) and where they diverge (perhaps on the metric --- they
disagree about the trade-off rate between welfare and equality).
Disagreement, in this framework, has \emph{address}.

The moral manifold is not the whole of ethics. It is the \emph{stage} on
which ethics plays out --- the space of possibilities that moral
reasoning navigates. The next chapter develops the actors: the tensors
of various ranks that live on M --- obligations as vectors, interests as
covectors, the moral metric, and the fundamental contraction S = I\_μ
O\^{}μ that yields satisfaction.

But without the stage, there is nowhere for the actors to stand.

\subsection{Technical Appendix: Formal
Definitions}\label{technical-appendix-formal-definitions}

\textbf{Definition A.1 (Moral Manifold, Formal).} \emph{A moral manifold
is a paracompact Hausdorff topological space M equipped with:} 1. *A
Whitney stratification \{S\_α\}\_\{α ∈ A\} into smooth manifolds of
(possibly) varying dimensions; \emph{2.} A partial order ⪯ on A
satisfying the frontier condition: S\_α ∩ cl(S\_β) ≠ ∅ implies α ⪯ β;
\emph{3.} Whitney's condition (B) at all stratum boundaries; \emph{4.} A
smooth structure on each stratum S\_α making it a smooth manifold of
dimension d\_α.*

\textbf{Definition A.2 (Coordinate Chart on M).} \emph{A coordinate
chart on a stratum S\_α is a diffeomorphism φ: U → V from an open set U
⊂ S\_α to an open set V ⊂ ℝ\^{}\{d\_α\}. A coordinate transformation is
a composition φ' ∘ φ\^{}\{-1\}: ℝ\^{}\{d\_α\} → ℝ\^{}\{d\_α\}, required
to be a diffeomorphism.}

\textbf{Definition A.3 (Admissible Transformation). An admissible
transformation of the moral manifold is a stratification-preserving
homeomorphism (restricting to a diffeomorphism on each stratum interior)
ψ: M → M satisfying ψ(S\_α) ⊂ S\_\{σ(α)\} for some bijective,
order-preserving map σ of the index set A. Type 1 transformations are
admissible; Type 3 transformations are not.}

\textbf{Definition A.4 (Agent Bundle). The agent bundle over M is a
fiber bundle (E, π, M) where: 1. The fiber F\_p = π\^{}\{-1\}(p) at each
p ∈ M is the set of agent-perspectives available in situation p; 2. The
structure group G acts on fibers by perspective transformations; 3.
Correlative constraints (O\_a ↔ C\_b for all Hohfeldian correlative
pairs (a, b) at p) are structural invariants of the G-action.}

\textbf{Definition A.5 (Moral Singularity).} \emph{A point p ∈ M is a
moral singularity if:} 1. \emph{The metric tensor g\_p is degenerate:
det(g\_p) = 0; or} 2. \emph{The satisfaction function S is not
differentiable at p; or} 3. \emph{Multiple constraint surfaces of
codimension 1 intersect transversally at p, and the feasible cone at p
has no interior.}

\emph{Singularities represent genuine dilemmas: points where the moral
structure does not determine a unique best response.}

\textbf{Definition A.6 (Absorbing Stratum).} *A stratum S\_0 is
absorbing if there exists a continuous retraction r: N → S\_0 from a
tubular neighborhood N of S\_0 such that, for any moral evaluation
function S compatible with the stratification, S\_N factors through r:
S(p) = S(r(p)) for all p ∈ N.* Additionally, S₀ is a trapping region:
for any continuous path γ with γ(t₀) ∈ S₀, we have γ(t) ∈ N ∪ S₀ for all
t ≥ t₀. This trapping condition reconciles the formal definition with
the informal path-based characterization (Definition 5.6): once a moral
trajectory enters an absorbing stratum, the evaluation is governed by
that stratum\textquotesingle s rules thereafter.

❖

\emph{The manifold is the ground. The tensors are the figures.}

\emph{Before we can say what obligation points toward, we must know the
space in which it points.}

\emph{This chapter has constructed that space: M, the moral manifold ---
nine-dimensional, stratified, equipped with a context-varying metric,
and rich enough to serve as common ground for every ethical theory that
has a claim to completeness.}

\section{Chapter 6: The Tensor
Hierarchy}\label{chapter-6-the-tensor-hierarchy}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya now thinks in tensors. Her TrialMatch score S is rank-0: a
scalar. Patient needs form an obligation vector Oᵋ in nine dimensions.
What patients want from the trial---hope, knowledge, dignity,
access---forms an interest covector Iₘ. The contraction S = Iₘ Oᵋ would
give a meaningful scalar, but only after respecting all nine dimensions.
Her model never builds Oᵋ or Iₘ. It goes straight to S using a neural
network whose internal representations have no guaranteed correspondence
to moral dimensions. She writes in her notebook: `My model doesn't
contract a tensor. It guesses the contraction without ever forming the
tensor. That's why it can't see the structure it's losing.'}
\end{quote}

\emph{Chapter 5 built the stage --- the moral manifold M, a
nine-dimensional stratified space of structured moral situations. This
chapter populates the stage with actors: the tensors of various ranks
that encode moral evaluation. We develop obligations as vectors,
interests as covectors, the moral metric as the structure of permissible
trade-offs, and the fundamental formula S = I\_μ O\^{}μ in which
satisfaction is the contraction of interest with obligation.}

\subsection{6.1 From Scalars to Tensors: Why Rank
Matters}\label{from-scalars-to-tensors-why-rank-matters}

Recall the argument of Chapter 2: scalar moral evaluation loses
information. A single number S ∈ ℝ assigned to a situation p ∈ M tells
us \emph{how good} the situation is, but not \emph{along which
dimensions} it is good, not \emph{in which direction} improvement lies,
and not \emph{how robust} the assessment is to changes in perspective or
weighting.

The remedy is to replace the scalar with objects of successively higher
rank, each encoding more of the structure of moral evaluation:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0812}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2183}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3879}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3126}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Rank}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Object}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What It Captures}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What It Loses}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
(0,0) & Scalar S & Overall moral assessment & Everything else \\
(1,0) & Obligation vector O\^{}μ & Direction and magnitude of duty & How
interests weight the dimensions \\
(0,1) & Interest covector I\_μ & How much each dimension matters to a
stakeholder & What the duties actually are \\
(0,2) & Metric tensor g\_μν & The structure of trade-offs between
dimensions & Which duties and interests are operative \\
(1,1) & Evaluation tensor E\^{}μ\_ν & The full map from interests to
obligation-responses & The specific stakeholder and context \\
\end{longtable}
}

The hierarchy continues beyond the rank-1 obligation vector. Chapters 14
and 16 introduce higher-rank tensors for collective agency and
uncertainty, and the DEME V3 implementation (Chapter 19) extends the
hierarchy to rank 6:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0812}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2555}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4865}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1768}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Rank}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Object}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{What It Adds}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Dimensions}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
2 & Per-party (distributional) tensor & Per-stakeholder obligation
profiles across all affected parties (the DEME distributional axis) &
k×n \\
3 & Temporal tensor & Time evolution with discount \(\gamma\);
trajectory analysis & k×n×τ \\
4 & Action--coalition tensor & Obligations across candidate actions and
coalitions & k×n×a×c \\
5 & Coalition tensor & Multi-agent coordination over time; Shapley credit
assignment & k×n×τ×a×c \\
6 & Sample tensor & Monte Carlo distributional risk (CVaR, VaR) &
k×n×τ×a×c×s \\
\end{longtable}
}

where \(k = 9\) is the moral manifold dimension, \(n\) the number of
stakeholders or parties, \(\tau\) the number of time steps, \(a\) the
number of candidate actions, \(c\) the number of coalitions, and \(s\)
the number of Monte Carlo samples. Per-dimension uncertainty --- the
covariance structure \(\Sigma^{\mu\nu}\) of Chapter 16 --- is not carried
as a separate tensor rank in DEME V3; it is realized as uncertainty
metadata attached to each tensor cell and reconstructed through the
rank-6 Monte Carlo sample axis. Tucker decomposition and tensor-train
formats maintain tractability as rank increases (see Chapter 19 for
implementation details).
\includegraphics[width=5.5in,height=3.52431in]{media/image4.png}Figure~\hyperref[fig:tensor-hierarchy]{1}
illustrates the full rank hierarchy and its contraction structure.

\textbar{} The Tensor Rank Tower

Each step up the hierarchy preserves more information. The crucial
insight is that the scalar S is always recoverable from the higher-rank
objects by \emph{contraction} --- summation over paired indices --- but
the higher-rank objects are \emph{not} recoverable from S. Information
flows downward (by contraction), not upward. The tensor hierarchy is a
hierarchy of informativeness, with the scalar at the bottom.

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=5in,height=2.32083in]{media/image5.png}
\caption{Tucker decomposition of the MoralTensor: the full rank-3 tensor
T decomposes into a compact core tensor S multiplied by factor matrices
along each mode (moral dimensions, agents, time steps), enabling
tractable computation while preserving multi-modal structure.}
\end{figure}

\textbar{} Tucker Decomposition for Tractability

\subsection{6.2 Obligations as Vectors}\label{obligations-as-vectors}

\subsubsection{The Directional Character of
Duty}\label{the-directional-character-of-duty}

An obligation is not merely strong or weak. It \emph{points} --- from
the current state of affairs toward a required state. ``You ought to
return the borrowed book'' specifies both an intensity of oughtness and
a direction: from the state ``book unreturned'' toward the state ``book
returned.'' The spatial metaphor is not merely metaphorical. It is the
content of a precise mathematical claim: obligations are tangent vectors
on the moral manifold.

\textbf{Definition 6.1 (Obligation Vector). An obligation at a point p ∈
M is a tangent vector O(p) ∈ T\_pM. An obligation field is a smooth
section O: M → TM of the tangent bundle, assigning an obligation vector
to each point of M . Smoothness is defined stratum-wise: within each
stratum S\_α, the section O\textbar\_\{S\_α\} is smooth; across stratum
boundaries, O may be discontinuous.}

In the nine-dimensional coordinates (x¹, \ldots, x⁹) of Chapter 5, an
obligation vector has nine components:

\[O = O^{\mu}\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{\mu}} = O^{1}\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{1}} + O^{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{2}} + \cdots + O^{9}\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{9}}\]

Each component O\^{}μ gives the strength of the obligation \emph{along
dimension μ}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \includegraphics[width=2.71329in,height=3.44048in]{media/image6.png}O¹
  = obligation to improve consequences/welfare
\item
  O² = obligation to respect rights and fulfill duties
\item
  O³ = obligation to promote justice and fairness
\item
  O⁴ = obligation to preserve autonomy
\item
  O⁵ = obligation to protect privacy
\item
  O⁶ = obligation to consider societal/environmental impact
\item
  O⁷ = obligation to exercise virtue and care
\item
  O⁸ = obligation to ensure procedural legitimacy
\end{itemize}

\textbar{} The Nine Dimensions of the Moral Manifold

Figure~\hyperref[fig:moral-dims]{3} provides a visual map of these nine
dimensions and shows how higher-rank tensors build upon them.

\protect\phantomsection\label{fig:moral-dims}{}

\subsubsection{}\label{section}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Rank progression: from rank-1 obligation
vectors to rank-6 distributional tensors.
}{Rank progression: from rank-1 obligation vectors to rank-6 distributional tensors. }}\label{rank-progression-from-rank-1-obligation-vectors-to-rank-6-distributional-tensors.}

Higher ranks encode richer moral structure at the cost of greater
computational complexity.
