fixed points --- moral pluralism at the level of metric choice. The
constructivist metric is not unique but convergent: as deliberation
proceeds under better conditions, the class of admissible metrics
narrows. Whether it narrows to a point is the key open question.

\subsection{9.4 The Expressivist Account:
Projection}\label{the-expressivist-account-projection}

\subsubsection{The Claim}\label{the-claim-2}

The expressivist holds that moral claims express attitudes rather than
describe facts. On this view, the moral metric is a \emph{projection} of
our evaluative sensibilities onto the world --- a structure we impose,
not one we discover or construct.

\textbf{Formal statement.} The moral metric is agent-indexed:
\(g_{\mu\nu}(a)\) is the metric of agent \(a\), reflecting \(a\)'s
pattern of evaluative responses. ``Objectivity'' is intersubjective
convergence --- the overlap between individual metrics --- not
correspondence to a metric-independent fact.

\subsubsection{Strengths}\label{strengths-2}

\textbf{Motivational force.} Expressivism explains why moral judgments
motivate action. If the metric reflects my evaluative sensibilities,
then recognizing that situation \(p\) requires action (that
\(\nabla S \neq 0\) in my metric) is simultaneously recognizing that I
\emph{care} about the relevant dimensions. The motivation is built into
the metric.

\textbf{Diversity.} Expressivism naturally accommodates the diversity of
moral outlook across cultures, epochs, and individuals. Different agents
project different metrics --- and this is expected, not anomalous.

\textbf{Metaphysical economy.} No Platonic realm, no idealized
deliberation. Just agents with attitudes, projecting structure onto the
moral landscape.

\subsubsection{Challenges}\label{challenges-2}

\textbf{Structure, not just attitudes.} The moral metric is not merely
an attitude --- it is a geometric object with mathematical properties
(symmetry, non-degeneracy conditions, transformation behavior). Can
expressivism account for this structure? An attitude might be ``I care
more about welfare than justice.'' The metric encodes not just this
ranking but the precise trade-off rate, the coupling between dimensions,
the degeneracies, and the transformation law under change of
coordinates. It is unclear that attitudes have this kind of structure.

\textbf{The Frege-Geach problem.} Moral claims embed in complex logical
contexts: ``If torturing innocents is wrong, then getting others to
torture innocents is wrong.'' Expressivist semantics for embedded moral
claims remains difficult. The metric framework sharpens the problem: how
do we express ``if \(g_{12} = 0\) (welfare and justice are
incommensurable), then\ldots'' in expressivist terms?

\textbf{Convergence.} If the metric is merely projected, why is there as
much moral agreement as there is? The convergence of diverse moral
traditions on certain structural features --- the obligation/liberty
distinction (which transfers cross-linguistically at 100\% in BIP
experiments), the correlative symmetry of Hohfeldian states, the
universality of nullifiers --- is difficult to explain if the metric is
pure projection. Something structural appears to constrain the
projection.

\subsubsection{Geometric Gloss}\label{geometric-gloss-2}

The expressivist metric is subjective: \(g_{\mu\nu}(a)\) varies with the
agent. Intersubjective moral facts are features of the \emph{overlap}
between agent-metrics. The set of ``objective'' moral claims is the set
of claims invariant under all agents' metrics:

\[\text{Objective claims} = \bigcap_{a}\{ c:c\text{ is true under }g(a)\}\]

If this intersection is large, moral objectivism is approximately
correct. If it is small, moral relativism wins. The size of the
intersection is an empirical question.

\subsection{9.5 The Governance Account: Democratic
Determination}\label{the-governance-account-democratic-determination}

\subsubsection{The Proposal}\label{the-proposal}

I propose a fourth account suited to the geometric framework: the moral
metric is neither discovered, constructed by idealized reason, nor
projected by individual sensibility. It is \emph{governed} --- the
output of legitimate governance processes that have authority to
determine, for a community, how moral trade-offs are to be structured.

\textbf{Formal statement.} The moral metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) for a
community \(\mathcal{C}\) is determined by a governance process
\(\Gamma_{\mathcal{C}}\):

\[g_{\mu\nu} = \Gamma_{\mathcal{C}}(\text{inputs})\]

where the inputs include empirical data about the community's moral
commitments, the structural constraints of §9.6, the outcomes of
legitimate deliberative processes, and the community's constitutional
commitments.

\subsubsection{What Governance Means}\label{what-governance-means}

The governance account occupies a specific position in logical space,
distinct from the three traditional accounts:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1748}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2235}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3120}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2897}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Account}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Source of the Metric}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Authority}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Revisability}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Realism & Nature / moral reality & Independent of us & Not revisable
(only our knowledge improves) \\
Constructivism & Idealized rational procedure & Rational authority &
Revisable if the procedure refines \\
Expressivism & Individual sensibility & No external authority &
Revisable as attitudes change \\
Governance & Legitimate institutional process & Democratic/institutional
authority & Revisable through legitimate process \\
\end{longtable}
}

The governance account holds that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The metric is a governance artifact} --- like a constitution,
  a legal code, or a set of regulatory standards. It is neither a
  natural fact nor a subjective projection. It is a socially
  authoritative determination of how trade-offs are to be structured.
\item
  \textbf{Different communities may legitimately adopt different
  metrics.} This is not relativism --- some metrics are inadmissible
  (§9.6) --- but it is \emph{pluralism}: within the space of admissible
  metrics, legitimate governance processes may select differently,
  reflecting different histories, values, and circumstances.
\item
  \textbf{The metric can be revised.} If a community's moral
  understanding changes --- if formerly acceptable trade-offs come to be
  seen as illegitimate --- the metric can be updated through legitimate
  institutional processes. This is what moral reform \emph{is}: a change
  in the collectively authoritative metric.
\item
  \textbf{Disagreement about the metric is political as well as
  philosophical.} Whether welfare can be traded against justice at a
  particular rate is not merely a question for moral philosophy. It is a
  question for democratic deliberation, institutional design, and
  constitutional law.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Why Not Relativism?}\label{why-not-relativism}

The governance account might seem to collapse into relativism: if the
metric is whatever a community says it is, then anything goes. But this
objection misunderstands the account in two ways.

First, the metric is constrained (§9.6). Not every metric is admissible.
Some structural constraints --- non-degeneracy on protected dimensions,
internal consistency, invariance under admissible transformations ---
are non-negotiable. These constraints narrow the space of admissible
metrics, ruling out metrics that encode arbitrary discrimination,
internal contradiction, or dependence on morally irrelevant features.

Second, governance processes can be better or worse, more or less
legitimate. A metric adopted through transparent democratic
deliberation, informed by the relevant moral considerations, responsive
to the claims of affected parties, and consistent with constitutional
commitments, has more authority than a metric imposed by fiat or adopted
through corrupt process. The governance account does not say ``any
metric is as good as any other.'' It says: ``the authority of a metric
derives from the legitimacy of the process that produced it.''

\subsubsection{The Analogy to Legal
Systems}\label{the-analogy-to-legal-systems}

The governance account draws on an analogy with law. Legal systems
determine the structure of permissible actions: what counts as property,
what counts as a contract, what constitutes a crime. These are not
natural facts (different legal systems answer differently), nor are they
mere conventions (they carry real authority and have real consequences).
They are governance artifacts --- socially authoritative determinations
backed by legitimate institutional processes.

The moral metric is analogous. It determines the structure of
permissible trade-offs: whether welfare can be exchanged for justice, at
what rate, and under what conditions. These determinations are not
natural facts, and they are not mere preferences. They are governance
decisions with real moral authority.

The analogy extends further. Just as constitutions constrain ordinary
legislation (some laws are unconstitutional, regardless of popular
support), there are \emph{meta-constraints} on the moral metric (§9.6)
that no governance process can override. These constraints play the role
of moral constitutionalism: they define the boundaries within which
legitimate metric choice operates.

\subsubsection{Connection to the
Framework}\label{connection-to-the-framework}

The governance account integrates naturally with the geometric
framework:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The manifold \(\mathcal{M}\) (Chapter 5) and the obligation fields
  \(O^{\mu}\) (Chapter 6) are \emph{prior to} the metric. They represent
  the structure of the moral situation and the duties it generates.
  These are facts about the situation, not governance choices.
\item
  The metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) is \emph{determined by governance}. It
  represents the community's authoritative determination of how values
  trade off. Different legitimate governance processes may yield
  different metrics.
\item
  The contraction \(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\) is the \emph{interface} between
  the factual and the normative. The obligation vector is factual; the
  interest covector reflects the governed metric; the satisfaction
  scalar is the verdict.
\item
  Stratification (Chapter 8) --- the boundaries, nullifiers, and
  constraint surfaces --- is \emph{partially factual and partially
  governed}. Some boundaries are structural invariants (the Hohfeldian
  correlative symmetry, the universality of abuse as a nullifier).
  Others are governance choices (where to set the age of majority, the
  threshold for informed consent).
\end{itemize}

\subsection{9.6 Constraints on Admissible
Metrics}\label{constraints-on-admissible-metrics}

Not every metric is admissible. Even on the governance account,
structural constraints narrow the space of legitimate choices.

\subsubsection{Symmetry}\label{symmetry}

The moral metric must be symmetric: \(g_{\mu\nu} = g_{\nu\mu}\). The
trade-off rate between welfare and justice must be the same regardless
of which direction the trade is made: if one unit of welfare can
compensate for \(k\) units of justice, then one unit of justice must
compensate for \(1/k\) units of welfare.

This is a mathematical constraint (symmetric bilinear forms are the
standard definition of a metric), but it has moral content. A metric
where trading welfare for justice costs more than trading justice for
welfare would encode an asymmetric privilege for one value over the
other --- not in magnitude (which is permissible and encoded in the
diagonal components), but in the \emph{structure of exchange}. This is
ruled out.

\subsubsection{Non-Degeneracy on Protected
Dimensions}\label{non-degeneracy-on-protected-dimensions}

The metric should not be \emph{fully} degenerate: there must be some
directions along which comparison is possible. A totally degenerate
metric (\(g_{\mu\nu} = 0\) for all \(\mu,\nu\)) would make all moral
comparisons impossible, rendering the framework vacuous.

More substantively, certain dimensions should have \emph{guaranteed
weight}: the metric should not assign zero weight to dimensions that a
legitimate governance process has identified as morally relevant. If a
community has determined that rights matter (Dimension 2 has positive
diagonal weight), a governance revision that sets \(g_{22} = 0\) ---
effectively declaring that rights don't exist --- requires a higher
level of constitutional justification.

\subsubsection{Invariance}\label{invariance}

The metric must respect the admissible transformation structure (Chapter
5, §5.5). Under Type 1 transformations (coordinate redescriptions), the
metric must transform as a (0,2)-tensor:

\[{\widetilde{g}}_{\mu\nu} = \frac{\partial x^{\alpha}}{\partial{\widetilde{x}}^{\mu}}\frac{\partial x^{\beta}}{\partial{\widetilde{x}}^{\nu}}g_{\alpha\beta}\]

This ensures that the trade-off structure does not depend on how the
situation is described. The metric is a geometric object, not a
coordinate artifact. The Bond Invariance Principle (Chapter 5, Axiom
5.1) requires this.

\subsubsection{Non-Discrimination}\label{non-discrimination}

The metric must not encode trade-offs that depend on morally irrelevant
features --- specifically, protected characteristics. If two situations
differ only in a morally irrelevant feature (the gender of the patient,
the race of the applicant), the metric must assign the same distances
and inner products.

\textbf{Formal statement.} Let \(\phi\mathcal{:M \rightarrow M}\) be a
transformation that changes only morally irrelevant features. Then:

\[g_{\mu\nu}(\phi(p)) = g_{\mu\nu}(p)\quad\text{for all }p\mathcal{\in M}\]

The metric is invariant under morally irrelevant transformations. This
is a constraint on the metric, not on the manifold: the manifold may
include morally irrelevant coordinates (they describe the situation),
but the metric must not depend on them.

\subsubsection{Consistency with Structural
Invariants}\label{consistency-with-structural-invariants}

The metric must be consistent with the structural invariants identified
in Chapter 8: the \(D_{4}\) group structure of Hohfeldian transitions,
the universality of certain nullifiers, and the cross-linguistic
transfer of deontic structure. These invariants are not governance
choices --- they are features of the moral manifold itself. The metric
must be compatible with them.

\textbf{Example.} The correlative symmetry O ↔ C requires that if the
metric assigns a positive weight to the obligation dimension for agent
\(a\), it assigns a corresponding positive weight to the claim dimension
for the correlative agent \(b\). A metric that violates correlative
symmetry is inadmissible.

\subsubsection{Summary of Constraints}\label{summary-of-constraints}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2342}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3491}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Constraint}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Mathematical Form}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Moral Content}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Symmetry & g\_μν = g\_νμ & Trade-off structure is bidirectional \\
Non-total-degeneracy & \(\det(g) \neq 0\) on at least some subspace &
Some comparisons must be possible \\
Tensor transformation & \(g\) transforms as (0,2)-tensor & Trade-offs
don't depend on description \\
Non-discrimination & \(g(\phi(p)) = g(p)\) for irrelevant \(\phi\) &
Morally irrelevant features don't affect trade-offs \\
Structural compatibility & Compatible with \(D_{4}\), nullifiers, etc. &
Metric respects known invariants \\
\end{longtable}
}

The space of admissible metrics is the space satisfying all five
constraints. It is large --- allowing genuine pluralism --- but not
unlimited. The constraints rule out the most egregious metrics
(discriminatory, inconsistent, description-dependent) while leaving room
for legitimate governance to determine the metric's specific structure.

\subsection{9.7 Learning the Metric from
Data}\label{learning-the-metric-from-data}

\subsubsection{The Empirical Project}\label{the-empirical-project}

A practical question: can the metric be \emph{learned} from data? If we
have access to moral judgments --- ordinal comparisons, trade-off
behavior, evaluations of situations --- can we infer the underlying
metric that rationalizes them?

This question connects the governance account to empirical ethics. The
governance account says the metric is determined by legitimate
institutional processes. But those processes produce \emph{outputs} ---
laws, regulations, institutional decisions, advisory column verdicts ---
from which the operative metric can, in principle, be
reverse-engineered.

\subsubsection{Formal Setup}\label{formal-setup}

\textbf{The metric learning problem.} Given a dataset of moral judgments
\(\{(p_{i},q_{i}, \succ )\}\), where \(p \succ q\) means ``situation
\(p\) is morally preferable to situation \(q\),'' find a metric
\(g_{\mu\nu}\) such that:

\[d_{g}(p_{i},\text{ideal}) < d_{g}(q_{i},\text{ideal})\quad\text{whenever }p_{i} \succ q_{i}\]

where \(d_{g}\) is the geodesic distance in the metric \(g\), and
``ideal'' is the point in \(\mathcal{M}\) representing the best
achievable moral state.

This is the moral analogue of \emph{metric learning} in machine
learning: given similarity judgments, find the metric that reproduces
them.

\subsubsection{Evidence from the Dear Abby
Corpus}\label{evidence-from-the-dear-abby-corpus}

The Dear Abby corpus --- 20,030 real moral dilemmas with expert
ground-truth responses, spanning 32 years (1985--2017) --- provides a
natural dataset for metric learning. Each letter presents a moral
situation; the columnist's response reveals an implicit judgment about
the relevant trade-offs.

Analysis reveals several features of the operative metric:

\textbf{Context-dependent weighting.} The relative weights of moral
dimensions shift by context (Chapter 5, §5.3): in family contexts, care
(Dimension 7) dominates; in workplace contexts, procedural legitimacy
(Dimension 8) and fairness (Dimension 3) dominate; in neighbor disputes,
rights (Dimension 2) dominate. This is evidence that the operative
metric is not constant but varies across the manifold --- precisely the
structure of a Riemannian metric field.

\textbf{Temporal stability of structure.} Despite variation in specific
advice over three decades, the \emph{structural} features of the metric
--- the relative ordering of dimension weights, the pattern of
couplings, the location of stratum boundaries --- remain stable. This
suggests that while the metric's parameters may drift, its qualitative
structure is robust. The moral manifold has a stable topology, even if
its geometry evolves.

\textbf{Correlative symmetry.} The correlative rates O ↔ C (87\%) and L
↔ N (82\%) are consistent across subperiods and context types. This is
evidence of a \emph{structural invariant} of the metric --- a feature
preserved under the variation that metric learning would discover.

\subsubsection{Evidence from BIP
Experiments}\label{evidence-from-bip-experiments}

The Bond Invariance Principle experiments across 109,294 passages in 11
languages provide a different kind of evidence. The key finding: the
deontic axis --- the obligation/permission distinction --- transfers at
100\% across all languages tested, even when specific moral content
fails to transfer.

\textbf{Interpretation for metric learning.} The deontic axis is a
\emph{structural feature of the metric} that is independent of language
and culture. It is not learned from data --- it is a constraint that
data universally satisfies. This supports the claim (§9.6) that some
features of the metric are non-negotiable structural invariants, while
others are legitimately variable across communities.

The pattern is suggestive:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter

  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2290}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4362}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Feature}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Status}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Evidence}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Deontic axis (O/L) & Structural invariant & \% cross-lingual transfer \\
Correlative symmetry & Structural invariant & --87\% across contexts \\
Nullifier structure & Structural invariant & Universal across domains \\
Dimension weightings & Governance variable & Context-dependent;
culture-sensitive \\
Off-diagonal couplings & Governance variable & Vary by tradition and
epoch \\
Lexicographic priority & Governance variable & Present in some systems,
absent in others \\
\end{longtable}
}

The structural invariants constrain the space of admissible metrics. The
governance-variable features are the parameters that legitimate
institutional processes determine.

\subsubsection{Challenges for Metric
Learning}\label{challenges-for-metric-learning}

\textbf{Underdetermination.} Many metrics may fit the same data. A
finite dataset of ordinal comparisons does not uniquely determine a
metric --- it determines only an equivalence class of metrics that are
ordinally equivalent. To pin down the cardinal structure (the actual
trade-off rates), richer data are needed: not just ``A is better than
B'' but ``A is \emph{twice as much} better than B as C is better than
D.''

\textbf{Noise.} Human moral judgments are noisy --- affected by framing,
mood, order effects, and cognitive biases. The metric learned from raw
judgments is contaminated by this noise. Denoising requires either very
large datasets (which the 109K-passage BIP corpus provides) or
structural assumptions (which the admissibility constraints of §9.6
provide).

\textbf{Whose judgments?} Aggregating moral judgments across persons
reintroduces the metric problem at a meta-level: what is the metric for
combining different people's judgments about the metric? The governance
account resolves this recursively: the combination procedure is itself a
governance choice, subject to legitimacy constraints.

\subsection{9.8 Pluralism and the
Meta-Metric}\label{pluralism-and-the-meta-metric}

\subsubsection{The Question}\label{the-question}

If different communities legitimately adopt different metrics, is there
a \emph{meta-metric} for comparing metrics? Can we say that one
community's metric is \emph{closer to correct} than another's?

\subsubsection{Three Positions}\label{three-positions}

\textbf{Metric monism.} There is exactly one correct moral metric
\(g^{*}\), and all governance processes are attempts to approximate it.
This is the realist position, transferred to the governance context. Its
difficulty: it requires a standard independent of governance, which the
governance account by design avoids.

\textbf{Metric relativism.} There is no basis for comparing metrics
across communities. If community A's metric permits a trade-off that
community B's metric forbids, neither is wrong --- they are simply
different. Its difficulty: it cannot account for moral criticism across
communities, which is a central feature of moral life. Calling another
community's practices ``wrong'' (as we do with slavery, genocide, and
systematic oppression) requires a standard that transcends the
community's own metric.

\textbf{Structured pluralism.} Metrics can be partially compared along
several dimensions --- but not totally ordered. There is no single
meta-metric, but there are \emph{meta-criteria}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Internal consistency.} A metric that violates its own
  structural constraints (e.g., a metric that encodes a trade-off
  between dimensions it elsewhere declares incommensurable) is defective
  by its own standards.
\item
  \textbf{Coverage.} A metric that assigns zero weight to an entire
  dimension of the moral manifold --- ignoring, say, the environmental
  dimension entirely --- has a deficiency: it cannot even formulate
  moral claims along the neglected dimension.
\item
  \textbf{Invariance preservation.} A metric that violates the
  structural invariants of §9.6 (correlative symmetry,
  non-discrimination, transformation consistency) is inadmissible by
  structural criteria, not by any community's particular moral
  commitments.
\item
  \textbf{Reflective stability.} A metric is reflectively stable if the
  community that endorses it would continue to endorse it after full
  information and ideal deliberation. A metric adopted under conditions
  of ignorance or coercion is reflectively unstable.
\item
  \textbf{Empirical adequacy.} A metric is empirically adequate if it
  rationalizes the community's considered moral judgments (not all
  judgments --- considered ones, after removing distortions from bias,
  ignorance, and coercion).
\end{enumerate}

These meta-criteria partially order the space of metrics without totally
ordering it. Two metrics may each satisfy all five criteria while
differing in their off-diagonal components --- representing genuinely
different but equally legitimate moral perspectives.

\subsubsection{Formal Statement}\label{formal-statement-1}

\textbf{Definition 9.1 (Partial Order on Metrics).} \emph{Define the
relation} \(g \preccurlyeq g'\) \emph{(metric} \(g'\) \emph{dominates
metric} \(g\)\emph{) if and only if} \(g'\) \emph{satisfies all five
meta-criteria at least as well as} \(g\)\emph{, and strictly better on
at least one. This relation is a partial order on the space of
admissible metrics.}

The partial order has the structure of a \emph{lattice} in favorable
cases: given two metrics, there may be a \emph{join} (the least upper
bound --- the weakest metric that dominates both) and a \emph{meet} (the
greatest lower bound --- the strongest metric dominated by both).
Whether the space of admissible metrics forms a lattice is an open
question with substantial mathematical interest.

\subsubsection{The Irreducibility of Metric
Disagreement}\label{the-irreducibility-of-metric-disagreement}

Structured pluralism implies that some metric disagreements are
\emph{irreducible}: they cannot be resolved by appeal to meta-criteria,
because both metrics satisfy all meta-criteria equally well. At this
level, disagreement is genuinely political --- it reflects different
legitimate choices about how to structure moral trade-offs.

This is not a defect of the framework. It is a structural theorem: the
space of admissible metrics, constrained by the criteria of §9.6, is not
a singleton. Genuine moral pluralism exists at the metric level --- and
any framework that pretends otherwise is either concealing its
assumptions or restricting the space of admissible moral perspectives
too narrowly.

The geometric framework's contribution is to make metric disagreement
\emph{precise}: we can say exactly where two metrics differ (in which
components \(g_{\mu\nu}\)), what moral consequences follow from the
difference, and which meta-criteria each metric satisfies. This is
progress --- not the progress of resolving all disagreement, but the
deeper progress of understanding its structure.

\subsection{9.9 The Metric as Moral
Infrastructure}\label{the-metric-as-moral-infrastructure}

\subsubsection{An Analogy}\label{an-analogy}

Consider a city's transportation infrastructure: roads, bridges, transit
lines, sidewalks. This infrastructure determines how people can move ---
which destinations are accessible, at what cost, by what routes. The
infrastructure is neither a natural fact (cities don't grow roads
spontaneously) nor a subjective preference (my desire for a road doesn't
create one). It is a \emph{governance artifact}: the output of
institutional processes (city planning, legislation, funding decisions)
with real consequences for how people live.

The moral metric is moral infrastructure. It determines how moral agents
can reason --- which values are comparable, at what trade-off rates,
through what deliberative routes. It is neither a natural fact nor a
subjective preference. It is a governance artifact with real
consequences for how moral life is structured.

And like physical infrastructure:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{It can be well-designed or poorly designed.} A city with no
  sidewalks privileges drivers over pedestrians; a moral metric with
  zero weight on environmental impact privileges present benefit over
  future harm.
\item
  \textbf{It is designed for a community.} The infrastructure of Tokyo
  differs from the infrastructure of rural Montana, and both may be
  well-designed for their contexts. Similarly, different communities may
  have different moral metrics, both well-suited to their circumstances.
\item
  \textbf{It constrains without determining.} Infrastructure constrains
  which trips are easy and which are hard, but it does not determine
  where anyone actually goes. The moral metric constrains which
  trade-offs are legitimate and which are not, but it does not determine
  which actions anyone actually takes. The metric is the field on which
  moral agency plays out --- not a substitute for agency.
\item
  \textbf{It requires maintenance and revision.} Infrastructure that was
  adequate in 1950 may be inadequate in 2025. A moral metric that was
  adequate before the development of powerful AI may be inadequate
  after. The governance account allows --- indeed requires --- periodic
  revision of the metric through legitimate institutional processes.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Implications for AI
Systems}\label{implications-for-ai-systems}

The governance account has direct implications for AI alignment. If the
moral metric is a governance artifact, then AI systems that make morally
significant decisions must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Be calibrated to the community's metric.} An AI system
  operating in a community with a utilitarian metric should not impose a
  lexicographic one, and vice versa.
\item
  \textbf{Be transparent about the metric.} The operative metric should
  be inspectable --- which dimensions are weighted, what trade-offs are
  permitted, where the constraint surfaces lie.
\item
  \textbf{Be updatable.} When the community revises its metric through
  legitimate processes, the AI system should be updated accordingly.
\item
  \textbf{Respect the structural constraints.} The invariance
  requirements (§9.6) apply to AI systems as much as to human moral
  reasoning. The Bond Invariance Principle requires that the AI's moral
  evaluations be invariant under admissible redescription --- a testable
  engineering requirement.
\end{enumerate}

This is the connection between the philosophical question of this
chapter (where does the metric come from?) and the engineering question
of Chapter 18 (how do we build ethical AI?). The governance account
provides the \emph{source} of the metric; the geometric framework
provides the \emph{form}; the engineering implementation provides the
\emph{mechanism}.

\subsection{9.10 Summary}\label{summary-2}

The moral metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) --- the structure of permissible
trade-offs between moral dimensions --- is the most consequential and
most contentious element of the geometric framework.

Four accounts of its origin:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1748}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2863}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2167}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Account}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Strengths}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Weaknesses}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Role of the Metric}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Realism & Explains moral progress, error & Epistemological and
metaphysical difficulties & A fact to be discovered \\
Constructivism & Grounds authority in reason & Regress of procedures;
underdetermination & A fixed point of ideal deliberation \\
Expressivism & Explains motivation, diversity & Cannot explain
convergence or structure & A projection of sensibility \\
Governance & Explains authority, revisability, pluralism; connects to
institutional practice & Requires legitimacy theory; risks deference to
unjust institutions & An artifact of legitimate institutional process \\
\end{longtable}
}

The governance account is the most natural fit for the geometric
framework. It locates the metric's authority in the same place that the
framework's \emph{implementation} must locate it: in the institutional
processes that determine, for a community, how moral trade-offs are to
be structured.

The space of admissible metrics is constrained by structural
requirements --- symmetry, non-discrimination, invariance, compatibility
with structural invariants --- but not reduced to a singleton. Genuine
moral pluralism exists within these constraints, and the framework makes
this pluralism precise rather than concealing it.

The metric is moral infrastructure: designed, revisable,
community-specific within structural constraints, and consequential for
how moral life is lived. The geometric framework does not provide the
metric. It provides the vocabulary for articulating, comparing,
constraining, and implementing whatever metric a community adopts
through legitimate governance.

\subsection{Technical Appendix}\label{technical-appendix-2}

\textbf{Definition A.1 (Admissible Metric).} \emph{A metric}
\(g_{\mu\nu}\) \emph{on the moral manifold} \(\mathcal{M}\) \emph{is
admissible if it satisfies:} 1. \emph{Symmetry:}
\(g_{\mu\nu} = g_{\nu\mu}\)\emph{.} 2. \emph{Non-total-degeneracy: there
exists a subspace} \(V \subset T_{p}\mathcal{M}\) \emph{of dimension}
\(\geq 1\) \emph{on which} \(g|_{V}\) \emph{is non-degenerate.} 3.
\emph{Tensor transformation:} \(g\) \emph{transforms as a (0,2)-tensor
under Type 1 transformations.} 4. \emph{Non-discrimination:}
\(g_{\mu\nu}(\phi(p)) = g_{\mu\nu}(p)\) \emph{for all morally irrelevant
transformations} \(\phi\)\emph{.} 5. \emph{Structural compatibility:}
\(g\) \emph{is compatible with the} \(D_{4}\) \emph{group structure and
known nullifier constraints.}

\textbf{Proposition 9.1 (Non-Uniqueness of Admissible Metrics).}
\emph{The space of admissible metrics on} \(\mathcal{M}\) \emph{is
non-empty and has dimension} \(> 0\)\emph{. In particular, there exist
admissible metrics that differ in their off-diagonal components while
satisfying all five admissibility conditions.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}. The space Adm(M) is non-empty by Theorem 9.1(i),
and has dimension ≥ 10 by Theorem 9.1(iii). In particular, the flat
metric δ\_μν and any perturbation δ\_μν + ε h\_μν (for symmetric h
respecting the D₄ and U(1)\_H constraints) are both admissible for
sufficiently small ε \textgreater{} 0. These metrics differ on
off-diagonal components, establishing positive dimension.
□}\(gg'\epsilon h_{\mu\nu}\epsilon g' = g + \epsilon h▫\)

Note on logical structure. This proposition's proof references Theorem
9.1 (stated below). The logical dependence is not circular: Theorem 9.1
provides the explicit dimension count for Adm(M), which is used here
only as an existence claim (Adm(M) is non-empty with positive
dimension). The existence of admissible metrics can also be established
directly: the flat Euclidean metric on ℝ⁹ satisfies all axioms, and
small perturbations of it remain admissible.

\textbf{Proposition 9.2 (Structural Invariants Constrain the Metric).}
\emph{If the correlative symmetry rates}
\(P(\text{O} \leftrightarrow \text{C}) = p_{1}\) \emph{and}
\(P(\text{L} \leftrightarrow \text{N}) = p_{2}\) \emph{are fixed
structural invariants, then the diagonal components of the metric on the
rights/duties dimension (Dimension 2) must satisfy:}

\[g_{22}(a) = f(p_{1},p_{2}) \cdot g_{22}(b)\quad\text{for correlative agents }a,b\]

\emph{where} \(f\) \emph{is a function of the correlative rates. Perfect
correlative symmetry (}\(p_{1} = p_{2} = 1\)\emph{) implies} \(f = 1\)
\emph{--- identical metric weights for correlative partners.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}. Under the correlative swap s\_c: O ↔ C, L ↔ N, the
expected satisfaction functional must be invariant: ⟨Σ⟩\_\{s\_c\} = ⟨Σ⟩.
The proportionality factor f must satisfy: (1) f(p₁, p₂) =
g₂₂(a)/g₂₂(b), which by the symmetry of the correlative exchange yields
f(p₁, p₂) = p₁/p₂; (2) f(1,1) = 1 (perfect symmetry implies equal metric
weights); and (3) f(p₁, p₂) · f(p₂, p₁) = 1 (applying the swap twice
returns to the original). All three are satisfied by f(p₁, p₂) = p₁/p₂,
and this is the unique solution consistent with the correlative symmetry
constraint. □}\(abp_{1}baf(p_{1},p_{2})▫\)

\subsubsection{}\label{section-1}

\subsubsection{Characterization of the Admissible Metric
Space}\label{characterization-of-the-admissible-metric-space}

The preceding propositions established that the space of admissible
metrics is non-trivial. We now prove structural results that
characterize this space precisely.

\textbf{Lemma 9.1 (D₄ Constraint on Metric Components). {[}Conditional
on Def. A.1, condition 5{]}} \emph{Let g\_μν be an admissible metric on
M. The D₄ structural compatibility requirement forces g\_\{2μ\}(O) =
g\_\{2μ\}(C) = g\_\{2μ\}(N) = g\_\{2μ\}(L) for all μ. This eliminates 27
degrees of freedom.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} D₄ acts on the Hohfeldian positions via the
4-cycle r = (O C N L) and reflection s\_c = (O C)(L N). Structural
compatibility (Def. A.1, condition 5) requires g\_μν(σ(p)) = g\_μν(p)
for all σ ∈ D₄. Since D₄ acts transitively on \{O, C, N, L\}, the orbit
of O under ⟨r⟩ is the entire set, so g\_\{2ν\}(O) = g\_\{2ν\}(C) =
g\_\{2ν\}(N) = g\_\{2ν\}(L). This collapses four independent values to
one for each of nine metric components g\_\{2μ\}, eliminating 9 × (4 −
1) = 27 degrees of freedom. □

\textbf{Lemma 9.2 (U(1)\_H Constraint on the Harm Dimension).
{[}Conditional on Def. A.1, condition 5{]} The U(1)\_H symmetry
generates a Killing vector field ξ = ∂/∂x⁷ along Dimension 7. The
Killing equation ensures that the metric is independent of x⁷
(∂7g\_\{μν\} = 0 for all μ, ν). Combined with the product-structure
interpretation of Axiom A5 --- that the harm dimension is geometrically
orthogonal to all other dimensions --- this forces g\_\{7μ\} = 0 for all
μ ≠ 7. This eliminates 8 off-diagonal degrees of freedom.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} The U(1)\_H action is a continuous one-parameter
group of isometries along Dimension 7. Its generator is the Killing
vector field ξ = ∂/∂x⁷. The Killing equation ∇\_μ ξ\_ν + ∇\_ν ξ\_μ = 0
forces ∂₇g\_\{μν\} = 0 for all μ, ν --- i.e., the metric is independent
of the harm coordinate x⁷ (if g\_\{7μ\} ≠ 0 for some μ ≠ 7, geodesics in
the harm direction would couple to non-harm dimensions, breaking the
independence of harm magnitude). This eliminates 8 off-diagonal
components: g\_\{71\}, g\_\{72\}, \ldots, g\_\{76\}, g\_\{78\},
g\_\{79\}. □

\textbf{Theorem 9.1 (Structure of the Admissible Metric Space).
{[}Conditional on Def. A.1{]} Let Sym⁺₉(G) denote the space of
positive-definite symmetric bilinear forms on ℝ⁹ invariant under the
gauge group G = D₄ × U(1)\_H. The space Adm(M) of admissible metrics
admits a Whitney stratification:}

(i) Adm(M) is non-empty: the flat metric δ\_μν ∈ Adm(M).

(ii) Adm(M) has the structure of a stratified space Adm(M) = ⨆\_α S\_α,
where: the top stratum S₀ consists of all fully non-degenerate
admissible metrics (those with det(g\textbar ₐ) \textgreater{} 0 on
every protected subspace ₐ), and is a smooth open submanifold of
Sym⁺₉(G) of dimension ≥ 10; the boundary strata S\_k (k ≥ 1) consist of
metrics where non-degeneracy fails on exactly k protected dimensions,
representing incommensurability surfaces where specific value trade-offs
become undefined; the stratification satisfies the Whitney (b)
condition, inheriting regularity from the smooth structure of the
ambient space.

(iii) dim S₀ ≥ 45 − 27 − 8 = 10. The boundary strata have strictly lower
dimension.

\emph{\textbf{Proof. (i) Non-emptiness. The flat metric δ\_μν satisfies
all five admissibility conditions (symmetry, non-degeneracy, tensor
transformation, non-discrimination, and structural compatibility with
G). Since det(δ\textbar ₐ) = 1 \textgreater{} 0 on every subspace, δ ∈
S₀ (the top stratum). (ii) Stratified structure. The gauge-invariance
constraints are linear, so Sym⁺₉(G) is a linear subspace of the
45-dimensional space of symmetric bilinear forms, intersected with the
open positive-definite cone. The admissibility conditions partition this
space into strata by the rank of the restriction g\textbar ₐ to each
protected subspace ₐ (corresponding to protected moral dimensions from
Def. A.1, condition 2). The top stratum S₀ = \{g ∈ Sym⁺₉(G) :
det(g\textbar ₐ) \textgreater{} 0 for all protected ₐ, plus
non-discrimination and structural compatibility\} is the intersection of
finitely many open conditions (det(g\textbar ₐ) \textgreater{} 0 is open
for each ₐ) with the linear and smooth constraints, yielding an open
subset of a finite-dimensional affine subspace --- hence a smooth open
submanifold. The boundary strata S\_k = \{g : det(g\textbar ₐ) = 0 for
exactly k protected subspaces\} are smooth submanifolds of codimension k
(since each det(g\textbar ₐ) = 0 is a smooth hypersurface in generic
position). The Whitney (b) condition holds because the strata are
defined by polynomial equalities and inequalities on the components of
g, and polynomial stratifications satisfy Whitney (b) (see Bochnak,
Coste, and Roy, Real Algebraic Geometry, §9.7). Path-connectedness of S₀
follows from convexity: if g, g' ∈ S₀, then (1−t)g + tg' ∈ Sym⁺₉(G) for
all t ∈ {[}0,1{]} (positive-definite forms are closed under convex
combination), and det(((1−t)g + tg')\textbar ₐ) \textgreater{} 0 for all
t ∈ {[}0,1{]} (by the Minkowski determinant inequality: det(A +
B)\^{}\{1/n\} ≥ det(A)\^{}\{1/n\} + det(B)\^{}\{1/n\} for
positive-definite A, B). Thus S₀ is convex, hence path-connected. (iii)
Dimension bound. A symmetric 9×9 matrix has 45 independent components.
D₄ invariance eliminates 27 (Lemma 9.1). U(1)\_H invariance eliminates 8
(Lemma 9.2). The open conditions (non-degeneracy, non-discrimination) do
not reduce dimension. Thus dim S₀ ≥ 10. The boundary strata have dim
S\_k = dim S₀ − k. □}}

\textbf{Theorem 9.2 (Structured Pluralism). {[}Conditional on Def. A.1
and Def. 9.1{]}} \emph{The partial order ≼ on Adm(M) is not total. That
is, there exist admissible metrics g, g' ∈ Adm(M) such that neither g ≼
g' nor g' ≼ g.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof. We construct two admissible metrics in S₀ that are
incomparable under ≼. The argument proceeds in four steps.\\
\strut \\
Step 0 (Explicit G-invariant perturbation directions). We first
construct two linearly independent elements of Sym₉(G), the space of D₄
× U(1)\_H-invariant symmetric bilinear forms on ℝ⁹. By Lemma 9.1, D₄
invariance requires that the metric components g\_\{μν\} are constant
across Hohfeldian states \{O, C, L, N\} for each dimension pair (μ, ν).
By Lemma 9.2, U(1)\_H invariance requires ∂₇g\_\{μν\} = 0. These
constraints are linear and leave a ≥10-dimensional subspace (Theorem
9.1(iii)). Within this subspace, define the following two elements
explicitly:\\
e¹³\_\{μν\} = ½(δ\_\{μ1\}δ\_\{ν3\} + δ\_\{μ3\}δ\_\{ν1\}), the symmetric
tensor with e¹³\_\{13\} = e¹³\_\{31\} = ½ and all other components
zero;\\
e⁴⁵\_\{μν\} = ½(δ\_\{μ4\}δ\_\{ν5\} + δ\_\{μ5\}δ\_\{ν4\}), the symmetric
tensor with e⁴⁵\_\{45\} = e⁴⁵\_\{54\} = ½ and all other components
zero.\\
Both are symmetric by construction. Both satisfy D₄ invariance: e¹³
couples Dimensions 1 and 3 (consequences and fairness), which lie in the
same D₄-orbit (both are evaluative dimensions unaffected by Hohfeldian
state transitions); e⁴⁵ couples Dimensions 4 and 5 (autonomy and trust),
similarly within a single orbit. Both satisfy U(1)\_H invariance:
neither has components along Dimension 7 (harm), so ∂₇e\_\{μν\} = 0
trivially. Both are spatially constant, satisfying non-discrimination.
They are linearly independent (they have disjoint nonzero entries). Thus
e¹³, e⁴⁵ ∈ Sym₉(G) are two explicit, independent, admissible
perturbation directions.\\
\strut \\
Step 1 (Tangent directions). δ ∈ S₀ (Theorem 9.1(i)). S₀ is open in the
affine subspace δ + Sym₉(G) ⊂ Sym⁺₉ (Theorem 9.1(ii)). Therefore T\_δ S₀
= Sym₉(G), and in particular e¹³, e⁴⁵ ∈ T\_δ S₀.\\
\strut \\
Step 2 (Admissible curves). Define γ₁(t) = δ + t e¹³ and γ₂(t) = δ + t
e⁴⁵ for t ≥ 0. Since S₀ is open and δ ∈ S₀, there exists ε
\textgreater{} 0 such that B(δ, ε) ∩ (δ + Sym₉(G)) ⊂ S₀. For 0
\textless{} t \textless{} ε/‖e¹³‖ (resp. ε/‖e⁴⁵‖), we have γ₁(t), γ₂(t)
∈ S₀ ⊂ Adm(M). Fix such a t and set g = γ₁(t), g' = γ₂(t).\\
\strut \\
Step 3 (Incomparability). g = δ + t e¹³ assigns nonzero off-diagonal
weight g\_\{13\} = g\_\{31\} = t/2 \textgreater{} 0 to the
consequences--fairness cross-term, making welfare--justice trade-offs
metrically meaningful. g' = δ + t e⁴⁵ does the same for autonomy--trust.
Consider a community C₁ where welfare--justice coupling is empirically
supported, and a community C₂ where autonomy--trust coupling is
supported. Then g achieves higher empirical adequacy for C₁ while g'
achieves higher adequacy for C₂. Under any ordering ≼ respecting
empirical adequacy, neither g ≼ g' nor g' ≼ g. □}}

This is not a deficiency of the framework but a theorem: genuine moral
pluralism --- the existence of incomparable but individually coherent
moral evaluations --- is a mathematical consequence of the admissibility
constraints, not a philosophical assumption.

❖

\emph{The metric is not a datum and not a guess. It is a decision.}

\emph{A decision made by institutions, constrained by structure,
revisable by process, and consequential for every moral evaluation that
operates within it.}

\emph{This chapter has argued that the question ``Which metric is
correct?'' is not a philosophical puzzle with a unique answer, but a
governance challenge with structural constraints.}

\emph{The geometric framework does not resolve the challenge. It gives
us the language to face it honestly --- to know exactly what we are
choosing when we choose a metric, and what follows from the choice we
make.}

\section{Chapter 10: Moral Dynamics --- Parallel Transport, Holonomy,
and the Curvature of Moral
Space}\label{chapter-10-moral-dynamics-parallel-transport-holonomy-and-the-curvature-of-moral-space}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya presents her findings to two audiences in different orders
and gets two different outcomes. Path A: she begins with Mrs. Voss's
story, then shows the statistical pattern. The team is moved; they want
to act. Path B: she leads with statistics, then mentions individual
cases. Executives focus on legal exposure and suggest a disclaimer. Same
facts, same obligation---but the parallel transport of the argument
through different conversational paths produces different moral
conclusions. This is holonomy. The curvature of HealthBridge's
organizational moral space means the order of deliberation changes the
outcome. She cannot simply present `the truth.' She must choose a path,
and the path matters.}
\end{quote}

\subsection{10.1 Ethics in Motion}\label{ethics-in-motion}

The preceding chapters developed a static picture: the moral manifold
\(\mathcal{M}\) (Chapter 5), the tensors that live on it (Chapter 6),
the metric that measures them (Chapters 6, 9), and the stratified
boundaries where the rules change (Chapter 8). This apparatus captures
the geometry of a \emph{single moment} --- the structure of obligations,
interests, and trade-offs at one point in moral space.

But ethics is not static. Situations change. Obligations evolve. Moral
understanding develops. A promise made yesterday must be carried into
today's altered circumstances. A principle learned in one context must
be applied in another. An obligation incurred in peacetime must be
renegotiated in crisis. Ethics is not a photograph but a trajectory ---
a path through structured space.

This chapter introduces the dynamics of geometric ethics: how moral
tensors change as we move through the moral manifold, how obligations
are ``transported'' across changing circumstances, and what it means for
moral evaluation to be path-dependent. The mathematical tools are the
covariant derivative, parallel transport, holonomy, and curvature ---
developed abstractly in Chapter 4 (§4.6) and now given their moral
content.

\subsection{10.2 The Problem of Moral
Change}\label{the-problem-of-moral-change}

Consider a simple scenario. Alice promises to meet Bob for coffee on
Saturday. On Friday, Alice learns that Carol is having a medical
emergency and needs help on Saturday morning. What happens to Alice's
obligation to Bob?

A static analysis takes two snapshots:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  At \(t_{0}\): Alice has obligation \(O(t_{0})\) = ``meet Bob for
  coffee.''
\item
  At \(t_{1}\): Alice has obligation \(O(t_{1})\) = ``help Carol with
  the emergency.''
\end{itemize}

These are just two points in moral space, with two obligation vectors.
The static analysis can compare them --- \(O(t_{1})\) points in a
different direction from \(O(t_{0})\) --- but it cannot answer the
dynamic questions:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{How did} \(O(t_{0})\) \emph{\textbf{become}}
  \(O(t_{1})\)\textbf{?} Was the obligation to Bob destroyed,
  overridden, suspended, or transformed?
\item
  \textbf{Is there continuity?} Is \(O(t_{1})\) the ``same'' obligation
  as \(O(t_{0})\), reoriented by circumstances? Or is it a new
  obligation that replaced the old one?
\item
  \textbf{Is there residue?} Does the displaced obligation to Bob leave
  a trace --- a secondary obligation to apologize, to reschedule, to
  acknowledge the broken commitment?
\item
  \textbf{Is the result path-dependent?} Would Alice's final obligation
  be different if she had learned about Carol's emergency \emph{before}
  making the promise to Bob?
\end{enumerate}

These are questions about \emph{moral dynamics} --- how moral tensors
evolve along paths through \(\mathcal{M}\). To answer them, we need the
apparatus of connections, parallel transport, and curvature.

\subsection{10.3 The Moral Connection}\label{the-moral-connection}

\subsubsection{Comparing Obligations Across
Contexts}\label{comparing-obligations-across-contexts}

On a flat space, comparing vectors at different points is trivial:
translate one to the other's location and compare. On a curved space,
there is no canonical translation --- the comparison depends on the
\emph{path} taken between the two points. A \emph{connection} is the
additional structure that specifies how to make such comparisons.

\textbf{Definition 10.1 (Moral Connection).} \emph{A moral connection is
an affine connection} \(\nabla\) \emph{on the moral manifold}
\(\mathcal{M}\) \emph{(within each stratum) that specifies, for any
direction of change} \(X\) \emph{and any obligation field} \(O\)\emph{,
the rate of change of} \(O\) \emph{in the direction} \(X\)\emph{:}

Remark. The definition of the moral connection is restricted to
individual strata. Across stratum boundaries, the connection may be
discontinuous. The junction conditions governing cross-boundary behavior
--- including continuity of parallel transport and matching of holonomy
--- are stated formally in Definition 13.6.

\[\nabla_{X}O = \text{“how }O\text{ changes as the situation moves in direction }X\text{”}\]

In coordinates, the covariant derivative of an obligation vector field
\(O^{\mu}\) in the direction \(\partial_{i}\) is:

\[(\nabla_{i}O)^{\mu} = \frac{\partial O^{\mu}}{\partial x^{i}} + \Gamma_{i\nu}^{\mu}O^{\nu}\]

The first term, \(\partial O^{\mu}/\partial x^{i}\), is the \emph{naive}
rate of change --- how the components of \(O\) change if we simply track
them in coordinates. The second term, \(\Gamma_{i\nu}^{\mu}O^{\nu}\), is
the \emph{correction} --- the adjustment needed because the coordinate
system itself is changing as we move through moral space. The
Christoffel symbols \(\Gamma_{i\nu}^{\mu}\) encode this correction.

\subsubsection{What the Connection
Encodes}\label{what-the-connection-encodes}

The moral connection has a precise interpretation: it encodes how
\emph{moral concepts translate across contexts}.

Consider the obligation ``be kind.'' In a family context, kindness means
warmth, patience, emotional availability. In a professional context,
kindness means fairness, clarity, respect for boundaries. The
\emph{word} is the same; the \emph{content} --- the obligation vector
--- has rotated.

The connection \(\nabla\) specifies exactly how this rotation occurs.
Moving from the family region of \(\mathcal{M}\) to the professional
region, the Christoffel symbols tell us:

\[\Gamma_{(\text{context}),7}^{7} < 0\quad\text{(care obligation decreases)}\]

\[\Gamma_{(\text{context}),7}^{8} > 0\quad\text{(procedural legitimacy obligation increases)}\]

The connection does not say whether this rotation is ``correct'' ---
that depends on the metric (Chapter 9). It says: \emph{if you carry the
obligation faithfully from one context to another, here is how its
components change}. The connection is the \emph{grammar} of moral
translation.

\subsubsection{The Levi-Civita
Connection}\label{the-levi-civita-connection}

If the moral manifold is equipped with a metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) (Chapter
6), there is a distinguished connection: the \emph{Levi-Civita
connection}, which is the unique connection that is:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Metric-compatible}: \(\nabla g = 0\). Parallel transport
  preserves inner products --- if two obligations are orthogonal at one
  point, they remain orthogonal after transport.
\item
  \textbf{Torsion-free}:
  \(\nabla_{X}Y - \nabla_{Y}X = \lbrack X,Y\rbrack\). There is no
  ``twisting'' of the coordinate frame as we move through moral space.
\end{enumerate}

The Christoffel symbols of the Levi-Civita connection are determined
entirely by the metric:

\[\Gamma_{\nu\rho}^{\mu} = \frac{1}{2}g^{\mu\sigma}\left( \frac{\partial g_{\sigma\nu}}{\partial x^{\rho}} + \frac{\partial g_{\sigma\rho}}{\partial x^{\nu}} - \frac{\partial g_{\nu\rho}}{\partial x^{\sigma}} \right)\]

This is significant: \textbf{if we know the metric, we know the
connection.} The governance account of Chapter 9 --- which determines
the metric --- thereby also determines the dynamics. The trade-off
structure and the transport rules are not independent choices; both are
encoded in \(g_{\mu\nu}\).

\subsubsection{Non-Metric Connections}\label{non-metric-connections}

Must the moral connection be the Levi-Civita connection? Not
necessarily. A connection with \emph{torsion} would mean that the moral
coordinate frame itself twists as we move --- that there is a systematic
asymmetry in how moral concepts translate, beyond what the metric
accounts for.

\textbf{Example.} Suppose that transporting an obligation from context A
to context B and then from B to C yields a different result than
transporting directly from A to C, \emph{even after accounting for
curvature}. This excess rotation is torsion. It would represent a
structural asymmetry in moral translation: the order in which you
traverse contexts matters, not because of curvature (which is symmetric)
but because of a systematic twist.

Whether moral space has torsion is an open empirical question (Chapter
29). For the remainder of this chapter, we work with the torsion-free
Levi-Civita connection, noting where the analysis would change with
torsion.

\subsection{10.4 Parallel Transport of
Obligations}\label{parallel-transport-of-obligations}

\includesvg[width=5.63077in,height=4.575in]{media/image14.svg}

\textbar{} Parallel Transport \& Holonomy

\subsubsection{The Concept}\label{the-concept}

\emph{Parallel transport} is the operation of carrying a vector along a
path while keeping it ``as constant as possible,'' given the connection.
See Figure 8 for a visual schematic.

\textbf{Definition 10.2 (Parallel Transport).} \emph{An obligation
vector} \(O\) \emph{is parallel-transported along a curve} \(\gamma(t)\)
\emph{in} \(\mathcal{M}\) \emph{if:}

\[\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O = 0\]

\emph{That is, the covariant derivative of} \(O\) \emph{along the curve
vanishes.}

In coordinates, this becomes the system of ordinary differential
equations:

\[\frac{dO^{\mu}}{dt} + \Gamma_{\nu\rho}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}O^{\rho} = 0\]

Given an initial obligation vector \(O(0)\) at \(\gamma(0)\), the
parallel transport equations determine a unique \(O(t)\) at every point
along the curve.

\subsubsection{Moral Interpretation}\label{moral-interpretation-1}

Parallel transport is the \emph{faithful maintenance} of an obligation
across changing circumstances. When Alice carries her promise to Bob
from a normal Saturday context into a Saturday-with-emergency context,
she is parallel-transporting the obligation.

But ``faithful maintenance'' does not mean ``keeping the same
components.'' If the coordinate system --- the local meaning of ``be
helpful,'' ``be punctual,'' ``be caring'' --- changes from context to
context, then faithful maintenance requires adjusting the components to
compensate. The obligation is the \emph{same} in a geometric sense (it
satisfies \(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O = 0\)), but its expression in local
coordinates changes.

\textbf{Example: Carrying ``keep your promise'' across contexts.}

Let \(O(0) = (0,0.8,0,0,0,0,0.4,0,0)\) --- an obligation with strong
duty component (\(O^{2} = 0.8\)) and moderate care component
(\(O^{7} = 0.4\)).

As Alice moves from the ``casual Saturday'' context to the ``emergency
Saturday'' context, the connection rotates the obligation:

\[O^{1}\text{ increases (welfare dimension becomes salient)}\]

\[O^{2}\text{ decreases (the promise’s binding force weakens in emergency)}\]

\[O^{7}\text{ remains stable (care is context-robust)}\]

The parallel-transported obligation might be:

\[O(1) \approx (0.5,0.3,0,0,0,0,0.4,0,0)\]

The duty component has partially converted to a welfare component ---
the obligation has \emph{rotated} in moral space. The promise has not
been destroyed; it has been faithfully carried into a new context where
its expression changes.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{What Is \emph{Not} Parallel
Transport}{What Is Not Parallel Transport}}\label{what-is-not-parallel-transport}

Not every change in moral obligations is parallel transport. Some
changes represent \emph{genuine new information} --- a new obligation
that arises from the situation, not a transported version of the old
one. If Carol asks Alice directly for help (creating a new obligation),
this is not the old obligation transported --- it is a new vector added
to the tangent space.

The covariant derivative distinguishes these cases. If
\(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O = 0\), the change in \(O\) is entirely due to
transport (the obligation is faithfully maintained). If
\(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O \neq 0\), there is a \emph{genuine} change ---
new information, new duties, new considerations --- beyond what
transport accounts for. The magnitude \(|\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O|\)
measures the \emph{rate of genuine moral change} at each point along the
path.

\subsection{10.5 Holonomy: The Path-Dependence of Moral
Evaluation}\label{holonomy-the-path-dependence-of-moral-evaluation}

\subsubsection{The Phenomenon}\label{the-phenomenon}

The most striking consequence of curvature is \emph{holonomy}: if we
parallel-transport a vector around a closed loop, it may not return to
its original value. The vector has been ``rotated'' by the loop, even
though the loop returns to the same point.

\textbf{Definition 10.3 (Holonomy).} \emph{Let}
\(\gamma:\lbrack 0,1\mathcal{\rbrack \rightarrow M}\) \emph{be a closed
loop with} \(\gamma(0) = \gamma(1) = p\)\emph{. The holonomy of}
\(\gamma\) \emph{is the linear transformation}
\(H_{\gamma}:T_{p}\mathcal{M \rightarrow}T_{p}\mathcal{M}\)
\emph{defined by:}

\[H_{\gamma}(O) = \text{parallel transport of }O\text{ around }\gamma\]

\emph{If} \(H_{\gamma} = \text{id}\) \emph{for all loops}
\(\gamma\)\emph{, the connection is flat and moral space has no
curvature at} \(p\)\emph{. If} \(H_{\gamma} \neq \text{id}\) \emph{for
some} \(\gamma\)\emph{, the connection has nontrivial curvature.}

\subsubsection{A Moral Example}\label{a-moral-example}

Consider the following circuit in moral space:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Start}: Alice has a normal friendship with Bob. Obligation:
  moderate care (\(O^{7} = 0.5\)).
\item
  \textbf{Step 1}: Alice learns Bob's secret. The obligation gains a
  confidentiality component (\(O^{5}\) increases --- privacy dimension
  becomes active).
\item
  \textbf{Step 2}: Alice discovers the secret involves potential harm to
  Carol. The obligation acquires a duty-to-warn component (\(O^{1}\)
  increases --- welfare dimension activates).
\item
  \textbf{Step 3}: Alice warns Carol; the harm is averted. The immediate
  welfare obligation is discharged (\(O^{1}\) returns to baseline).
\item
  \textbf{Step 4}: The situation resolves; Alice and Bob return to
  ``normal'' friendship.
\end{enumerate}

Has the obligation returned to its original value? Geometrically, we
have parallel-transported the obligation around a loop: normal
friendship → confidentiality → duty-to-warn → resolution → normal
friendship.

If moral space is \emph{flat}, the obligation returns exactly to
\(O^{7} = 0.5\): the friendship is unchanged. But if moral space is
\emph{curved}, the holonomy \(H_{\gamma}\) rotates the obligation. The
care component may have shifted:

\[H_{\gamma}(O) = (0,0,0,0,0.1,0,0.35,0,0.05)\]

The care dimension has diminished (\(0.5 \rightarrow 0.35\)), a privacy
residue persists (\(O^{5} = 0.1\)), and an epistemic component has
appeared (\(O^{9} = 0.05\) --- Alice now evaluates the friendship with
greater caution). The loop has left a mark.

This is the geometric content of the moral intuition that
\textbf{experiences leave permanent traces}. A relationship that
survives a betrayal is not the same as one that never faced one. A
society that has gone through a war is not the same as one that has not,
even if it returns to ``peace.'' The holonomy measures the
\emph{irreducible residue} of moral experience.

\subsubsection{The Holonomy Group}\label{the-holonomy-group}

The set of all holonomy transformations at a point \(p\) forms a group
--- the \emph{holonomy group} \(\text{Hol}(\nabla,p)\):

\[\text{Hol}(\nabla,p) = \{ H_{\gamma}:\gamma\text{ is a loop at }p\}\]

The holonomy group captures the full range of transformations that moral
experience can induce. If \(\text{Hol}(\nabla,p)\) is trivial (only the
identity), moral space is flat at \(p\) --- all loops preserve
obligations exactly. If \(\text{Hol}(\nabla,p)\) is large, the curvature
is rich --- many different loops produce many different rotations.

\textbf{Connection to the} \(D_{4}\) \textbf{structure.} In stratified
moral space (Chapter 8), the holonomy around a loop that crosses
Hohfeldian boundaries is constrained by the \(D_{4}\) group. A loop that
enters the obligation stratum and returns to the liberty stratum may
induce a transformation from \(D_{4}\) --- a rotation or reflection of
the Hohfeldian square. The holonomy group of the stratified moral
manifold includes \(D_{4}\) as a discrete subgroup, contributing to what
might be called the \emph{non-abelian} character of moral dynamics.

\textbf{Selective path dependence.} {[}Empirical result
(preliminary).{]} Experimental testing of path dependence across 8 moral
scenarios (N = 640 evaluations) reveals that the non-abelian structure
is selective: path dependence was detected in exactly 2 of 8 scenarios
(combined χ² = 72.14, p \textless{} 10⁻⁸), and both path-dependent
scenarios involved considerations that point to different Hohfeldian
bond types.

In the journalist scenario (truth-telling vs. source protection), truth
considerations point toward Obligation while protection considerations
point toward Claim. Presenting truth first yields 77.5\% O, 22.5\% C;
presenting protection first yields 5\% O, 95\% C. The dominant
classification flips from O to C. In the teacher scenario (academic
integrity vs. compassion), integrity points toward Liberty while
compassion points toward Obligation. The order of presentation again
flips the dominant verdict between L and O.

The remaining 6 scenarios---where both considerations point to the same
bond type---showed no path dependence (p \textgreater{} 0.2 for all).
This yields a precise structural prediction: within-type moral
operations commute; cross-type operations do not. The non-abelian
character of the D₄ gauge group (§12.3) is not a global property of all
moral reasoning but a local property of boundary-crossing deliberation.

\subsection{10.6 The Curvature of Moral
Space}\label{the-curvature-of-moral-space}

\subsubsection{The Riemann Curvature
Tensor}\label{the-riemann-curvature-tensor-1}

The curvature of the moral manifold is measured by the Riemann curvature
tensor (Chapter 4, Definition 4.5):

\[R_{\nu\alpha\beta}^{\mu} = \frac{\partial\Gamma_{\nu\beta}^{\mu}}{\partial x^{\alpha}} - \frac{\partial\Gamma_{\nu\alpha}^{\mu}}{\partial x^{\beta}} + \Gamma_{\sigma\alpha}^{\mu}\Gamma_{\nu\beta}^{\sigma} - \Gamma_{\sigma\beta}^{\mu}\Gamma_{\nu\alpha}^{\sigma}\]

This (1,3)-tensor measures the path-dependence of parallel transport:
transporting an obligation vector \(O\) around an infinitesimal
parallelogram spanned by directions \(X\) and \(Y\) produces a change:

\[\delta O^{\mu} = R_{\nu\alpha\beta}^{\mu}O^{\nu}X^{\alpha}Y^{\beta} \cdot (\text{area of parallelogram})\]

If \(R = 0\), the space is flat and transport is path-independent. If
\(R \neq 0\), the result of transport depends on the path taken --- and
the Riemann tensor tells us exactly how much and in what direction.

\subsubsection{Sources of Moral
Curvature}\label{sources-of-moral-curvature}

What causes moral space to be curved? Since the Levi-Civita connection
is determined by the metric, the curvature is determined by how the
metric varies across \(\mathcal{M}\). Moral curvature arises wherever
the trade-off structure changes from point to point --- which is to say,
generically.

\textbf{Source 1: Context-dependent weighting.} The empirical finding
that moral dimension weights shift by context (Chapter 5, §5.3) --- care
dominates in family contexts, fairness in institutional contexts ---
directly implies a varying metric, and hence nonzero curvature. The
Christoffel symbols computed from these variations are nonzero, and the
Riemann tensor formed from them is generically nonzero.

\textbf{Source 2: Commitment structure.} Promises, contracts, and
relationships create local deformations of the moral metric. Near a
promise, the duty dimension (\(x^{2}\)) acquires extra weight --- the
metric \(g_{22}\) increases locally. This local deformation curves moral
space, just as mass curves spacetime in general relativity. The analogy
is structural: in both cases, curvature arises from the inhomogeneous
distribution of a ``source'' (mass-energy in physics; moral commitment
in ethics).

\textbf{Source 3: Threshold proximity. Near a stratum boundary (Chapter
8), the metric changes rapidly --- it may even become singular. This
rapid variation produces large curvature. Close to the consent
threshold, for instance, small changes in the situation produce large
changes in the metric (the weight of the autonomy dimension spikes as
consent becomes marginal). The curvature tensor diverges near the
boundary --- a moral analogue of the gravitational tidal forces near a
black hole's singularity, where curvature invariants diverge.}

\textbf{Source 4: Interaction effects.} When multiple agents are
involved, their interacting claims create curvature through the
off-diagonal metric components. Alice's obligation to Bob curves
differently depending on whether Carol is also present. The three-body
moral problem --- like the three-body gravitational problem --- is
generically not integrable, producing chaotic-seeming dynamics from
simple local rules.

\subsubsection{The Ricci Tensor and Scalar
Curvature}\label{the-ricci-tensor-and-scalar-curvature}

Contracting the Riemann tensor yields summary measures of curvature:

\textbf{The Ricci tensor} \(R_{\mu\nu} = R_{\mu\alpha\nu}^{\alpha}\) is
a symmetric (0,2)-tensor that measures, at each point, how much a small
ball of moral situations converges or diverges as it is
parallel-transported. Positive Ricci curvature along a direction means
that obligations converge: nearby moral paths tend toward agreement.
Negative Ricci curvature means they diverge: nearby paths tend toward
disagreement.

\textbf{The scalar curvature} \(R = g^{\mu\nu}R_{\mu\nu}\) is a single
number at each point that summarizes the overall curvature. Positive
scalar curvature means moral space is ``compressed'' --- there is less
room for variation than in flat space. Negative scalar curvature means
moral space is ``expanded'' --- more room for variation, more directions
of disagreement.

\subsubsection{Curvature and the Moral Metric: An
Equation?}\label{curvature-and-the-moral-metric-an-equation}

In general relativity, Einstein's field equations relate the curvature
of spacetime to the distribution of matter and energy:

\[R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}Rg_{\mu\nu} = 8\pi G\, T_{\mu\nu}\]

Is there an analogous equation for moral space --- a relation between
the curvature of the moral manifold and the ``sources'' of moral
structure (commitments, relationships, stakes)?

\textbf{{[}Speculation/Extension.{]}} This is among the deepest open
questions of the framework. The analogy is suggestive: just as matter
tells spacetime how to curve, moral commitments tell moral space how to
curve. And just as spacetime curvature tells matter how to move, moral
curvature tells obligations how to evolve. Whether this analogy can be
made into an equation --- a ``moral field equation'' relating the Ricci
tensor to a ``moral stress-energy tensor'' --- is a research program,
not a result.

What we can say is the direction of the analogy:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3472}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6354}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Physics}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Moral Geometry}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Mass-energy distribution \(T_{\mu\nu}\) & Distribution of commitments,
relationships, stakes \\
Spacetime metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) & Moral metric (trade-off structure) \\
Einstein tensor \(G_{\mu\nu}\) & Moral curvature tensor \\
Geodesic equation (free particle) & Moral trajectory of an agent with no
external obligations \\
Tidal forces (geodesic deviation) & Divergence of moral assessments for
nearby initial conditions \\
\end{longtable}
}

We do not claim the analogy is exact. We claim it is structural --- and
that the mathematical framework is ready if the analogy proves fruitful.

\subsection{10.7 Geodesics: Paths of Least Moral
Resistance}\label{geodesics-paths-of-least-moral-resistance}

\subsubsection{Definition}\label{definition-4}

A \emph{geodesic} is a curve \(\gamma(t)\) that parallel-transports its
own tangent vector:

\[\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}\dot{\gamma} = 0\]

In coordinates:

\[\frac{d^{2}\gamma^{\mu}}{dt^{2}} + \Gamma_{\nu\rho}^{\mu}\frac{d\gamma^{\nu}}{dt}\frac{d\gamma^{\rho}}{dt} = 0\]

Geodesics are the ``straightest possible'' paths in a curved space ---
the paths of least resistance, the natural trajectories that require no
external force.

\subsubsection{Moral Interpretation}\label{moral-interpretation-2}

A moral geodesic is a trajectory through moral space that \emph{carries
no external moral force} --- no new obligations, no new commitments, no
external constraints. It is the path an agent would follow purely under
the influence of the moral landscape's own curvature: the inertial
trajectory of moral evaluation.

\textbf{What does this mean concretely?} An agent on a moral geodesic:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Maintains their obligations faithfully (parallel-transporting them
  along the curve)
\item
  Makes no new commitments (no external force)
\item
  Follows the natural ``flow'' of the moral landscape (the curvature
  guides the path)
\end{itemize}

This is not the path of a \emph{virtuous} agent (who may resist the
curvature, swim against the moral current) or a \emph{vicious} agent
(who may violate parallel transport by arbitrarily discarding
obligations). It is the path of a morally \emph{inertial} agent --- one
who faithfully carries existing obligations without adding or removing
any.

\textbf{Example.} A society that makes no new moral commitments ---
passes no new laws, enters no new treaties, undergoes no moral reform
--- follows a moral geodesic. The trajectory of its obligations is
determined by the existing curvature (the structure of existing
commitments and relationships). The geodesic may lead somewhere
unappealing --- moral drift, gradual erosion of standards, convergence
on a local minimum. This is the cost of moral inertia.

\subsubsection{Geodesic Deviation}\label{geodesic-deviation}

The \emph{geodesic deviation equation} describes how nearby geodesics
converge or diverge:

\[\frac{D^{2}\xi^{\mu}}{dt^{2}} = R_{\nu\alpha\beta}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\alpha}\xi^{\beta}\]

where \(\xi\) is the separation vector between two nearby geodesics. The
Riemann tensor acts as a ``tidal force,'' pulling nearby moral
trajectories together (positive curvature) or pushing them apart
(negative curvature).

\textbf{Moral interpretation.} Two agents who start with nearly
identical moral commitments (nearby points in \(\mathcal{M}\)) but
follow different geodesics (evolving in slightly different directions)
will:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Converge} in regions of positive curvature: the moral
  landscape funnels them toward agreement. These are regions where the
  structure of commitments and relationships is such that diverse
  starting points lead to similar conclusions.
\item
  \textbf{Diverge} in regions of negative curvature: the moral landscape
  amplifies small initial differences. These are regions where moral
  disagreement grows with experience --- where agents who started
  similarly end up far apart.
\end{itemize}

The existence of regions of both positive and negative moral curvature
explains a familiar phenomenon: why some moral issues generate
convergence over time (societies tend to agree that slavery is wrong ---
a region of strong positive curvature) while others generate increasing
polarization (societies diverge on the ethics of economic redistribution
--- a region of negative curvature).

Chapter 11 shows that computing the exact optimal geodesic is
practically intractable at the resolution required for morally adequate
evaluation, and the associated discrete constrained-path problem
(MORAL-PATHFIND) is NP-hard, which is why agents use A* search with
pre-compiled heuristic functions --- the obligation vectors of §6.2 ---
rather than exhaustive computation.

\subsection{10.8 Gradient Flows and Moral
Improvement}\label{gradient-flows-and-moral-improvement}

\subsubsection{The Gradient Field}\label{the-gradient-field}

If the satisfaction function \(S\mathcal{:M \rightarrow}\mathbb{R}\)
assigns a scalar value to each point, its gradient (raised by the
metric) defines a vector field:

\[(\text{grad}\, S)^{\mu} = g^{\mu\nu}\frac{\partial S}{\partial x^{\nu}}\]

This vector field points, at each point, in the direction of
\emph{steepest increase} of \(S\) --- the direction of maximal moral
improvement.

\subsubsection{The Gradient Flow}\label{the-gradient-flow}

The \emph{gradient flow} of \(S\) is the family of curves \(\gamma(t)\)
satisfying:

\[\frac{d\gamma^{\mu}}{dt} = g^{\mu\nu}\frac{\partial S}{\partial x^{\nu}}|_{\gamma(t)}\]

An agent following the gradient flow always moves in the direction that
increases satisfaction most rapidly. This is the moral analogue of
gradient descent (or ascent) in optimization.

\subsubsection{Virtues and Vices of Gradient
Following}\label{virtues-and-vices-of-gradient-following}

\textbf{Virtues.} The gradient flow is locally optimal: at each point,
it makes the maximum possible improvement. An agent following the
gradient is never lazy (choosing a suboptimal direction) or perverse
(moving away from improvement).

\textbf{Vices.} The gradient flow is \emph{myopic} --- it sees only the
local landscape, not the global structure. This leads to familiar
pathologies:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Local maxima.} The gradient may lead to a local maximum that
  is not a global maximum. The agent is ``stuck'' in a morally
  acceptable but non-optimal configuration, unable to improve locally
  but far from the best achievable state. This is the geometric
  representation of moral complacency.
\item
  \textbf{Saddle points.} The gradient may vanish at a saddle point ---
  a point that is a maximum in some directions and a minimum in others.
  The agent is paralyzed: no direction offers clear improvement, despite
  the existence of better states nearby. This is the geometric
  representation of a moral impasse where deliberation stalls.
\item
  \textbf{Sensitivity to the metric.} The gradient direction depends on
  the metric: \(\text{grad}\, S = g^{- 1}dS\). Different metrics
  (different ethical theories) yield different gradient directions for
  the \emph{same} satisfaction function. Two agents in the same
  situation, following their respective gradient flows, may move in
  different directions --- not because they disagree about the value of
  \(S\), but because they disagree about the metric that defines
  ``direction.''
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Virtue as Non-Gradient
Agency}\label{virtue-as-non-gradient-agency}

A \emph{virtuous} agent does not simply follow the gradient. Virtue
involves:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Foresight}: seeing beyond the local landscape to avoid local
  maxima and saddle points. This requires information beyond
  \(\nabla S\) at the current point --- information about the global
  structure of \(\mathcal{M}\).
\item
