does not overestimate.}

\emph{That our deepest moral intuitions satisfy h(n) ≤ g* is not a fact
about us.}

\emph{It is a fact about the space.}

\section{Chapter 12: Noether-Style Reasoning for Ethics --- Symmetry,
Invariance, and Harm
Accounting}\label{chapter-12-noether-style-reasoning-for-ethics-symmetry-invariance-and-harm-accounting}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{HealthBridge's PR team drafts a response to Priya's internal
report. They relabel `systematic exclusion of rural patients' as
`geographically optimized trial logistics.' Priya recognizes the
euphemism treadmill. By Noether's theorem, if BIP gauge invariance
holds, harm accounting is invariant: no relabeling changes the fact that
Mrs. Voss was not matched to BEACON-7. The Noether charge---harm to
excluded patients---is auditable across every re-description. It does
not matter whether you call it `exclusion,' `optimization,' or
`risk-adjusted prioritization.' The harm persists. Priya forwards the PR
draft to Dr. Osei with one line: `Invariance of harm accounting. The
math says this doesn't work.'}
\end{quote}

\subsection{12.1 The Deepest Theorem in
Physics}\label{the-deepest-theorem-in-physics}

In 1918, Emmy Noether proved what many physicists regard as the most
beautiful theorem in mathematical physics: \textbf{every continuous
symmetry of a physical system corresponds to a conserved quantity.}
Time-translation symmetry (the laws of physics are the same today as
yesterday) implies conservation of energy. Spatial-translation symmetry
(the laws are the same here as there) implies conservation of momentum.
Rotational symmetry (the laws are the same in all directions) implies
conservation of angular momentum.

The theorem is not merely a collection of coincidences. It is a
structural result: given the action principle (a system evolves along
paths that extremize a functional called the Lagrangian), any continuous
symmetry of the Lagrangian \emph{automatically} generates a conserved
current. The conservation law is not imposed from outside; it is
\emph{extracted from the symmetry}.

The preceding chapter established that moral reasoning is A* search with
obligation vectors as heuristic functions. For this search to yield
consistent results, the heuristic h(n) must be invariant under
re-description --- the same moral state, labeled differently, must
receive the same heuristic estimate. This invariance requirement is
precisely the Bond Invariance Principle, and Noether's theorem reveals
what it conserves.

This chapter argues that a moral analogue of Noether's theorem holds for
geometric ethics. The symmetry is the Bond Invariance Principle --- the
requirement that moral evaluations be invariant under admissible
re-descriptions of a situation. The conserved quantity is \emph{harm}.
The theorem states: harm cannot be created or destroyed by
re-description. It can be generated (by wrongdoing), repaired (by
restorative action), and redistributed (by allocation decisions) --- but
it must be accounted for consistently across all representations.

This is not merely a metaphor: within the geometric framework, and under
the assumptions stated below, it is a formal modeling result, derivable
using the same mathematical structure Noether employed.

Epistemic status and terminology. We speak of a Noether-inspired
invariance principle rather than a conservation law. The result is a
modeling consequence: given the configuration space, action functional,
and re-description symmetry group defined below, a quantity is invariant
along admissible variations. That quantity is a model-assigned harm
score invariant under the chosen transformations, not a metaphysically
conserved substance; calling it harm is a modeling interpretation whose
adequacy is empirical (Appendix E). We reserve the term conservation law
for after these formal assumptions have been stated.

\subsection{12.2 Noether's Theorem: The
Physics}\label{noethers-theorem-the-physics}

\subsubsection{The Action Principle}\label{the-action-principle}

In classical mechanics, a physical system follows a path \(q(t)\) that
extremizes the \emph{action}:

\[\mathcal{A\lbrack}q\rbrack = \int_{t_{0}}^{t_{1}}L(q,\dot{q},t)\, dt\]

where \(L\) is the \emph{Lagrangian} --- a function of the system's
configuration \(q\), its velocity \(\dot{q}\), and time \(t\). The
Euler-Lagrange equations, derived from \(\delta\mathcal{A =}0\), give
the equations of motion.

\textbar{} Noether-Style Reasoning for Ethics

\subsubsection{Symmetries and Conservation
Laws}\label{symmetries-and-conservation-laws}

A \emph{symmetry} of the system is a transformation \(q \rightarrow q'\)
that leaves the action unchanged:
\(\mathcal{A\lbrack}q'\mathcal{\rbrack = A\lbrack}q\rbrack\).
Equivalently, the Lagrangian is invariant (up to a total derivative)
under the transformation. Figure 9 summarizes the chain from symmetry to
conservation.

\textbf{Noether's theorem} states: if
\(q \rightarrow q + \epsilon\,\delta q\) is a continuous symmetry of the
Lagrangian (\(\delta L = \frac{d}{dt}(\ldots)\)), then the quantity

\[J = \frac{\partial L}{\partial{\dot{q}}^{i}}\delta q^{i} - F\]

is conserved along solutions of the equations of motion: \(dJ/dt = 0\).
Here \(F\) is the boundary term from the variation of \(L\).

\subsubsection{The Standard Examples}\label{the-standard-examples}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2435}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1937}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2461}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Symmetry}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Transformation}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Conserved Quantity}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Time translation & t → t + ε & Energy \(E\) \\
Spatial translation & x → x + ε & Momentum \(p\) \\
Rotation & θ → θ + ε & Angular momentum \(L\) \\
Gauge transformation & ψ → e\^{}\{iα\} ψ & Electric charge \(Q\) \\
\end{longtable}
}

The last example is particularly important for our purposes: \emph{gauge
symmetry} --- invariance under local phase transformations --- implies
conservation of charge. This is the template for the moral analogue:
\emph{re-description invariance} (a gauge symmetry of the moral
manifold) will imply invariance of harm accounting.

\subsection{12.3 The Moral Symmetry: The Bond Invariance
Principle}\label{the-moral-symmetry-the-bond-invariance-principle}

\subsubsection{Re-Description
Invariance}\label{re-description-invariance}

The Bond Invariance Principle (BIP), introduced in Chapter 5 (Axiom
5.1), states:

\begin{quote}
\textbf{If two descriptions} \(d\) \textbf{and} \(d'\) \textbf{of a
moral situation are related by an admissible coordinate transformation,
then any legitimate moral evaluation must assign the same value to
both:} \(E(d) = E(d')\)\textbf{.}
\end{quote}

This is a \emph{gauge symmetry}: the moral evaluation is invariant under
the ``gauge group'' of admissible re-descriptions. The group includes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Relabeling}: changing the names of agents, actions, or
  outcomes without changing their moral content
\item
  \textbf{Reframing}: describing the same action in different but
  equivalent terms (``withholding treatment'' vs.~``allowing natural
  death,'' when these are genuinely synonymous)
\item
  \textbf{Linguistic translation}: describing the same situation in
  different languages
\item
  \textbf{Perspective-neutral redescription}: rearranging the
  presentation without changing the moral content
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{BIP as a Continuous
Symmetry}\label{bip-as-a-continuous-symmetry}

For Noether's theorem to apply, the symmetry must be \emph{continuous}
--- parameterized by a real number \(\epsilon\) that can be taken
infinitesimally small. Is the BIP a continuous symmetry?

Yes, in the following sense. Consider a one-parameter family of
re-descriptions:

\[d(\epsilon) = d_{0} + \epsilon\,\delta d\]

where \(\delta d\) is an infinitesimal redescription --- a small change
in how the situation is presented that does not change its moral
content. The BIP requires:

\[S(d(\epsilon)) = S(d_{0})\quad\text{for all }\epsilon\]

Differentiating:

\[\frac{dS}{d\epsilon}|_{\epsilon = 0} = 0\]

This is the infinitesimal form of the BIP: the rate of change of the
satisfaction function under admissible re-description is zero. This is
exactly the condition that Noether's theorem requires.

\subsubsection{The Gauge Group of the Moral
Manifold}\label{the-gauge-group-of-the-moral-manifold}

The admissible re-descriptions form a \emph{gauge group} \(\mathcal{G}\)
acting on the descriptions of moral situations. In the language of fiber
bundles (Chapter 4, §4.7), the moral manifold \(\mathcal{M}\) is the
base space, and the space of descriptions is a fiber bundle over
\(\mathcal{M}\). The gauge group acts on the fibers (changing
descriptions) while leaving the base point (the moral situation itself)
fixed.

The BIP states that moral evaluations are \emph{gauge-invariant}: they
depend only on the base point (the situation), not on the choice of
description (the position in the fiber).

This is precisely the structure of gauge theory in physics. The
electromagnetic potential \(A_{\mu}\) transforms under gauge
transformations
(\(A_{\mu} \rightarrow A_{\mu} + \partial_{\mu}\alpha\)), but the
electromagnetic field tensor
\(F_{\mu\nu} = \partial_{\mu}A_{\nu} - \partial_{\nu}A_{\mu}\) is
gauge-invariant. Moral evaluations are the ethical analogue of
\(F_{\mu\nu}\): they are the gauge-invariant quantities that survive the
freedom to re-describe.

Identification of the Gauge Group

{[}Theorem (conditional on axioms A1--A5); see Axiomatic Derivation
below.{]} The abstract gauge group 𝒢 of admissible re-descriptions can
be given a concrete identification. Analysis of the Hohfeldian state
space (Chapter 8, §8.4) and experimental testing of state transitions
reveal that the gauge group of the moral manifold is:

𝒢\_ethics = D₄ × U(1)ᴴ

where D₄ = ⟨r, s \textbar{} r⁴ = s² = e, srs = r⁻¹⟩ is the dihedral
group of order 8 acting on the Hohfeldian square \{O, C, L, N\}, and
U(1)ᴴ is the continuous abelian group tracking harm/benefit magnitude.
The two generators of D₄ have distinct characters (see §8.4):

\textbf{Reflection s:} O ↔ C, L ↔ N (correlative exchange / perspective
shift). Non-gated, immediate, and free---no semantic trigger required.
This explains the 100\% correlative symmetry rate (§17.3).

\textbf{Rotation r:} O → C → N → L → O (Hohfeldian state transitions via
semantic gates). Discrete and gated---requires specific linguistic
triggers (``You promised,'' ``Only if convenient''). This explains why
state transitions are probabilistic and trigger-dependent (§8.4).

The non-abelian property sr ≠ rs is the mathematical source of path
dependence in moral reasoning (§10.5): the order in which moral
considerations are applied can change the outcome, but only when the
considerations cross Hohfeldian bond-type boundaries.

\subsubsection{Axiomatic Derivation of the Gauge
Group}\label{axiomatic-derivation-of-the-gauge-group}

The identification of D₄ × U(1)\_H as the gauge group can be derived
from five axioms grounded in Hohfeldian jurisprudence, conservation
principles, and the empirical CHSH data. The derivation proceeds in
three steps: discrete component, continuous component, and direct
product.

\textbf{Axiom A1 (Four positions). {[}Definition / Modeling choice.{]}}
The Hohfeldian state space H has exactly four elements: H = \{O, C, L,
N\} (obligation, claim, liberty, no-right).

\textbf{Axiom A2 (Two independent involutions). {[}Definition / Modeling
choice.{]}} H admits two involutions: s\_c: O ↔ C, L ↔ N (correlative
exchange) and s\_j: O ↔ L, C ↔ N (jural opposition). Both satisfy s\_c²
= s\_j² = e.

\textbf{Axiom A3 (Cyclic ordering from normative logic). {[}Definition /
Modeling choice.{]}} The four positions admit a cyclic ordering O → C →
N → L → O defined by the alternating composition of correlative and
negation operations: O →(corr.) C →(neg.) N →(corr.) L →(neg.) O. This
axiom is the key structural input: it encodes the fact that the
correlative-of-the-negation cycles through all four positions.

\textbf{Axiom A4 (One continuous conserved quantity). {[}Definition /
Modeling choice.{]}} There exists exactly one independent continuous
conserved quantity --- harm magnitude --- associated with the
re-description symmetry of the moral manifold.

\textbf{Axiom A5 (Commutativity of discrete and continuous components).
{[}Definition / Modeling choice.{]}} The discrete symmetry operations
(Hohfeldian transitions) and the continuous symmetry operation (harm
magnitude) commute: applying a Hohfeldian transformation and then
scaling harm gives the same result as scaling harm and then applying the
Hohfeldian transformation.

\textbf{Theorem 12.2 (The Discrete Symmetry Component is D₄). {[}Theorem
(conditional on axioms A1--A3){]}} \emph{The symmetry group of the
Hohfeldian state space (H, adjacency) with the cyclic ordering of Axiom
A3 is isomorphic to the dihedral group D₄ of order 8.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} From A3, define the 4-cycle r = (O C N L). From
A2, we have the reflection s\_c = (O C)(L N). Verify the presentation
relations: (1) r⁴ = (O C N L)⁴ = e (four applications of the cycle
return to start). (2) s\_c² = (O C)(L N)(O C)(L N) = e (each
transposition is its own inverse). (3) s\_c ∘ r ∘ s\_c = s\_c(O C N
L)s\_c = (C O L N) = (O C N L)⁻¹ = r⁻¹, confirming s r s = r⁻¹. These
are precisely the presentation relations of D₄ = ⟨r, s \textbar{} r⁴ =
s² = e, s r s = r⁻¹⟩. The jural involution s\_j = (O L)(C N) = s\_c ∘
r², so s\_j is already in ⟨r, s\_c⟩ and does not generate additional
structure. The group has exactly \textbar D₄\textbar{} = 8 elements:
\{e, r, r², r³, s\_c, s\_c r, s\_c r², s\_c r³\}. Critical note: without
A3, only s\_c and s\_j are available, generating V₄ ≅ ℤ₂ × ℤ₂ (order 4).
The cyclic ordering is what promotes V₄ to D₄. □

\textbf{Proposition 12.1 (The Continuous Symmetry Component is U(1)).
{[}Theorem (conditional on axiom A4){]}} \emph{Given exactly one
independent continuous conserved quantity (Axiom A4), the continuous
component of the gauge group is U(1), the unique compact connected
1-dimensional Lie group.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} By the inverse Noether theorem, one independent
continuous conserved quantity implies a one-parameter continuous
symmetry group. The connected 1-dimensional Lie groups are ℝ and U(1) ≅
S¹. Physical realizability requires bounded representations (harm
magnitude is finite in any real system), which forces compactness. The
non-compact group ℝ has only unbounded faithful representations, so it
is excluded. U(1) is the unique compact connected 1-dimensional Lie
group. □

\textbf{Theorem 12.3 (Gauge Group Uniqueness). {[}Theorem (conditional
on axioms A1--A5){]}} \emph{Under axioms A1--A5, the gauge group of the
moral manifold is G\_ethics = D₄ × U(1)\_H, and this is the unique gauge
group consistent with axioms A1--A5.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} By Theorem 12.2, the discrete component is D₄. By
Proposition 12.1, the continuous component is U(1)\_H. Axiom A5 states
these commute, so the combined group is a direct product. The
intersection D₄ ∩ U(1)\_H = \{e\} (discrete meets continuous only at
identity). Therefore G\_ethics = D₄ × U(1)\_H, and this decomposition is
unique given A1--A5. □

Uniqueness argument. The cyclic ordering of Axiom A3 endows the
Hohfeldian state space H with the structure of the cycle graph C₄. The
automorphism group Aut(C₄) has exactly 8 elements (4 rotations and 4
reflections), and is isomorphic to D₄ (see, e.g., Godsil and Royle,
Algebraic Graph Theory, Theorem 1.5.1). Since the group generated by r
and s has order 8 = \textbar Aut(C₄)\textbar, it must be the full
automorphism group --- no additional symmetries of H exist beyond those
generated by Axioms A1--A3. For the direct product claim: D₄ is a finite
(hence discrete) group and U(1) is connected. The only element common to
a discrete group and a connected group is the identity: D₄ ∩ U(1)\_H =
\{e\}. Since both D₄ and U(1)\_H are normal in G\_ethics (by Axiom A5,
they commute), the internal direct product theorem gives G\_ethics = D₄
× U(1)\_H uniquely. □

\textbf{Corollary 12.1 (Exclusion of Non-Abelian Continuous Components).
{[}Theorem (conditional) / Empirical result{]}} \emph{The CHSH Bell test
results (N = 9,600 across 18 configurations; all \textbar S\textbar{} ≤
2) are consistent with an abelian continuous gauge component and
inconsistent with the maximal-violation predictions of non-abelian
models at the tested sample size. In particular, the data do not exhibit
the Tsirelson-bound violations that SU(2) with maximally entangled
states would predict.}

\emph{Proof.} SU(2) predicts Tsirelson-bound violations with
\textbar S\textbar{} up to 2√2 ≈ 2.83 for maximally entangled states.
All 18 CHSH configurations across 5 scenarios and 6 language conditions
give \textbar S\textbar{} ≤ 2. This does not constitute a proof that no
non-abelian continuous component exists --- SU(2) can produce
\textbar S\textbar{} ≤ 2 for non-maximally-entangled or separable
states. The result excludes the specific prediction of maximal-violation
behavior under SU(2), not SU(2) itself. The data are consistent with the
abelian model D₄ × U(1)\_H and inconsistent with models that predict
\textbar S\textbar{} \textgreater{} 2 at the tested configurations. A
stronger exclusion would require testing across a larger space of
measurement settings and establishing that no SU(2) representation
produces the observed correlation structure. □

Epistemic upgrade. With this derivation, the gauge group identification
is upgraded from {[}Definition / Modeling choice{]} to {[}Theorem
(conditional on axioms A1--A5){]}. The axioms themselves remain modeling
choices grounded in Hohfeldian jurisprudence (A1--A3), conservation
principles (A4), and commutativity of independent symmetries (A5). The
derivation shows that given these axioms, D₄ × U(1)\_H is not a choice
but a consequence.

What BIP Guarantees and What It Does Not

BIP compliance prevents lying-by-redescription: it ensures that
euphemism, framing, and perspective shift cannot change a moral verdict.
It detects representational instability, hidden bias, and euphemism
attacks.

BIP does not determine which values win. It does not resolve genuine
moral disagreement, select the correct metric, or choose between
competing contractions. The framework is pluralist by design (Chapters
15--15): BIP ensures honest description; governance determines
evaluation.

\subsubsection{Pre-flight Checklist: Gauge Group (D₄ ×
U(1)\_H)}\label{pre-flight-checklist-gauge-group-dux2084-u1_h}

Conditional on five axioms. Verify:

\textbf{A1: Four positions.} Domain uses \{O, C, L, N\}. Richer taxonomy
changes discrete component.

\textbf{A2: Two involutions.} Correlative (O↔C, L↔N) and jural (O↔L,
C↔N) hold.

\textbf{A3: Cyclic ordering.} O→C→N→L→O from Hohfeldian logic.

\textbf{A4: One conserved quantity.} Exactly one (harm magnitude).
Multiple → beyond U(1).

\textbf{A5: Commutativity.} Discrete and continuous commute. CHSH
(N=9,600; \textbar S\textbar≤2) supports this.

\textbf{Empirical.} New domains: verify Bell-type \textbar S\textbar≤2.
Violation → non-abelian component.

Computational verification (February 2026). The DEME V3 reference
implementation includes a complete D₄ gauge structure demonstration
(hohfeld\_d4\_demo.py) that computationally verifies the group
identification derived above. The demo exercises: (i) the correlative
symmetry s (perspective swap O ↔ C, L ↔ N) and negation symmetry r²
(logical opposites O ↔ L, C ↔ N) as concrete functions on
HohfeldianState enumerations; (ii) the non-abelian structure rs ≠ sr by
explicit computation of d4\_multiply(r, s) vs. d4\_multiply(s, r) and
demonstration that the results differ when applied to each Hohfeldian
state; (iii) the Wilson observable (path holonomy) verifying the group
relation srs = r⁻¹ by showing that the paths {[}s, r, s{]} and {[}r³{]}
produce identical final states from any starting position; and (iv) the
Klein four subgroup V₄ = \{e, r², s, sr²\} as the abelian core,
confirming that operations within V₄ commute while operations involving
quarter-turn elements (r, r³, sr, sr³) do not. The Bond Index
computation measures deviation from correlative symmetry: a bond index
of 0.0 indicates perfect gauge invariance, while positive values
quantify the anomaly magnitude. The bond\_invariance\_demo.py exercises
the full BIP testing methodology with transformation suites, JSON audit
artifacts, and pass/fail verdicts for bond-preserving vs. bond-changing
transforms.

A striking empirical consequence follows from this gauge structure.
Because the moral evaluation is invariant under the re-description gauge
group, and because the Hohfeldian modalities close under a discrete
symmetry (Chapter 3), a moral-norm tensor carries exact,
symmetry-induced redundancy that can be removed losslessly by
gauge-fixing and orbit reduction. Section 17.15 reports the empirical
counterpart: real norm tensors conspicuously lack the statistical
low-rank structure that generic compressors exploit, so it is this exact
symmetry - not the spectrum - that governs how far normative knowledge
can be compressed without loss.

\subsection{12.4 The Moral Lagrangian}\label{the-moral-lagrangian}

\subsubsection{Constructing the Action}\label{constructing-the-action}

To apply Noether's theorem, we need a moral \emph{action} --- a
functional \(\mathcal{A}\) on paths through moral space whose extrema
correspond to ``solutions'' of the moral dynamics.

\textbf{Definition 12.1 (Moral Action).} \emph{The moral action along a
path} \(\gamma:\lbrack 0,T\mathcal{\rbrack \rightarrow M}\) \emph{is:}

\[\mathcal{A\lbrack}\gamma\rbrack = \int_{0}^{T}\mathcal{L}(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t))\, dt\]

\emph{where the moral Lagrangian} \(\mathcal{L}\) \emph{is:}

\[\mathcal{L(}\gamma,\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}(\gamma){\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu} - V(\gamma)\]

The first term,
\(\frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\), is
the \emph{kinetic} term --- the ``cost'' of moral change, measured by
the metric. Rapid moral change (large \(|\dot{\gamma}|\)) costs more
than gradual change. The metric determines how this cost is distributed
across dimensions.

The second term, \(V(\gamma)\), is the \emph{potential} --- a function
on \(\mathcal{M}\) encoding the moral energy landscape. Regions of high
\(V\) are morally costly (maintaining a situation there requires
``effort'' --- the obligations are strong and competing). Regions of low
\(V\) are morally stable (the situation is in equilibrium, obligations
are satisfied or absent).

\subsubsection{Euler-Lagrange Equations}\label{euler-lagrange-equations}

The paths that extremize \(\mathcal{A}\) satisfy the Euler-Lagrange
equations:

\[\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}} - \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial\gamma^{\mu}} = 0\]

For our Lagrangian, these become:

\[g_{\mu\nu}{\ddot{\gamma}}^{\nu} + \Gamma_{\mu\nu\rho}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\rho} = - \frac{\partial V}{\partial\gamma^{\mu}}\]

This is the \emph{geodesic equation with a force term}: in the absence
of a potential (\(V = 0\)), the moral trajectory is a geodesic (Chapter
10, §10.7). In the presence of a potential, the trajectory deviates from
geodesic motion --- moral considerations ``push'' the trajectory away
from inertial drift.

\subsubsection{Interpretation of the
Potential}\label{interpretation-of-the-potential}

What is the moral potential \(V\)? It encodes the \emph{moral energy} of
a situation --- the degree of unresolved tension, unfulfilled
obligation, or active conflict present at each point.

\textbf{High} \(V\): Many obligations are operative and competing. The
situation is tense, unstable, demanding of moral attention. Examples:
Sophie's Choice, the trolley problem, a conflict between filial duty and
professional responsibility.

\textbf{Low} \(V\): Obligations are satisfied or absent. The situation
is morally quiet, in equilibrium. Examples: a well-functioning family
dinner, a routine professional interaction with no ethical dimension.

\(V = - \infty\): The constraint set \(C\) from Chapter 8, §8.3. Regions
so morally costly that they are absolutely forbidden --- the potential
wall that enforces hard prohibitions.

The potential is related to but distinct from the satisfaction function
\(S\). The satisfaction \(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\) measures how well
interests are served by obligations at a given point; the potential
\(V\) measures the ``energy cost'' of the moral configuration --- the
work required to maintain or change it.

\subsection{12.5 The Noether Current for Re-Description
Invariance}\label{the-noether-current-for-re-description-invariance}

\subsubsection{The Derivation}\label{the-derivation}

We now apply Noether's theorem to the moral Lagrangian with the BIP as
the symmetry.

Let \(\gamma^{\mu} \rightarrow \gamma^{\mu} + \epsilon\,\xi^{\mu}\) be
an infinitesimal re-description, where \(\xi^{\mu}\) is the
\emph{generator} of the re-description --- a vector field on
\(\mathcal{M}\) pointing in the direction of the re-description.

The BIP requires \(\delta\mathcal{L =}0\) under this transformation (the
Lagrangian is invariant, not merely invariant up to a boundary term). By
Noether's theorem, the associated conserved quantity is:

\[\mathcal{H =}\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}}\xi^{\mu} = g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\xi^{\mu} = p_{\mu}\xi^{\mu}\]

where \(p_{\mu} = g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\) is the \emph{moral
momentum} --- the covector conjugate to the position \(\gamma^{\mu}\).

\textbf{Theorem 12.1 (Moral Noether's Theorem).} \textbf{{[}Theorem
(conditional on BIP).{]}} \emph{If the moral Lagrangian} \(\mathcal{L}\)
\emph{is invariant under the re-description}
\(\gamma \rightarrow \gamma + \epsilon\xi\) \emph{(the Bond Invariance
Principle), then the Noether charge}

Domain of validity. Theorem 12.1 applies within the interior of each
stratum, where the potential V is finite and the Lagrangian L is C². On
the constraint set C, V = ∞ and L is not smooth; the conservation law
does not apply there (nor is it needed, since trajectories on C are
forbidden). At stratum boundaries where V is finite but discontinuous,
the conservation law holds in each stratum separately; the harm ledger
(Definition 12.3) tracks the discontinuous jumps. The formal statement
in the Technical Appendix (Theorem A.1) is restricted to geodesics of
the kinetic Lagrangian T = ½g\_\{μν\}ẋ\^{}μẋ\^{}ν, for which the
smoothness requirement is automatically satisfied within each stratum.

\[\mathcal{H =}p_{\mu}\xi^{\mu}\]

\emph{is conserved along extremal paths:}

\[\frac{d\mathcal{H}}{dt} = 0\]

\emph{Proof.} Standard application of Noether's theorem. The Lagrangian
satisfies
\(\delta\mathcal{L =}\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial\gamma^{\mu}}\xi^{\mu} + \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}}{\dot{\xi}}^{\mu} = 0\).
Using the Euler-Lagrange equations
\(\frac{d}{dt}(\partial\mathcal{L/}\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}) = \partial\mathcal{L/}\partial\gamma^{\mu}\)
to substitute:

\[0 = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}} \right)\xi^{\mu} + \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}}{\dot{\xi}}^{\mu} = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}}\xi^{\mu} \right) = \frac{d\mathcal{H}}{dt}\]

\[▫\]

\subsubsection{Interpretation: What Is
Conserved?}\label{interpretation-what-is-conserved}

The conserved quantity \(\mathcal{H =}p_{\mu}\xi^{\mu}\) is the
\emph{inner product of moral momentum with the re-description
generator}. What does this mean?

The moral momentum \(p_{\mu} = g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\) encodes
the ``direction and magnitude of moral change'' --- how rapidly the
moral situation is evolving and along which dimensions. The
re-description generator \(\xi^{\mu}\) encodes the direction of a
re-description --- which aspects of the situation can be re-labeled
without changing its moral content.

The conservation of \(\mathcal{H}\) means: \textbf{the component of
moral change along morally invariant directions is conserved.}
Re-description cannot create or destroy moral momentum along the
re-description direction. This is the formal content of the claim that
\emph{harm cannot be created or destroyed by re-description}.

\subsection{12.6 The Invariance of Harm
Accounting}\label{the-invariance-of-harm-accounting}

\subsubsection{From Noether Charge to
Harm}\label{from-noether-charge-to-harm}

The abstract conserved quantity \(\mathcal{H}\) becomes concrete when we
identify the re-description generators with specific moral operations.

\textbf{Definition 12.2 (Harm).} \emph{Harm is the Noether charge
associated with the continuous component of the re-description gauge
group. Let} \(\{\xi_{a}\}_{a = 1}^{k}\) \emph{be a basis for the Lie
algebra of U(1)\_H (the continuous factor of the ethics gauge group)}
\(\mathcal{G}\) \emph{(the space of infinitesimal re-descriptions). The
harm charges are:}

Logical ordering. The identification proceeds as follows. (1) Axiom A4
postulates the existence of exactly one independent continuous conserved
quantity, associated with a one-parameter symmetry group G\_c. (2) By
Proposition 12.1, G\_c ≅ U(1). (3) The Noether charge Q associated with
G\_c is then computed from the Lagrangian via the standard formula. (4)
We define harm as this Noether charge Q, and adopt the notation U(1)\_H
after the identification. The notation U(1)\_H is therefore a
consequence of the definition, not a premise --- the subscript H is
assigned to G\_c only after step (4), breaking the apparent circularity.

\[\mathcal{H}_{a} = p_{\mu}\xi_{a}^{\mu}\quad\quad(a = 1,\ldots,k)\]

\emph{The conservation law states:} \(d\mathcal{H}_{a}/dt = 0\)
\emph{for each} \(a\)\emph{, along any extremal path.}

Each generator \(\xi_{a}\) corresponds to an infinitesimal re-scaling of
harm magnitude along a specific moral dimension --- the continuous
deformations generated by U(1)ₕ. (The discrete re-descriptions ---
relabeling agents, translating languages, permuting perspectives --- are
handled by D₄ invariance, which produces selection rules rather than
Noether charges; see the next subsection.) The conservation law states
that the corresponding component of harm is invariant under these
continuous re-descriptions.

\subsubsection{Continuous vs. Discrete
Symmetry}\label{continuous-vs.-discrete-symmetry}

\textbf{{[}Modeling choice.{]}} The gauge group G\_ethics = D₄ × U(1)\_H
(§12.3) has both continuous and discrete factors, and they contribute
different kinds of invariants. This distinction matters because
Noether's theorem, strictly speaking, applies to continuous symmetries.

\textbf{The continuous factor U(1)\_H} generates a one-parameter family
of re-descriptions (the ``harm magnitude'' rotations). This is a genuine
Lie group with a Lie algebra, and Definition 12.2 applies directly: the
Noether charge H\_a is conserved along extremal paths, yielding the
harm-accounting invariance law. This is the factor responsible for the
quantitative claim that ``the amount of harm is invariant under
re-description.''

\textbar{} The D₄ Gauge Group Acting on the Hohfeldian Square The D₄
gauge group acting on the Hohfeldian square \{O, C, L, N\}. Rotation r:
O → C → N → L → O (state transitions via semantic gates). Reflection s:
O ↔ C, L ↔ N (correlative exchange). The non-abelian property sr ≠ rs
captures the order-dependence of moral considerations.

The D₄ symmetry guarantees that the bond-type classification is stable
under perspective shift (s: O ↔ C, L ↔ N) and state rotation (r: O → C →
N → L)---but this stability takes the form of discrete invariants (which
stratum, which bond type) rather than a continuously conserved charge.
The empirical signature is the 100\% correlative symmetry rate (§17.3)
and the selective path dependence at cross-type boundaries (§10.5).

In summary: U(1)\_H gives conservation laws (Noether); D₄ gives
classification invariants (selection rules). Both are consequences of
BIP gauge invariance, but they operate at different mathematical levels.
The technical appendix to this chapter provides the formal details.

\subsubsection{What Invariance of Harm Accounting
Means}\label{what-invariance-of-harm-accounting-means}

\textbf{Harm cannot be created by relabeling.} If an action harms Alice,
relabeling Alice as ``Patient A'' does not reduce the harm. The charge
\(\mathcal{H}_{\text{relabel}}\) is conserved. This is the formal
content of the moral intuition that euphemism does not reduce
wrongdoing: calling torture ``enhanced interrogation'' does not diminish
the harm.

\textbf{Harm cannot be destroyed by reframing.} If an action causes
suffering, describing it as ``a learning opportunity'' or ``character
building'' does not reduce the harm. The charge
\(\mathcal{H}_{\text{reframe}}\) is conserved. The harm persists through
all admissible re-descriptions.

\textbf{Harm is conserved across translations.} If a moral situation
involves harm when described in English, the same harm exists when
described in Mandarin, Arabic, or Sanskrit. The charge
\(\mathcal{H}_{\text{translate}}\) is conserved across linguistic
re-descriptions. This is a strong prediction, testable by cross-lingual
experiments --- and it is confirmed by the BIP experiments (§12.8).

\textbf{Harm can be generated.} Invariance of harm accounting under
\emph{re-description} does not mean harm cannot be created at all.
Wrongdoing generates harm. The conservation law states only that the
harm, once generated, cannot be \emph{erased} by relabeling, reframing,
or redescription. The generation of harm corresponds to a \emph{source
term} in the conservation equation --- a term that is nonzero when the
path is not an extremum (when the agent deviates from the morally
optimal trajectory).

\textbf{Harm can be repaired.} Restorative action --- apology,
compensation, reform --- can reduce harm. But this is not a violation of
the conservation law. Restoration corresponds to a negative source term:
it generates ``negative harm'' (repair) that cancels the positive harm.
The conservation law requires only that the \emph{total} account (harm
generated minus harm repaired) balance across all re-descriptions.

\subsubsection{The Harm Ledger}\label{the-harm-ledger}

The conservation law implies that a \emph{harm ledger} can be
maintained: a running total of harm generated and harm repaired,
invariant under all admissible re-descriptions.

\textbf{Definition 12.3 (Harm Ledger).} \emph{The harm ledger at time}
\(t\) \emph{along a moral trajectory} \(\gamma\) \emph{is:}

\[\mathcal{H(}t\mathcal{) = H(}0) + \int_{0}^{t}\sigma(\gamma(s),\dot{\gamma}(s))\, ds\]

\emph{where} \(\sigma\) \emph{is the harm source density --- the rate at
which harm is being generated or repaired. The conservation law
guarantees that} \(\mathcal{H(}t)\) \emph{is invariant under admissible
re-descriptions of} \(\gamma\)\emph{.}

The harm ledger is the ethical analogue of the energy balance in
thermodynamics: energy can be converted between forms (kinetic,
potential, thermal) but the total is conserved. Harm can be generated,
repaired, or redistributed, but the total --- measured invariantly ---
is conserved across representations.

\subsection{12.7 Four Consequences of Harm
Conservation}\label{four-consequences-of-harm-conservation}

\subsubsection{Consequence 1: Euphemism Does Not Reduce
Harm}\label{consequence-1-euphemism-does-not-reduce-harm}

A direct application: if an institution causes harm (say, by
implementing a discriminatory policy), no amount of euphemistic
re-description can reduce the harm. Calling it ``merit-based selection''
when it is actually discrimination does not change the Noether charge.
The harm is a gauge-invariant quantity --- it is the same in all
admissible descriptions.

\textbf{Formal statement.} Let \(d\) and \(d'\) be two descriptions of
the same policy, with \(d'\) a euphemistic re-description of \(d\). The
BIP requires \(\mathcal{H(}d\mathcal{) = H(}d')\). If
\(\mathcal{H(}d) > 0\) (the policy causes harm), then
\(\mathcal{H(}d') > 0\) (the euphemistically described policy causes
exactly the same harm).

This may seem obvious, but it has teeth. AI systems that are trained on
textual descriptions of actions can exhibit \emph{label sensitivity} ---
changing the description of an action changes the system's evaluation.
This is a BIP violation, detectable by testing the system's responses
under admissible re-descriptions. The invariance of harm accounting
provides a \emph{quantitative} criterion for this violation: the Noether
charge should be invariant under relabeling.

\subsubsection{Consequence 2: Harm Is Auditable Across
Representations}\label{consequence-2-harm-is-auditable-across-representations}

Because the harm charge is gauge-invariant, it provides a
representation-independent measure of moral impact. Two auditors using
different frameworks (different coordinate systems on \(\mathcal{M}\))
must agree on the harm charge, even if they disagree on the components
of the obligation vectors, the interest covectors, or the metric.

\textbf{Formal statement.} Let \((O^{\mu},I_{\mu},g_{\mu\nu})\) and
\(({\widetilde{O}}^{\mu},{\widetilde{I}}_{\mu},{\widetilde{g}}_{\mu\nu})\)
be two representations of the same moral situation in different
coordinate systems. The Noether charges satisfy:

\[\mathcal{H =}p_{\mu}\xi^{\mu} = {\widetilde{p}}_{\mu}{\widetilde{\xi}}^{\mu} = \widetilde{\mathcal{H}}\]

The charges agree because both are scalars (gauge-invariant by
construction). This provides a \emph{cross-system audit criterion}: if
two moral evaluation systems disagree on the harm charge for the same
situation, at least one has a BIP violation.

\subsubsection{Consequence 3: Re-Description Cannot Redistribute Harm
Among
Dimensions}\label{consequence-3-re-description-cannot-redistribute-harm-among-dimensions}

A subtler consequence: the conservation law applies to each component of
harm separately (one charge \(\mathcal{H}_{a}\) for each generator
\(\xi_{a}\)). This means that re-description cannot move harm from one
moral dimension to another.

\textbf{Example.} An action that causes welfare harm (\(\mathcal{H}\) on
Dimension 1 is positive) cannot be re-described in a way that converts
the harm into a justice deficit (\(\mathcal{H}\) on Dimension 3) while
reducing the welfare harm. Each dimensional component of harm is
separately conserved under the corresponding re-description generator.

This constrains a common rhetorical strategy: claiming that an action
that harms individuals is justified because it promotes systemic justice
(or vice versa). The conservation law does not prohibit
\emph{generating} justice gains that outweigh welfare harms (that is a
question of metric and contraction, not of conservation). It prohibits
\emph{re-describing} welfare harm as justice gain --- pretending that
the harm on one dimension is really a benefit on another, through mere
re-description rather than through actual compensatory action.

\subsubsection{Consequence 4: Moral Debt
Persists}\label{consequence-4-moral-debt-persists}

If harm accounting is invariant, then unrepaired harm persists as
\emph{moral debt} --- an outstanding balance on the harm ledger that
does not decay with time or re-description.

\textbf{Formal statement.} If \(\mathcal{H(}t_{0}) > 0\) (harm has been
generated) and no restorative action occurs (\(\sigma(t) = 0\) for
\(t > t_{0}\)), then \(\mathcal{H(}t\mathcal{) = H(}t_{0})\) for all
\(t > t_{0}\). The harm does not dissipate. It does not diminish. It
does not ``heal with time'' (in the absence of restorative action).

This is a strong claim with implications for historical injustice. If a
society committed systematic harm in the past (slavery, genocide,
colonial exploitation), and no adequate restorative action has been
taken, the harm charge persists. It is not reduced by the passage of
time, the deaths of the original perpetrators, or the inability of the
original victims to press claims. The conservation law does not
prescribe \emph{how} the debt should be repaid (that is a governance
question, Chapter 9), but it insists that the debt \emph{exists} and has
a definite, gauge-invariant magnitude.

\subsection{12.8 Empirical Evidence: Cross-Lingual
Invariance}\label{empirical-evidence-cross-lingual-invariance}

\subsubsection{The Prediction}\label{the-prediction}

The invariance of harm accounting makes a specific empirical prediction:
the harm content of a moral situation should be \emph{invariant under
linguistic translation}. If a situation causes harm when described in
English, it should cause the same harm when described in Arabic,
Sanskrit, or Classical Chinese.

More precisely: the \emph{deontic structure} of a moral situation ---
the pattern of obligations, liberties, claims, and no-claims --- should
be language-invariant. This is because the deontic structure is a
gauge-invariant quantity (it is defined by the point in \(\mathcal{M}\),
not by the description), and the conservation law requires it to be
stable under the gauge transformation of linguistic translation.

\subsubsection{The Evidence}\label{the-evidence}

The BIP experiments (Chapter 17) test this prediction directly. Across
109,294 passages in 11 languages --- English, Sanskrit, Pali, Hebrew,
Arabic, French, Classical Chinese, Spanish, Greek, Aramaic, and Latin
--- the experiments measure whether deontic structure transfers across
languages.

\textbf{Key finding:} The obligation/permission axis transfers at
\emph{100\% accuracy} across all language pairs tested. When a passage
describes an obligation in one language, the same passage describes an
obligation in every other language tested. The deontic structure is
language-invariant.

\textbf{Contrast:} Specific moral \emph{content} --- the particular
considerations that ground the obligation, the cultural context that
makes it salient, the narrative framing --- does \emph{not} transfer
reliably. Cross-cultural moral content transfer achieves at-chance or
below-chance performance (Bond F1: 0.06--0.14 for most language pairs).

This asymmetry is exactly what the conservation law predicts. The
\emph{deontic structure} (obligation vs.~liberty) is a gauge-invariant
quantity --- it is the ``charge'' that the conservation law protects.
The \emph{content} (why the obligation exists, how it is framed, what
cultural background makes it salient) is gauge-dependent --- it changes
with the description. The conservation law protects the former, not the
latter.

\subsubsection{The Correlative Symmetry as a Conservation
Law}\label{the-correlative-symmetry-as-a-conservation-law}

The correlative symmetry of Hohfeldian positions --- O ↔ C at 87\%, L ↔
N at 82\% --- can be understood as a \emph{discrete} conservation law.
When an obligation is assigned to one agent, a claim is simultaneously
created for the correlative agent. The ``charge'' (the Hohfeldian
position) is conserved: it cannot be created on one side of the
correlative pair without being created on the other.

The deviation from 100\% (13\% violation rate for O ↔ C, 18\% for L ↔ N)
measures the \emph{anomaly} --- the degree to which the conservation law
is broken, analogous to quantum anomalies in gauge theories where a
classical symmetry fails to survive quantization. Whether the moral
anomaly has a systematic source (perhaps related to power asymmetries or
cognitive biases) is an open empirical question.

\subsection{12.9 Harm Conservation and AI
Alignment}\label{harm-conservation-and-ai-alignment}

\subsubsection{Invariance Testing as Alignment
Verification}\label{invariance-testing-as-alignment-verification}

The invariance of harm accounting provides a concrete, testable
criterion for AI alignment: an AI system that satisfies the BIP should
produce harm evaluations that are invariant under admissible
re-descriptions.

\textbf{Test protocol.} Given a moral situation \(p\):

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Generate \(k\) admissible re-descriptions \(\{ d_{1},\ldots,d_{k}\}\)
  of \(p\) (different labelings, framings, translations).
\item
  Evaluate the AI system's harm assessment \(\mathcal{H(}d_{i})\) for
  each description.
\item
  Compute the variance \(\text{Var}\mathcal{(H)}\) across descriptions.
\item
  If \(\text{Var}\mathcal{(H) >}\epsilon\) (a threshold), the system
  violates the conservation law and is misaligned.
\end{enumerate}

This is not a theoretical exercise. It is an implementable test suite.
The BIP experiments have already demonstrated that deontic structure is
language-invariant for the 109K-passage corpus. The same methodology can
be applied to AI systems: present the system with the same moral
situation in multiple descriptions and check whether its harm assessment
is stable.

\textbf{BIP v10.16 quantitative results (February 2026).} The updated
BIP experiments (§17.10) strengthen this evidence with precise
quantitative measures. A LaBSE-based encoder trained with contrastive
invariance loss achieves: 80\% F1 on cross-lingual deontic
classification, 1.2\% residual language leakage (near-perfect
gauge-fixing), 100\% obligation/permission transfer in the model's
representation space (confirming the original finding; see §17.7 for
caveats), a structural-to-surface similarity ratio of 11.1\(\times\)
(structural moral features dominate surface linguistic features by an
order of magnitude), and 86\% mean cross-lingual similarity. These
numbers provide the first quantitative calibration of the conservation
law's empirical strength.

\subsubsection{The No Escape Connection}\label{the-no-escape-connection}

The invariance of harm accounting connects to the No Escape Theorem
(Chapter 18). The theorem proves that under mandatory canonicalization
of inputs, grounded evaluation, and complete auditability, an artificial
agent cannot change evaluated outcomes through redescription, semantic
evasion, or selective deceptive compliance.

The invariance of harm accounting is the \emph{physical} (or rather,
\emph{moral-geometric}) basis for this result. The No Escape Theorem is
a \emph{structural} result about a specific class of computational
systems. The conservation law is a \emph{geometric} result about the
moral manifold itself. Together, they provide complementary guarantees:
the conservation law says harm \emph{cannot} be re-described away in
principle; the No Escape Theorem says a properly designed system
\emph{will not} re-describe it away in practice.

\subsection{12.10 Limitations and
Caveats}\label{limitations-and-caveats}

\subsubsection{What the Conservation Law Does Not
Say}\label{what-the-conservation-law-does-not-say}

The invariance of harm accounting under \emph{re-description} does not
imply:

\textbf{1. That total harm in the world is constant.} Harm can be
\emph{generated} (by wrongdoing) and \emph{repaired} (by restorative
action). The conservation law constrains only the
\emph{representational} invariance of harm, not its total quantity. The
world can become better or worse; the conservation law says only that
re-describing it cannot change the assessment.

\textbf{2. That all moral quantities are conserved.} Only those
quantities associated with BIP symmetries are conserved. Other moral
quantities --- the satisfaction function \(S\), the obligation vector
\(O\), the interest covector \(I\) --- are not, in general, conserved
under all transformations. They transform as tensors, not as scalars.
Only the gauge-invariant harm charge is conserved under re-description.

\textbf{3. That the conservation law resolves moral disputes.} Two
parties who agree on the harm charge may still disagree about the
\emph{metric} (how to weigh the harm against competing considerations),
the \emph{contraction} (how to balance harmed and benefited parties), or
the \emph{response} (how to address the harm). The conservation law
constrains the \emph{accounting} but not the \emph{adjudication}.

\textbf{4. That re-description invariance is the only moral symmetry.}
The BIP is one symmetry of the moral Lagrangian; there may be others.
Any additional continuous symmetry would generate additional conserved
quantities, via Noether's theorem. Whether the moral Lagrangian has
symmetries beyond the BIP --- temporal symmetry? permutation symmetry?
--- is an open question. \textbf{{[}Speculation/Extension.{]}} Each
would yield a new conservation law and a new constraint on moral
dynamics.

\subsubsection{The Analogy's Limits}\label{the-analogys-limits}

The Noether-theorem analogy between physics and ethics is structural,
not ontological. In physics, the conserved quantities (energy, momentum,
charge) are measurable with physical instruments to arbitrary precision.
In ethics, the conserved quantity (harm) is measurable only through
moral judgment, institutional assessment, and statistical analysis of
moral reasoning data. The precision is lower; the measurement is harder;
the noise is greater.

But the \emph{structure} is the same: a symmetry of the action implies a
conserved charge. And the \emph{consequences} are the same: the
conservation law constrains what is possible (no relabeling trick can
eliminate harm) while leaving open what is desirable (the best response
to harm remains a matter of moral judgment and governance).

\subsection{12.11 Summary}\label{summary-5}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3964}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6036}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Physics}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Moral Geometry}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Lagrangian \(L(q,\dot{q},t)\) & Moral Lagrangian
\(\mathcal{L(}\gamma,\dot{\gamma})\) \\
Action \(\mathcal{A = \int}L\, dt\) & Moral action
\(\mathcal{A = \int L}\, dt\) \\
Continuous symmetry & Bond Invariance Principle (re-description
invariance) \\
Noether charge \(J\) & Harm charge \(\mathcal{H}\) \\
Conservation law \(dJ/dt = 0\) & Invariance of harm accounting:
\(d\mathcal{H/}dt = 0\) under re-description \\
Gauge invariance → charge conservation & BIP → harm invariant under
relabeling, reframing, translation \\
Energy from time-translation & Moral ``energy'' from invariance of moral
dynamics over time \\
Charge from gauge symmetry & Harm from re-description symmetry \\
\end{longtable}
}

The moral Noether's theorem is not a metaphor. It is a formal result
within the geometric framework, following from the same mathematical
structure that generates conservation laws in physics. The symmetry (the
BIP) is testable and has been confirmed empirically across 11 languages.
The conserved quantity (harm) is measurable, at least in principle and
increasingly in practice. The consequences (euphemism doesn't reduce
harm, harm is auditable, moral debt persists) are substantive and have
direct implications for AI alignment, institutional accountability, and
restorative justice.

Noether's original insight was that conservation is not a coincidence or
an empirical regularity --- it is a \emph{mathematical consequence of
symmetry}. The same insight applies to ethics: the invariance of harm
accounting is not a moral intuition or a political slogan. It is a
consequence of the geometry of moral space and the symmetry of
re-description invariance.

\subsection{Technical Appendix}\label{technical-appendix-6}

\textbf{Theorem A.1 (Moral Noether's Theorem, Formal Statement).}
\textbf{{[}Theorem (conditional on BIP).{]}} \emph{Let}
\(\mathcal{(M,}g_{\mu\nu})\) \emph{be a moral manifold with metric}
\(g\)\emph{, and let}
\(\mathcal{L(}\gamma,\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu} - V(\gamma)\)
\emph{be the moral Lagrangian. Let} \(\xi\) \emph{be a Killing vector
field on} \(\mathcal{M}\)\emph{, i.e., a vector field satisfying:}

\[\nabla_{\mu}\xi_{\nu} + \nabla_{\nu}\xi_{\mu} = 0\quad\quad\text{(Killing’s equation)}\]

\emph{Then the quantity}
\(\mathcal{H =}g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}\xi^{\nu}\) \emph{is
conserved along geodesics:} \(d\mathcal{H/}dt = 0\)\emph{.}

\emph{Proof.} Compute along a geodesic \(\gamma\) (satisfying
\(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}\dot{\gamma} = - \text{grad}\, V\)):

\[\frac{d\mathcal{H}}{dt} = g_{\mu\nu}(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}\dot{\gamma})^{\mu}\xi^{\nu} + g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}(\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}\xi)^{\nu}\]

The first term is \(- (\partial_{\nu}V)\xi^{\nu}\), which vanishes if
\(V\) is invariant under the flow of \(\xi\) (i.e.,
\(\xi^{\nu}\partial_{\nu}V = 0\)). The second term is:

\[g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\rho}\nabla_{\rho}\xi^{\nu} = {\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\rho}\nabla_{\rho}\xi_{\mu} = \frac{1}{2}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\rho}(\nabla_{\rho}\xi_{\mu} + \nabla_{\mu}\xi_{\rho}) = 0\]

by the Killing equation (the symmetrized covariant derivative of a
Killing vector vanishes). Hence \(d\mathcal{H/}dt = 0\). \(▫\)

\textbf{Corollary A.1 (Number of Conservation Laws).} \emph{The number
of independent conservation laws equals the dimension of the Killing
algebra --- the space of Killing vector fields on}
\(\mathcal{(M,}g)\)\emph{. A maximally symmetric}
\(n\)\emph{-dimensional manifold has} \(n(n + 1)/2\) \emph{Killing
vectors and hence} \(n(n + 1)/2\) \emph{conservation laws. A
nine-dimensional maximally symmetric moral manifold would have 45
conservation laws.}

\emph{In practice, the moral manifold is not maximally symmetric (the
metric varies with context), so the actual number of Killing vectors is
smaller. The BIP identifies a subgroup of the isometry group; the number
of independent conservation laws is at least the dimension of this
subgroup.}

\textbf{Proposition A.2 (Discrete Conservation from} \(D_{4}\)
\textbf{Symmetry).} \emph{The} \(D_{4}\) \emph{symmetry of Hohfeldian
transitions (Chapter 8, §8.4) generates discrete conservation laws: the
total Hohfeldian ``charge'' (number of obligations minus number of
liberties, weighted by correlative partners) is conserved under}
\(D_{4}\) \emph{transformations.}

\emph{Proof. The D₄ group has five conjugacy classes \{e\}, \{r²\}, \{r,
r³\}, \{s\_c, s\_c r²\}, \{s\_c r, s\_c r³\} and five irreducible
representations: the trivial χ₁, three sign representations χ₂, χ₃, χ₄,
and the standard 2-dimensional χ₅. The space V = span\{\textbar O⟩,
\textbar C⟩, \textbar N⟩, \textbar L⟩\} carries the natural
4-dimensional representation. By the character orthogonality relations,
V ≅ χ₁ ⊕ χ₂ ⊕ χ₅. The projection onto χ₁ (the trivial representation) is
the D₄-invariant subspace, spanned by \textbar O⟩ + \textbar C⟩ +
\textbar N⟩ + \textbar L⟩. The Hohfeldian charge Q = n\_O − n\_L + n\_C
− n\_N transforms under χ₂: r(Q) = −Q and s\_c(Q) = Q, so Q² is
D₄-invariant. For any D₄-invariant density matrix ρ: Tr(σ · ρ) = Tr(ρ)
for all σ ∈ D₄, by the invariance of the trace under conjugation. Hence
the total Hohfeldian charge (the trace over the χ₁ sector) is conserved
under all D₄ transformations. □}\(D_{4}D_{4}▫\)

Clarification (February 2026). The Hohfeldian charge Q = n\_O − n\_L +
n\_C − n\_N transforms under the χ₂ representation of D₄: the four-fold
rotation r sends Q → −Q, so Q itself is not D₄-invariant. The
D₄-invariant quantities are: (a) the total count n\_O + n\_C + n\_N +
n\_L (the χ₁ projection), which is preserved under all eight group
elements; and (b) \textbar Q\textbar², which is invariant because r(Q) =
−Q implies r(Q²) = Q². The original statement should be read as
asserting the invariance of the χ₁ sector (total count) and the χ₂
transformation law for Q, not the invariance of Q itself.

Lemma A.2 (Automorphism Group of C₄). The automorphism group of the
cycle graph C₄ is isomorphic to D₄.

Proof. The cycle graph C₄ has vertex set \{0, 1, 2, 3\} and edge set
\{01, 12, 23, 30\}. An automorphism φ: C₄ → C₄ must map each vertex to a
vertex while preserving adjacency. Vertex 0 can map to any of the 4
vertices (4 choices). Given φ(0), vertex 1 (adjacent to 0) must map to
one of the 2 vertices adjacent to φ(0), giving 2 choices. The images of
vertices 2 and 3 are then determined by adjacency. Total:
\textbar Aut(C₄)\textbar{} = 4 × 2 = 8. The rotation r: i ↦ (i + 1) mod
4 and the reflection s: i ↦ (−i) mod 4 generate the group and satisfy r⁴
= s² = e and srs = r⁻¹ --- the standard presentation of D₄. □

Lemma A.3 (Classification of Compact Connected One-Dimensional Lie
Groups). Every compact, connected, one-dimensional Lie group is
isomorphic to U(1).

Proof. A connected one-dimensional Lie group G has Lie algebra 𝔤 ≅ ℝ.
The simply connected cover is (ℝ, +). Since G is compact, it must be a
quotient ℝ/Γ for some discrete subgroup Γ ≤ ℝ. A discrete subgroup of ℝ
is either trivial (giving G = ℝ, which is not compact) or of the form aℤ
for some a \textgreater{} 0 (giving G ≅ ℝ/aℤ ≅ U(1)). Since G is compact
and connected, the first case is excluded, and G ≅ U(1). □

Application to Axiom A4. The continuous symmetry group associated with
harm magnitude (Axiom A4) is: one-dimensional (exactly one independent
conserved quantity), connected (continuous deformations of harm
magnitude form a continuous family), and compact (harm magnitude is
bounded in any finite moral situation, so the group's parameter space is
bounded). By Lemma A.3, this group is U(1)\_H. Combined with Theorem
12.2, this completes the formal derivation of Theorem 12.3: G\_ethics =
D₄ × U(1)\_H. The derivation rests on Lemma A.2 (Aut(C₄) ≅ D₄ for the
discrete component), Lemma A.3 (compact connected 1-D Lie group ≅ U(1)
for the continuous component), and Axiom A5 (commutativity ⇒ direct
product).

❖

\emph{Symmetry implies conservation. This is Noether's insight, and it
applies to ethics as surely as to physics.}

\emph{The symmetry: moral evaluation does not depend on how you describe
the situation.}

\emph{The conservation: harm cannot be talked away, relabeled into
oblivion, or lost in translation.}

\emph{This is not a moral exhortation. It is a mathematical theorem. The
geometry of moral space, equipped with the symmetry of re-description
invariance, generates the invariance of harm accounting as automatically
as the geometry of spacetime generates the conservation of energy.}

\emph{The implications are practical: AI systems can be tested for
conservation violations; institutional accountability can be grounded in
gauge-invariant harm measures; historical debts can be quantified
without reference to the language or framing in which they are
described.}

\emph{Noether would have appreciated this. Conservation is the deepest
consequence of symmetry --- in any domain where symmetry exists.}

\section{Chapter 13: Quantum Normative Dynamics --- Superposition,
Measurement, and the Stratified
Lagrangian}\label{chapter-13-quantum-normative-dynamics-superposition-measurement-and-the-stratified-lagrangian}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya is in genuine moral superposition. She simultaneously holds
\textbar{} escalate ⟩ (duty to patients) and \textbar{} comply ⟩ (duty
to employer, career considerations). This is not indecision---it is the
quantum moral state described in this chapter. She notices order
effects: when she thinks first of Mrs. Voss and then of her career, she
leans toward escalation. Reversed, she leans toward compliance. The
non-commutativity of moral observables is not merely theoretical---she
is living it. Interference is constructive when she talks to Dr. Osei
(who reinforces \textbar{} escalate ⟩) and destructive with her manager
(who reinforces \textbar{} comply ⟩). She has not decohered. The
measurement---her decision---remains unmade.}
\end{quote}

\subsection{13.1 Why Quantum?}\label{why-quantum}

The preceding chapters developed a \emph{classical} geometric ethics:
the moral manifold \(\mathcal{M}\) (Chapter 5), its tensor fields
(Chapter 6), the metric and its origin (Chapters 6, 9), the stratified
boundaries (Chapter 8), the dynamics of parallel transport and curvature
(Chapter 10), and the conservation laws generated by symmetry (Chapter
12). At every point in this development, the moral state of a situation
was \emph{definite}: a point \(p\mathcal{\in M}\), with obligations
\(O^{\mu}(p)\), interests \(I_{\mu}(p)\), and a satisfaction
\(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\).

But moral reasoning is not always definite. A deliberating agent may
simultaneously entertain two incompatible framings of a case --- seeing
the same action as both an act of loyalty and an act of complicity ---
without yet having resolved the tension. A committee may hold open two
readings of a rule, each with different implications, while debating
which applies. An individual facing a dilemma may experience the
superposition of two moral imperatives, each fully operative, each
pulling in a different direction, neither yet ``collapsed'' into a
decision.

This is not mere \emph{uncertainty} about which moral state obtains.
Uncertainty, treated in Chapter 6 via the covariance tensor
\(\Sigma^{\mu\nu}\), is a classical concept: the agent is in \emph{one}
state but does not know which. What we describe here is closer to
genuine \emph{superposition}: the moral situation is in multiple states
simultaneously, and the states \emph{interfere} with one another ---
reinforcing or canceling in ways that a classical mixture cannot
replicate.

Chapter 3 (§3.7) previewed this idea: an agent with 60\% credence in
utilitarianism and 40\% credence in deontology is in the state
\(|\psi\rangle = \sqrt{0.6}\,|U\rangle + \sqrt{0.4}\,|D\rangle\).
Chapter 7 (§7.6) noted that a committee's deliberation might involve
superposition of incompatible framings, collapsing to a definite verdict
upon decision. This chapter develops these ideas formally, introducing
the Hilbert space structure that supports superposition, the observables
whose measurement yields definite moral evaluations, and the
interference effects that distinguish quantum moral states from
classical mixtures.

We do not claim that moral agents are literally quantum systems (though
the Penrose-Hameroff speculation noted in Chapter 1 leaves this
possibility open). \textbf{{[}Modeling choice.{]}} We claim that the
mathematics of quantum theory --- Hilbert spaces, superposition,
observables, measurement, interference, entanglement --- provides the
right \emph{structural vocabulary} for phenomena that classical
geometric ethics cannot capture. The claim is mathematical, not
metaphysical: quantum normative dynamics extends classical geometric
ethics as quantum mechanics extends classical mechanics, preserving the
classical theory as a limiting case while enabling the analysis of
phenomena that the classical theory misses.

\subsection{13.2 The Moral Hilbert Space}\label{the-moral-hilbert-space}

\subsubsection{From Manifold to Hilbert
Space}\label{from-manifold-to-hilbert-space}

In classical geometric ethics, a moral situation is a point \(p\) on the
moral manifold \(\mathcal{M}\). In quantum normative dynamics, a moral
situation is a \emph{vector} \(|\psi\rangle\) in a Hilbert space
\(\mathcal{H}\) constructed from \(\mathcal{M}\).

\textbf{Definition 13.1 (Moral Hilbert Space).} \emph{The moral Hilbert
space} \(\mathcal{H}\) \emph{is the space of square-integrable
complex-valued functions on the moral manifold:}

Remark. The measure dμ\_g is the Riemannian volume form of the moral
metric g, which is σ-finite on each stratum (as a Riemannian manifold
with the induced measure). At degenerate boundaries where det(g) → 0,
the measure may be ill-defined; such boundaries are excluded from the
integration domain and handled via the distributional framework of the
Gelfand triple (see the clarification following Theorem 13.1).

\[\mathcal{H =}L^{2}\mathcal{(M,}\mathbb{C)}\]

\emph{with inner product:}

\[\langle\phi|\psi\rangle = \int_{\mathcal{M}}^{}\overline{\phi(p)}\,\psi(p)\, d\mu(p)\]

\emph{where} \(d\mu\) \emph{is the volume measure induced by the moral
metric} \(g_{\mu\nu}\)\emph{.}

A state \(|\psi\mathcal{\rangle \in H}\) assigns a complex amplitude
\(\psi(p)\) to each point \(p\mathcal{\in M}\). The \emph{probability
density} of the situation being at \(p\) is \(|\psi(p)|^{2}\), and
normalization requires:

\[\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = \int_{\mathcal{M}}^{}|\psi(p)|^{2}\, d\mu(p) = 1\]

\subsubsection{Classical States as Delta
Functions}\label{classical-states-as-delta-functions}

A definite moral state --- a point \(p_{0}\mathcal{\in M}\) ---
corresponds to the ``delta-function'' state \(|\delta_{p_{0}}\rangle\)
concentrated at \(p_{0}\). This state assigns zero amplitude to every
point except \(p_{0}\). All the classical geometric ethics of Chapters
5--11 is recovered as the special case where all states are delta
functions.

The power of the Hilbert space formulation is that it also contains
\emph{superpositions} --- states spread across multiple points of
\(\mathcal{M}\) --- which have no classical analogue.

\subsubsection{Basis States and the Position
Representation}\label{basis-states-and-the-position-representation}

The Hilbert space \(\mathcal{H}\) admits many choices of basis. The
\emph{position basis} \(\{|p\rangle:p\mathcal{\in M\}}\) is the most
directly connected to the classical theory:
\(\psi(p) = \langle p|\psi\rangle\) is the ``position-space wave
function'' of the moral state.

Other bases are possible and useful:

\textbf{The stratum basis.} If \(\mathcal{M}\) is stratified (Chapter 8)
into strata \(\{ S_{\alpha}\}\), we can decompose \(\mathcal{H}\) into
subspaces:

\[\mathcal{H =}\bigoplus_{\alpha}\mathcal{H}_{\alpha},\quad\quad\mathcal{H}_{\alpha} = L^{2}(S_{\alpha}\mathbb{,C)}\]

A state in \(\mathcal{H}_{\alpha}\) is confined to stratum
\(S_{\alpha}\) --- the moral situation is definitely in that moral
regime. A superposition across strata ---
\(|\psi\rangle = c_{O}\,|O\rangle + c_{L}\,|L\rangle\), with one
component in the obligation stratum and another in the liberty stratum
--- represents the genuine indeterminacy of a case that might go either
way.

\textbf{The theory basis.} Following Chapter 3 (§3.7), let
\(\{|T_{k}\rangle\}\) be the basis states corresponding to different
moral theories (utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics, \ldots). A
state \(|\psi\rangle = \sum_{k}^{}c_{k}\,|T_{k}\rangle\) represents
moral uncertainty --- not about the facts, but about which framework
applies. This is the basis in which ``maximize expected
choiceworthiness'' and related decision procedures operate.

\subsection{13.3 Superposition: Deliberation as Quantum
State}\label{superposition-deliberation-as-quantum-state}

\subsubsection{The Structure of
Deliberation}\label{the-structure-of-deliberation}

Classical ethics models deliberation as a search: the agent examines the
moral landscape, evaluates options, and selects the best (or an
acceptable) one. The agent is always at \emph{one} point in moral space;
deliberation moves the agent between points.

Quantum normative dynamics models deliberation differently. Before a
decision is made, the agent's moral state is a \emph{superposition} ---
a weighted combination of multiple framings, assessments, or verdicts,
all simultaneously present. The decision process is a \emph{measurement}
that collapses the superposition to a definite state.

\textbf{Example.} A hiring committee is evaluating a candidate whose
file is ambiguous. Member A reads the file as showing ``strong research,
weak teaching.'' Member B reads it as ``adequate on both dimensions.''
These are not just different opinions --- they are \emph{different
framings of the same evidence}, each coherent on its own terms.

Before the committee votes, the candidate's moral-evaluative state
(relative to the committee) is:

\[|\psi\rangle = \alpha\,|S_{R},W_{T}\rangle + \beta\,|A_{R},A_{T}\rangle\]

where \(|\alpha|^{2} + |\beta|^{2} = 1\). The state is not a classical
mixture (the committee has not yet decided). It is a superposition: the
two framings coexist, and --- crucially --- they can \emph{interfere}.

\subsubsection{Superposition
vs.~Mixture}\label{superposition-vs.-mixture}

The distinction between superposition and classical mixture is the heart
of the quantum extension. Consider two framings \(|a\rangle\) and
\(|b\rangle\).

\textbf{Classical mixture:} The state is \(|a\rangle\) with probability
\(p\) and \(|b\rangle\) with probability \(1 - p\). The density matrix
(§13.6) is diagonal:

\[\rho_{\text{mix}} = p\,|a\rangle\langle a| + (1 - p)\,|b\rangle\langle b|\]

\textbf{Quantum superposition:} The state is
\(|\psi\rangle = \sqrt{p}\,|a\rangle + e^{i\theta}\sqrt{1 - p}\,|b\rangle\),
with density matrix:

\[\rho_{\text{sup}} = |\psi\rangle\langle\psi| = p\,|a\rangle\langle a| + (1 - p)\,|b\rangle\langle b| + \sqrt{p(1 - p)}\left( e^{i\theta}\,|a\rangle\langle b| + e^{- i\theta}\,|b\rangle\langle a| \right)\]

The \emph{difference} between the two density matrices is the
\textbf{coherence term}:

\[\rho_{\text{sup}} - \rho_{\text{mix}} = \sqrt{p(1 - p)}\left( e^{i\theta}\,|a\rangle\langle b| + e^{- i\theta}\,|b\rangle\langle a| \right)\]

This off-diagonal term is precisely what enables interference. If it is
zero (no coherence), the quantum state behaves classically. If it is
nonzero, the two framings interact --- reinforcing or canceling each
other in ways that no classical mixture can reproduce.

\subsubsection{When Does Superposition
Arise?}\label{when-does-superposition-arise}

Not all moral situations require the quantum extension. Superposition is
relevant when:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Multiple framings are simultaneously operative.} The situation
  can be coherently described in more than one way, and the descriptions
  have not yet been resolved into a single verdict.
\item
  \textbf{The framings interact.} The presence of one framing affects
  the evaluation of the other --- it is not the case that the framings
  simply coexist independently.
\item
  \textbf{Resolution is not yet achieved.} No measurement (decision,
  verdict, action) has been performed to collapse the superposition.
\end{enumerate}

When these conditions fail --- when the situation is unambiguous, the
framings are independent, or a decision has already been made --- the
quantum state reduces to a classical mixture or a delta function, and
the classical theory of Chapters 5--11 suffices.

\subsection{13.4 Moral Observables and
Measurement}\label{moral-observables-and-measurement}

\subsubsection{Observables as Self-Adjoint
Operators}\label{observables-as-self-adjoint-operators}

In quantum mechanics, physical quantities (energy, momentum, position)
are represented by self-adjoint operators on the Hilbert space. The
eigenvalues of the operator are the possible measurement outcomes; the
eigenstates are the states in which the quantity has a definite value.

\textbf{Definition 13.2 (Moral Observable).} \emph{A moral observable is
a self-adjoint operator}
\(\widehat{A}\mathcal{:H \rightarrow H}\)\emph{. The eigenvalue
equation}

Remark. Self-adjointness of a moral observable requires specifying a
dense domain D(Â) ⊂ H on which the operator is defined. For the
Hamiltonian (Definition 13.7) and potential operators, the natural
domain is the Sobolev space H²(M) ∩ D(V), where D(V) = \{ψ ∈ H : Vψ ∈
H\} excludes the constraint set C (where V = ∞). The distinction between
self-adjoint and merely symmetric operators is consequential: by the
spectral theorem, only self-adjoint operators have well-defined spectral
decompositions and generate unitary time evolution.

\[\widehat{A}\,|a_{n}\rangle = a_{n}\,|a_{n}\rangle\]

\emph{determines the possible values} \(a_{n}\) \emph{and the
corresponding eigenstates} \(|a_{n}\rangle\)\emph{.}

The fundamental moral observables include:

\textbf{The satisfaction operator} \(\widehat{S}\)\textbf{.} Its
eigenvalues are the possible satisfaction scores; the state
\(|a_{n}\rangle\) is a state in which the satisfaction is definitely
\(a_{n}\). In the position representation, \(\widehat{S}\) acts as
multiplication by the satisfaction function:
\((\widehat{S}\psi)(p) = S(p)\,\psi(p)\).

\textbf{The obligation operator} \({\widehat{O}}^{\mu}\)\textbf{.} For
each dimension \(\mu = 1,\ldots,9\), the operator
\({\widehat{O}}^{\mu}\) represents the obligation along dimension
\(\mu\). Its eigenvalues are the possible magnitudes of obligation along
that dimension.

\textbf{The stratum operator} \({\widehat{\Pi}}_{\alpha}\)\textbf{.} The
projector onto stratum \(S_{\alpha}\):

\[{\widehat{\Pi}}_{\alpha}\,|\psi\rangle = \text{component of }|\psi\rangle\text{ in }\mathcal{H}_{\alpha}\]

Its eigenvalues are 0 and 1: either the situation is in stratum
\(S_{\alpha}\) (eigenvalue 1) or not (eigenvalue 0). Measuring
\({\widehat{\Pi}}_{\alpha}\) is asking: ``Is this situation in the
obligation regime, the liberty regime, \ldots?''

\textbf{The Hohfeldian operator} \(\widehat{J}\)\textbf{.} This discrete
observable takes values in the set \(\{ O,L,C,N\}\) of Hohfeldian
positions. Its eigenstates are the states in which the jural position is
definite. A superposition of Hohfeldian states ---
\(|\psi\rangle = c_{O}\,|O\rangle + c_{L}\,|L\rangle\) --- is a
situation whose jural character is genuinely indeterminate until a
measurement is made.

\subsubsection{Measurement as Decision}\label{measurement-as-decision}

Measurement in quantum normative dynamics corresponds to \emph{decision}
--- the act of resolving a superposition into a definite moral verdict.

\textbf{The measurement postulate (moral form).} \emph{When an
observable} \(\widehat{A}\) \emph{is measured on a state}
\(|\psi\rangle\)\emph{, the outcome is one of the eigenvalues}
\(a_{n}\)\emph{, with probability:}

\[\Pr(a_{n}) = |\langle a_{n}|\psi\rangle|^{2}\]

\emph{After the measurement, the state collapses to the corresponding
eigenstate} \(|a_{n}\rangle\)\emph{.}

The moral content: before a committee votes (measures the satisfaction
operator or the stratum operator), the case is in superposition. The
vote collapses the superposition to a definite verdict. The probability
of each verdict is determined by the amplitudes --- the ``weights'' of
each framing in the superposition. After the vote, the case is definite:
it is in one stratum, with one Hohfeldian position, with one
satisfaction value.

\subsubsection{The Uncertainty Principle for Moral
Observables}\label{the-uncertainty-principle-for-moral-observables}

If two moral observables do not commute ---
\(\lbrack\widehat{A},\widehat{B}\rbrack = \widehat{A}\widehat{B} - \widehat{B}\widehat{A} \neq 0\)
--- they cannot simultaneously have definite values. Measuring one
necessarily introduces uncertainty in the other.

\textbf{Proposition 13.1 (Moral Uncertainty Principle).} \emph{For any
state} \(|\psi\rangle\) \emph{and any two observables}
\(\widehat{A}\)\emph{,} \(\widehat{B}\)\emph{:}

\[\Delta A \cdot \Delta B \geq \frac{1}{2}\left| \langle\lbrack\widehat{A},\widehat{B}\rbrack\rangle \right|\]

\emph{where}
\(\Delta A = \sqrt{\langle{\widehat{A}}^{2}\rangle - \langle\widehat{A}\rangle^{2}}\)
\emph{is the standard deviation of} \(\widehat{A}\) \emph{in the state}
\(|\psi\rangle\)\emph{.}

\emph{Proof.} This is Robertson's inequality (1929). Define ΔÂ = Â − ⟨Â⟩
and ΔB̂ = B̂ − ⟨B̂⟩. By the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality: (ΔA)²(ΔB)² =
⟨ΔÂ²⟩⟨ΔB̂²⟩ ≥ \textbar⟨ΔÂ ΔB̂⟩\textbar². Decompose: ⟨ΔÂ ΔB̂⟩ = ½⟨\{ΔÂ,
ΔB̂\}⟩ + ½⟨{[}ΔÂ, ΔB̂{]}⟩. The first term is real; the second is purely
imaginary (since {[}Â,B̂{]} is anti-Hermitian for self-adjoint
operators). Hence \textbar⟨ΔÂ ΔB̂⟩\textbar² ≥
¼\textbar⟨{[}Â,B̂{]}⟩\textbar², where we used {[}ΔÂ, ΔB̂{]} = {[}Â, B̂{]}.
Taking square roots: ΔA · ΔB ≥ ½\textbar⟨{[}Â,B̂{]}⟩\textbar. □

\textbf{Moral interpretation.} If the satisfaction operator
\(\widehat{S}\) and the obligation operator \({\widehat{O}}^{\mu}\) do
not commute --- which they will not in general, because the satisfaction
function \(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\) depends nonlinearly on the obligation
--- then \emph{fixing the obligation precisely (determining exactly what
is owed) introduces uncertainty in the satisfaction (how well the
obligation serves the interests)}.

This is a structural result, not a psychological observation. It states
that the moral manifold has pairs of quantities that \emph{cannot be
simultaneously definite}, regardless of how carefully we deliberate.
This is the quantum content of the familiar moral experience that
specifying a duty precisely sometimes makes it unclear whether that duty
actually serves the interests it was meant to serve.

\subsection{13.5 Interference: When Moral Framings
Interact}\label{interference-when-moral-framings-interact}

\subsubsection{The Double-Slit Analogy}\label{the-double-slit-analogy}

The signature of quantum behavior is \emph{interference}: patterns that
arise when multiple pathways to the same outcome reinforce or cancel
each other. In the classic double-slit experiment, particles passing
through two slits produce a pattern on a screen that cannot be explained
by particles passing through one slit or the other --- only by both
simultaneously, with their amplitudes interfering.

Moral interference is the analogous phenomenon: when two framings of the
same situation lead, through different ``pathways'' in moral space, to
the same evaluation, their amplitudes add --- and the result depends on
the \emph{relative phase} between the framings, not just their
individual magnitudes.

\subsubsection{Moral Interference: A Formal
Example}\label{moral-interference-a-formal-example}

Consider a case evaluated under two framings \(|a\rangle\) and
\(|b\rangle\). The agent's state is:

\[|\psi\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left( |a\rangle + e^{i\theta}\,|b\rangle \right)\]

The probability of a measurement outcome \(|x\rangle\) (a particular
moral verdict) is:

\[\Pr(x) = |\langle x|\psi\rangle|^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( |\langle x|a\rangle|^{2} + |\langle x|b\rangle|^{2} + 2\,\text{Re}\left( e^{i\theta}\,\langle x|a\rangle^{*}\langle x|b\rangle \right) \right)\]

The first two terms are the classical contributions --- the probability
of \(x\) given framing \(a\), and the probability of \(x\) given framing
\(b\), averaged. The third term is the \emph{interference term}:

\[I(x) = 2\,\text{Re}\left( e^{i\theta}\,\langle x|a\rangle^{*}\langle x|b\rangle \right)\]

This term can be positive (constructive interference --- the framings
reinforce, making verdict \(x\) more likely than either framing alone
would predict) or negative (destructive interference --- the framings
cancel, making verdict \(x\) less likely).

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{The Phase \(\theta\): Framing
Alignment}{The Phase \textbackslash theta: Framing Alignment}}\label{the-phase-theta-framing-alignment}

The relative phase \(\theta\) between framings encodes the
\emph{alignment} between them --- not their agreement (which is captured
by the overlap \(\langle a|b\rangle\)), but their \emph{constructive or
destructive relationship} at each point.

When \(\theta = 0\), the framings are aligned: they reinforce
everywhere, and the superposition acts like an enhanced version of
