either framing alone. When \(\theta = \pi\), the framings are
anti-aligned: they cancel everywhere, and the superposition suppresses
both framings simultaneously. For intermediate values, the interference
pattern is complex --- some verdicts are enhanced, others suppressed.

\textbf{Moral content.} Two framings of a case may \emph{individually}
point toward the same verdict (both give high amplitude to
\(|x\rangle\)), yet \emph{in superposition} they may suppress that
verdict through destructive interference. This occurs when the framings
are ``out of phase'' --- when their paths through moral space to the
same verdict encode opposite orientations.

\textbf{Example.} Consider ``mercy'' and ``justice'' as two framings of
a sentencing decision. Each, taken alone, might support a moderate
sentence (mercy counsels lenience; justice counsels proportionality;
both point to the middle range). But if the framings are anti-aligned
--- if entertaining both simultaneously reveals a tension that neither
reveals alone --- the interference term may suppress the moderate option
and amplify the extremes. The superposition of mercy and justice does
not simply average them; it creates a new evaluative landscape that
neither framing alone contains.

\subsubsection{Empirical Signatures of Moral
Interference}\label{empirical-signatures-of-moral-interference}

If moral reasoning exhibits genuine quantum-like interference (as
opposed to classical mixing), this makes testable predictions:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Violation of the law of total probability.} For a classical
  mixture of framings \(a\) and \(b\), the probability of verdict \(x\)
  satisfies \(\Pr(x) = p\, Pr(x|a) + (1 - p)\, Pr(x|b)\). If
  interference is present, this equality fails --- the actual
  probability of \(x\) deviates systematically from the classical
  prediction.
\item
  \textbf{Order effects.} If an agent considers framing \(a\) before
  framing \(b\), the verdict may differ from considering \(b\) before
  \(a\). This corresponds to non-commutativity of the projection
  operators:
  \({\widehat{\Pi}}_{a}{\widehat{\Pi}}_{b} \neq {\widehat{\Pi}}_{b}{\widehat{\Pi}}_{a}\).
\item
  \textbf{Conjunction/disjunction errors.} The probability of a
  conjunction of moral assessments may exceed the probability of either
  conjunct --- a ``conjunction fallacy'' that is irrational in classical
  probability but natural in quantum probability.
\end{enumerate}

These predictions are consistent with decades of experimental findings
in cognitive psychology, collected under the label \emph{quantum
cognition} (Busemeyer and Bruza, 2012). The application to specifically
moral reasoning is an empirical program outlined in Chapter 16.

\textbf{Empirical confirmation (February 2026). Two independent
experiments now confirm prediction 2 (order effects) in moral
evaluation. A commutator matrix experiment (N=16,798 responses, 8 moral
axes × 6 scenarios) found significant non-commutativity in 10+ framework
pairs, with effect sizes of 15--29 percentage points (e.g., {[}Fairness,
Harm{]} = −28.5 pp, t=9.6). An AITA ordering experiment (N=150 posts)
found a 29.3\% order-effect rate (z=13.67, p\textless10⁻²²), with
contested cases (NAH/ESH) showing 2.07× higher susceptibility than clear
cases.}\(6.3\sigma p < 10^{- 21}{\widehat{\Pi}}_{a}{\widehat{\Pi}}_{b} \neq {\widehat{\Pi}}_{b}{\widehat{\Pi}}_{a}\)

\subsection{13.6 The Density Matrix: Mixed Moral
States}\label{the-density-matrix-mixed-moral-states}

\subsubsection{Pure States and Mixed
States}\label{pure-states-and-mixed-states}

A \emph{pure state} \(|\psi\rangle\) represents complete information
about the moral situation --- the full superposition with all its
coherences. But we rarely have complete information. More often, we know
that the situation is one of several states but do not know which, and
the states may themselves be superpositions.

\textbf{Definition 13.3 (Density Matrix).} \emph{A mixed moral state is
represented by a density matrix} \(\rho\) \emph{--- a positive
semi-definite, trace-one operator on} \(\mathcal{H}\)\emph{:}

\[\rho = \sum_{k}^{}p_{k}\,|\psi_{k}\rangle\langle\psi_{k}|,\quad\quad p_{k} \geq 0,\quad\sum_{k}^{}p_{k} = 1\]

A pure state \(|\psi\rangle\) has density matrix
\(\rho = |\psi\rangle\langle\psi|\) (rank one). A classical mixture of
definite moral states \(\{|p_{k}\rangle\}\) with probabilities
\(\{ w_{k}\}\) has a diagonal density matrix (in the position basis). A
general mixed state may combine classical uncertainty \emph{and} quantum
coherence.

\subsubsection{Decoherence: From Quantum to
Classical}\label{decoherence-from-quantum-to-classical}

In physical quantum mechanics, interaction with the environment destroys
coherence --- the off-diagonal elements of the density matrix decay to
zero, and the quantum state becomes effectively classical. This process
is \emph{decoherence}.

The moral analogue: as a deliberation proceeds and constraints are
imposed --- evidence is weighed, precedents are cited, rules are applied
--- the coherence between framings is reduced. The superposition of
competing framings gradually becomes a classical mixture, and eventually
a definite verdict.

\textbf{Definition 13.4 (Moral Decoherence).} \emph{A deliberative
process is a decoherence channel} \(\mathcal{E}\) \emph{acting on the
density matrix:}

\[\rho\overset{\mathcal{E}}{\rightarrow}\rho' = \sum_{k}^{}E_{k}\,\rho\, E_{k}^{\dagger}\]

\emph{where} \(\{ E_{k}\}\) \emph{are Kraus operators satisfying}
\(\sum_{k}^{}E_{k}^{\dagger}E_{k} = I\)\emph{. Decoherence suppresses
the off-diagonal elements of} \(\rho\) \emph{without changing the
diagonal elements --- it converts coherent superposition into incoherent
mixture.}

The \emph{rate} of decoherence varies with context. In a highly
structured deliberative process (a court trial, a committee with
Robert's Rules), decoherence is rapid: the rules force the framings to
be evaluated separately and the superposition to collapse quickly. In an
open-ended ethical reflection (a philosophical dialogue, a personal
soul-searching), decoherence is slow: the superposition persists, and
interference effects remain relevant throughout the deliberation.

\subsubsection{The Purity of a Moral
State}\label{the-purity-of-a-moral-state}

The \emph{purity} of a density matrix is:

\[\gamma = \text{Tr}(\rho^{2})\]

For a pure state, \(\gamma = 1\). For a maximally mixed state (uniform
classical mixture over \(n\) states), \(\gamma = 1/n\). Intermediate
values indicate partial coherence.

Purity measures how much ``quantum'' structure remains in the moral
state. High purity means the superposition is coherent --- the framings
are still interfering, and the deliberation retains its full quantum
character. Low purity means decoherence has advanced --- the framings
have been separated, interference effects have washed out, and the
situation is effectively classical.

\subsection{13.7 The Stratified
Lagrangian}\label{the-stratified-lagrangian}

\subsubsection{Combining Lagrangian Dynamics with
Stratification}\label{combining-lagrangian-dynamics-with-stratification}

Chapter 12 developed the moral Lagrangian for smooth regions of the
moral manifold:

\[\mathcal{L(}\gamma,\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{2}\, g_{\mu\nu}(\gamma)\,{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}\,{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu} - V(\gamma)\]

Chapter 8 developed the stratified structure of the moral manifold:
strata joined along boundaries where the rules change. The
\emph{stratified Lagrangian} combines these two structures, providing a
variational principle for moral dynamics that respects the patchwork
structure of moral space.

\subsubsection{The Classical Stratified
Lagrangian}\label{the-classical-stratified-lagrangian}

\textbf{Definition 13.5 (Stratified Lagrangian).} \emph{On a stratified
moral manifold} \(\mathcal{M =}\bigsqcup_{\alpha}S_{\alpha}\)\emph{, the
stratified Lagrangian is:}

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{strat}}(\gamma,\dot{\gamma}) = \left\{ \begin{matrix}
\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}(\gamma,\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{2}\, g_{\mu\nu}^{(\alpha)}\,{\dot{\gamma}}^{\mu}\,{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu} - V_{\alpha}(\gamma) & \text{if }\gamma \in S_{\alpha}
\end{matrix} \right.\ \ \]

\emph{where} \(g_{\mu\nu}^{(\alpha)}\) \emph{is the metric restricted to
stratum} \(S_{\alpha}\)\emph{, and} \(V_{\alpha}\) \emph{is the
potential on that stratum.}

The key feature: the metric \(g^{(\alpha)}\) and the potential
\(V_{\alpha}\) may \emph{differ between strata}. Crossing a stratum
boundary changes the Lagrangian --- the cost structure of moral change
and the moral energy landscape both shift.

\subsubsection{Boundary Conditions at Stratum
Boundaries}\label{boundary-conditions-at-stratum-boundaries}

When a moral trajectory crosses a stratum boundary, the transition must
satisfy \emph{matching conditions} that ensure physical (moral)
consistency.

\textbf{Definition 13.6 (Junction Conditions).} \emph{At a stratum
boundary}
\(\partial S_{\alpha\beta} = \overline{S_{\alpha}} \cap \overline{S_{\beta}}\)\emph{,
a moral trajectory} \(\gamma\) \emph{must satisfy:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Continuity:} \(\gamma\) is continuous across the boundary ---
  the moral situation does not ``teleport.''
\item
  \textbf{Momentum matching:} The moral momentum
  \(p_{\mu} = g_{\mu\nu}{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\) is matched (up to
  projection) across the boundary:
\end{enumerate}

\[\Pi_{\alpha\beta}\, p_{\mu}^{(\alpha)} = \Pi_{\alpha\beta}\, p_{\mu}^{(\beta)}\]

\emph{where} \(\Pi_{\alpha\beta}\) \emph{is the projection onto the
tangent space of the boundary.}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Energy balance:} The Lagrangian may be discontinuous (the
  potential \(V\) jumps at the boundary), but the total action remains
  well-defined:
\end{enumerate}

\[\mathcal{A\lbrack}\gamma\rbrack = \int_{\gamma \cap S_{\alpha}}^{}\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}\, dt + \int_{\gamma \cap S_{\beta}}^{}\mathcal{L}_{\beta}\, dt + \Phi_{\alpha\beta}\]

\emph{where} \(\Phi_{\alpha\beta}\) \emph{is the boundary cost --- the
``activation energy'' required to cross from stratum} \(S_{\alpha}\)
\emph{to stratum} \(S_{\beta}\)\emph{.}

The boundary cost \(\Phi_{\alpha\beta}\) has moral content: it is the
difficulty of transitioning between moral regimes. The cost of moving
from ``permissible'' to ``obligatory'' (recognizing a new duty) may be
low; the cost of moving from ``obligatory'' to ``forbidden'' (violating
a strong prohibition) is extremely high. These costs are encoded in the
boundary terms of the stratified Lagrangian.

\subsubsection{The Quantum Stratified
Lagrangian}\label{the-quantum-stratified-lagrangian}

The quantum extension replaces paths with wave functions and the
classical Lagrangian with the \emph{Hamiltonian operator} that governs
the time evolution of the moral state.

\textbf{Definition 13.7 (Moral Hamiltonian).} \emph{The moral
Hamiltonian is the self-adjoint operator:}

Convention. Throughout Chapter 13, we work in natural units with
ℏ\_moral = 1. The moral Planck constant ℏ\_moral sets the scale of moral
uncertainty: it determines the minimum width of penumbral zones (via the
moral uncertainty principle, Theorem 13.5) and the rate of quantum moral
evolution. Restoring ℏ\_moral, the Hamiltonian reads H = −(ℏ²\_moral/2)Δ
+ V.

\[\widehat{H} = - \frac{1}{2}\,\nabla_{g}^{2} + \widehat{V}\]

\emph{where} \(\nabla_{g}^{2}\) \emph{is the Laplace-Beltrami operator
on} \(\mathcal{(M,}g)\) \emph{and} \(\widehat{V}\) \emph{is the
potential operator (multiplication by} \(V\)\emph{).}

On a stratified manifold, the Hamiltonian is defined piecewise:

\[\widehat{H} = \left\{ \begin{matrix}
{\widehat{H}}_{\alpha} = - \frac{1}{2}\,\nabla_{g^{(\alpha)}}^{2} + {\widehat{V}}_{\alpha} & \text{on }S_{\alpha}
\end{matrix} \right.\ \ \]

with domain conditions at the stratum boundaries that implement the
junction conditions.

\subsubsection{The Moral Schrödinger
Equation}\label{the-moral-schruxf6dinger-equation}

The time evolution of the moral state is governed by the Schrödinger
equation:

\[i\,\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\,|\psi(t)\rangle = \widehat{H}\,|\psi(t)\rangle\]

This equation describes how a superposition of moral framings evolves
under the influence of the moral potential and the curvature of moral
space. The kinetic term \(- \frac{1}{2}\nabla_{g}^{2}\) produces
\emph{spreading} --- moral states that are initially localized tend to
spread across nearby regions of moral space, exploring possibilities.
The potential term \(\widehat{V}\) produces \emph{localization} ---
moral states tend to concentrate in regions of low potential (morally
stable configurations) and are repelled from regions of high potential
(morally costly configurations).

The interplay between spreading and localization determines the
character of the deliberation:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  In regions of low potential with gentle curvature, the moral state
  spreads freely --- deliberation explores many framings.
\item
  Near a deep potential well (a strongly preferred moral outcome), the
  state localizes --- deliberation converges.
\item
  Near a stratum boundary, the wave function may tunnel --- a framing
  that is classically inaccessible (the potential barrier between strata
  is high) may be reached through quantum tunneling.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Tunneling Across Moral
Barriers}\label{tunneling-across-moral-barriers}

Quantum tunneling is the process by which a quantum state penetrates a
potential barrier that would be classically impassable. In moral terms:

\textbf{Definition 13.8 (Moral Tunneling).} \emph{A moral state}
\(|\psi\rangle\) \emph{tunnels across a stratum boundary if the wave
function has nonzero amplitude on both sides of a potential barrier}
\(V > E\)\emph{, where} \(E\) \emph{is the energy of the state.}

\textbf{Moral interpretation.} A moral agent may find themselves ``on
the other side'' of a moral boundary --- having adopted a position that
seemed inaccessible given their existing commitments --- not through a
classical trajectory (a deliberate, step-by-step moral argument that
crosses the boundary) but through a quantum process (a sudden gestalt
shift, an ``aha'' moment, a reframing that skips the barrier entirely).

This is the geometric content of moral \emph{conversion} --- the
experience of suddenly seeing a situation entirely differently, without
having traversed the intermediate positions. Augustine's conversion in
the garden, Saul's on the road to Damascus, the whistle-blower's sudden
decision to speak --- these are not well-modeled as gradient descents
across a smooth landscape. They are better modeled as tunneling events:
the wave function, trapped on one side of a high barrier, leaks through
to the other side, and a measurement (decision, action) catches it
there.

The tunneling rate through a barrier of height \(V_{0}\) and width \(d\)
is approximately:

\[T \sim \exp\left( - 2d\sqrt{2(V_{0} - E)} \right)\]

Higher barriers (more deeply entrenched moral positions) and wider
barriers (more intermediate positions that must be skipped) yield
exponentially smaller tunneling rates --- consistent with the rarity of
genuine moral conversions.

\subsection{13.8 Entanglement: Correlated Moral
States}\label{entanglement-correlated-moral-states}

\subsubsection{Multiagent Quantum
States}\label{multiagent-quantum-states}

When multiple agents are involved in a moral situation, the total moral
state lives in the \emph{tensor product} of their individual Hilbert
spaces:

\[\mathcal{H}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{H}_{A} \otimes \mathcal{H}_{B} \otimes \cdots\]

A \emph{product state} \(|\psi_{A}\rangle \otimes |\psi_{B}\rangle\)
represents agents whose moral states are independent --- knowing one
agent's moral state tells you nothing about the other's.

\textbf{Definition 13.9 (Moral Entanglement).} \emph{A multiagent state}
\(|\Psi\rangle \in \mathcal{H}_{A} \otimes \mathcal{H}_{B}\) \emph{is
entangled if it cannot be written as a product:}

\[|\Psi\rangle \neq |\psi_{A}\rangle \otimes |\psi_{B}\rangle\]

\emph{for any choice of} \(|\psi_{A}\rangle\) \emph{and}
\(|\psi_{B}\rangle\)\emph{.}

\subsubsection{Moral EPR States}\label{moral-epr-states}

The maximally entangled moral state is:

\[|\Psi^{+}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left( |O\rangle_{A} \otimes |C\rangle_{B} + |L\rangle_{A} \otimes |N\rangle_{B} \right)\]

This state correlates Alice's jural position with Bob's Hohfeldian
correlative: if Alice is measured to be in the obligation state
\(|O\rangle\), Bob is \emph{necessarily} found in the correlative claim
state \(|C\rangle\). If Alice is in the liberty state \(|L\rangle\), Bob
is in the no-claim state \(|N\rangle\).

This is the quantum version of the Hohfeldian correlative structure
(Chapter 8, §8.4). Classically, the correlative constraint is O ↔ C, L ↔
N --- an obligation for one party implies a claim for the other. In the
quantum framework, the correlative constraint is an \emph{entanglement}
constraint: the agents' Hohfeldian states are correlated in a way that
cannot be decomposed into independent states.

The BIP experiments (Chapter 17) found that the correlative symmetry O ↔
C holds at 87\% accuracy across the Dear Abby corpus. The 13\% violation
rate corresponds, in the quantum framework, to imperfect entanglement
--- the moral state is not maximally entangled but partially mixed:

\[\rho_{AB} = 0.87\,|\Psi^{+}\rangle\langle\Psi^{+}| + 0.13\,\rho_{\text{noise}}\]

where \(\rho_{\text{noise}}\) is an uncorrelated noise term. This is a
\emph{mixed entangled state} --- predominantly entangled (the
correlative structure is strong) but with a noise floor (some violations
of the correlative constraint).

\subsubsection{Entanglement and Collective
Obligation}\label{entanglement-and-collective-obligation}

Moral entanglement connects to the problem of collective moral agency
(Chapter 14). When agents are morally entangled --- when their
obligations are correlated in a way that resists decomposition --- the
collective has properties that no individual member possesses alone. The
obligation is genuinely shared, not merely distributed.

A collective obligation that is represented as an entangled state
\(|\Psi\rangle_{AB}\) cannot be ``traced out'' to individual obligations
without losing information. The \emph{partial trace} over agent B:

\[\rho_{A} = \text{Tr}_{B}\left( |\Psi\rangle\langle\Psi| \right)\]

is a mixed state --- agent A's individual moral state is uncertain, even
though the collective state is perfectly definite. This is the quantum
content of the intuition that collective obligations are not reducible
to individual ones: when two agents are morally entangled, neither has a
definite individual obligation, even though their joint obligation is
perfectly clear.

\subsection{13.9 The Classical Limit: When Classical Ethics
Suffices}\label{the-classical-limit-when-classical-ethics-suffices}

\subsubsection{The Correspondence
Principle}\label{the-correspondence-principle}

Quantum mechanics reduces to classical mechanics in the limit where
quantum effects (superposition, interference, entanglement) are
negligible. Analogously, quantum normative dynamics reduces to classical
geometric ethics in the limit where:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The moral state is sharply localized.} If \(|\psi(p)|^{2}\) is
  concentrated near a single point \(p_{0}\mathcal{\in M}\) (the wave
  function is a narrow peak), the quantum dynamics approximates
  classical dynamics at \(p_{0}\). This corresponds to a moral situation
  with a definite, unambiguous assessment --- no competing framings, no
  genuine indeterminacy.
\item
  \textbf{Decoherence is complete.} If all off-diagonal elements of the
  density matrix have decayed to zero, the quantum state is a classical
  mixture, and all interference effects vanish. This corresponds to a
  deliberation that has resolved all framing conflicts and is simply
  uncertain about the facts.
\item
  \textbf{The potential barriers are high.} If the potential barriers
  between strata are much larger than the state's energy, tunneling is
  exponentially suppressed, and transitions between strata occur only
  through classical (deliberate) boundary crossings. This corresponds to
  a moral landscape where the boundaries are firm and well-understood
  --- the agent knows which regime applies.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{When to Use Quantum Normative
Dynamics}\label{when-to-use-quantum-normative-dynamics}

The quantum extension is not needed for most everyday moral reasoning.
Classical geometric ethics handles:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Cases with a single operative framing
\item
  Cases where the uncertainty is purely empirical (about facts, not
  about frameworks)
\item
  Cases where the stratum boundaries are clear and the applicable rules
  are unambiguous
\end{itemize}

The quantum extension is needed for:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Genuine dilemmas} where two moral frameworks yield
  incompatible verdicts and the agent must hold both in superposition
\item
  \textbf{Framing effects} where the order or manner of considering
  options affects the outcome
\item
  \textbf{Moral conversion} where an agent suddenly shifts moral
  orientation without traversing intermediate positions
\item
  \textbf{Collective obligations} where the moral state of a group is
  entangled and resists decomposition into individual states
\item
  \textbf{Anomalous probability patterns} where the probability of a
  moral verdict violates the classical law of total probability
\end{itemize}

The quantum framework \emph{contains} the classical framework as a
special case. Using it when the classical theory suffices produces the
same answers with more apparatus. Using it when the classical theory
fails produces new answers that the classical theory cannot provide.

The quantum extension is the component of this framework most likely to
be revised or abandoned as empirical evidence accumulates. The
order-effect results (§17.10) provide the first direct support, but
interference and entanglement predictions remain untested. Readers
primarily interested in AI applications (Part V) may treat this chapter
as optional background; the DEME architecture (Chapter 19) does not
depend on quantum normative dynamics.

Empirical Update: CHSH Tests and the Classical Gauge Group

\textbf{{[}Empirical result.{]}} Two rounds of Bell tests have now been
conducted. The initial test (N=600 evaluations across four
entangled-agent scenarios) and the expanded v3 Bell test (N=9,000, 18
configurations across 5 maximally entangled scenarios in 6 languages)
both found no violation of the CHSH inequality: all \textbar S\textbar{}
≤ 2. The v3 test used scenarios specifically designed to maximize
entanglement: the Trolley Standoff, Mutual Confession, Sacrifice
Lottery, Symmetric Duel, and Entangled Lie. Cross-lingual conditions
(en-ja, en-ar, ja-ar) showed language-specific variation (Japanese:
S=−1.91; Arabic: S=0.14) but none violated \textbar S\textbar{} ≤ 2. The
Hardy non-locality test met 3 of 4 conditions but not the fourth.
No-signaling conditions were satisfied in all scenarios (p
\textgreater{} 0.05).

\textbf{Interpretation.} The non-abelian structure identified in the
Hohfeldian state space (§8.4; see §12.3 for the revised gauge group) is
classical D₄, not quantum SU(2). The original gauge group proposal
(SU(2)ᵢ × U(1)ᴴ) predicted quantum contextuality with
\textbar S\textbar{} up to 2√2 ≈ 2.83 for appropriate moral states. The
data are consistent with classical correlations only.

This does not invalidate the quantum formalism developed in this
chapter. The mathematical structure of superposition, interference, and
measurement remains a valid modeling tool for moral deliberation under
genuine ambiguity (§13.3--12.5). What the CHSH results establish is that
the gauge group governing Hohfeldian state transitions is the discrete
D₄ (see §12.3), not the continuous SU(2) originally proposed. Quantum
effects, if present in moral cognition, operate at a level different
from the Hohfeldian state transitions tested here.

\subsection{13.10 Worked Example: The Whistleblower's
Dilemma}\label{worked-example-the-whistleblowers-dilemma}

\subsubsection{Setup}\label{setup-3}

Emma, an engineer at a chemical company, discovers that the company has
been concealing data about toxic emissions. She faces the
whistleblower's dilemma: loyalty to her employer vs.~duty to public
safety.

\subsubsection{The Classical Analysis
(Review)}\label{the-classical-analysis-review}

In the classical framework (Chapters 5--7), Emma's situation is a point
\(p\) in \(\mathcal{M}\) with competing obligation vectors:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \(O_{\text{loyalty}} = (0,0.7,0,0,0.5,0,0.4,0,0)\): duty to employer,
  privacy of company data, relational loyalty
\item
  \(O_{\text{safety}} = (0.9,0.6,0.3,0,0,0,0.2,0,0)\): public welfare,
  duty to warn, fairness to affected communities
\end{itemize}

The classical analysis computes the satisfaction function for each
option and evaluates the trade-offs via the metric. It yields a definite
(if difficult) answer.

\subsubsection{The Quantum Analysis}\label{the-quantum-analysis}

But Emma's real experience is not like this. She does not simply weigh
two definite obligations. She is uncertain which \emph{framing} of her
situation is correct:

\textbf{Framing} \(|a\rangle\)\textbf{: Loyal Employee.} The data is
ambiguous. The company may have reasonable explanations. Going public
would betray trust and possibly be wrong. The operative obligation is
confidentiality.

\textbf{Framing} \(|b\rangle\)\textbf{: Public Guardian.} The data is
clear. The company is concealing genuine harm. Going public is a duty,
not a betrayal. The operative obligation is safety.

Before Emma decides, her moral state is a superposition:

\[|\psi\rangle = \alpha\,|a\rangle + \beta\,|b\rangle,\quad\quad|\alpha|^{2} + |\beta|^{2} = 1\]

The framings are not independent --- they \emph{interfere}. Considering
the ``loyal employee'' framing makes the evidence seem more ambiguous
(constructive interference between loyalty and doubt). Considering the
``public guardian'' framing makes the evidence seem more damning
(constructive interference between duty and clarity). The interference
is not symmetric: the relative phase \(\theta\) between the framings
determines whether one reinforces or suppresses the other.

\subsubsection{Time Evolution}\label{time-evolution}

Emma's moral state evolves under the moral Hamiltonian \(\widehat{H}\).
As she gathers more evidence (lowering the potential barrier between the
``loyalty'' and ``safety'' strata), the wave function begins to tunnel
from \(|a\rangle\) toward \(|b\rangle\). The tunneling is initially slow
(the barrier is high --- betraying an employer is a serious moral cost).
But as evidence accumulates, the barrier lowers, the tunneling rate
increases, and \(|\beta|^{2}\) grows.

\subsubsection{The Measurement}\label{the-measurement}

The moment of decision --- when Emma picks up the phone to call the
regulator --- is a measurement. The superposition collapses:

\[|\psi\rangle \rightarrow |b\rangle\quad\text{with probability }|\beta|^{2}\]

or

\[|\psi\rangle \rightarrow |a\rangle\quad\text{with probability }|\alpha|^{2}\]

The measurement is irreversible. Once Emma has spoken (or not), the
superposition is destroyed. She is now definitely a whistleblower or
definitely a loyal employee. The quantum indeterminacy is over;
classical ethics applies from this point forward.

\subsubsection{What the Quantum Analysis
Adds}\label{what-the-quantum-analysis-adds}

The quantum analysis captures three features that the classical analysis
misses:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Interference effects.} Emma's evaluation of the evidence is
  not a simple average of two framings. The framings interact:
  considering loyalty makes the evidence seem weaker (destructive
  interference with safety); considering duty makes it seem stronger
  (constructive interference with safety). The probabilities of her
  eventual decision are not classical averages --- they include
  interference terms that depend on the relative phase.
\item
  \textbf{Tunneling.} Emma's conversion from ``loyal employee'' to
  ``whistleblower'' need not follow a continuous path through moral
  space. She may tunnel --- suddenly seeing the situation entirely
  differently, without having traversed intermediate positions. The
  classical theory requires a continuous trajectory; the quantum theory
  allows discontinuous transitions through potential barriers.
\item
  \textbf{Entanglement with the company.} Emma's moral state is
  entangled with the company's: if she is in the ``loyal employee''
  state, the company is in the ``trusted employer'' state; if she is in
  the ``whistleblower'' state, the company is in the ``concealing
  wrongdoer'' state. The entanglement means that her decision does not
  merely affect her own moral position --- it simultaneously determines
  the company's moral status, in a way that cannot be decomposed into
  independent individual decisions.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{13.11 Summary}\label{summary-6}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3872}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4387}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Classical Geometric Ethics}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Quantum Normative Dynamics}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Moral state: point \(p\mathcal{\in M}\) & Moral state: vector
\(\parallel \psi\mathcal{\rangle \in H}\) \\
Definite obligations \(O^{\mu}(p)\) & Superposition of obligations \\
Classical uncertainty: covariance \(\Sigma^{\mu\nu}\) & Quantum
indeterminacy: density matrix \(\rho\) \\
Trajectory \(\gamma(t)\) through \(\mathcal{M}\) & Wave function
\(\psi(p,t)\) on \(\mathcal{M}\) \\
Euler-Lagrange equations & Schrödinger equation \\
Classical boundary crossing & Quantum tunneling \\
Independent agents & Entangled moral states \\
Definite Hohfeldian position & Superposition of jural states \\
Correlative constraint O ↔ C & Entanglement:
\(\parallel O\rangle_{A} \otimes \parallel C\rangle_{B}\) \\
Decision as evaluation & Decision as measurement (collapse) \\
No interference & Interference between framings \\
\end{longtable}
}

Quantum normative dynamics extends the classical geometric framework
developed in Chapters 5--11 by adding the Hilbert space structure that
supports superposition, interference, and entanglement. The extension is
conservative: classical geometric ethics is recovered as the limit where
quantum effects (coherence, tunneling, entanglement) are negligible.

The extension is motivated by phenomena that classical ethics handles
poorly: the structure of deliberation (simultaneous consideration of
incompatible framings), moral conversion (sudden shifts across high
barriers), collective obligation (irreducibly shared duties), and
framing effects (the manner of considering options affects the verdict).

The key new structures are:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The moral Hilbert space}
  \(\mathcal{H =}L^{2}\mathcal{(M,}\mathbb{C)}\), in which moral states
  are vectors that can be superposed.
\item
  \textbf{Moral observables} --- self-adjoint operators whose
  eigenvalues are possible moral verdicts and whose eigenstates are
  states of definite moral assessment.
\item
  \textbf{The moral Schrödinger equation}
  \(i\partial_{t}|\psi\rangle = \widehat{H}|\psi\rangle\), governing the
  evolution of moral states under the moral Hamiltonian.
\item
  \textbf{The stratified Lagrangian} \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{strat}}\),
  which provides a variational principle for dynamics on the patchwork
  moral manifold.
\item
  \textbf{Moral entanglement}, encoding irreducible correlations between
  agents' moral states.
\end{enumerate}

These structures do not replace moral judgment. They provide a
mathematical vocabulary for analyzing phenomena that classical geometric
ethics can describe but cannot fully explain --- and they make
predictions (interference patterns, tunneling rates, entanglement
correlations) that are, in principle, empirically testable.

\subsection{Technical Appendix}\label{technical-appendix-7}

\textbf{Theorem 13.1 (Decomposition of} \(\mathcal{H}\) \textbf{by
Strata).} \textbf{{[}Theorem (conditional).{]}} \emph{Let}
\(\mathcal{M =}\bigsqcup_{\alpha}S_{\alpha}\) \emph{be a
Whitney-stratified moral manifold. Then the moral Hilbert space
decomposes as:}

\[\mathcal{H =}\bigoplus_{\alpha}\mathcal{H}_{\alpha} \oplus \mathcal{H}_{\partial}\]

\emph{where} \(\mathcal{H}_{\alpha} = L^{2}(S_{\alpha})\) \emph{is the
subspace of states confined to stratum} \(S_{\alpha}\)\emph{, and}
\(\mathcal{H}_{\partial}\) \emph{is the subspace of states supported on
stratum boundaries.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}. By Proposition 8.1, M admits a Whitney
stratification M = ⨆\_α S\_α. Since the strata are disjoint, L²(M, dμ) =
⨁\_α L²(S\_α, dμ\textbar\_\{S\_α\}) where dμ is the Riemannian volume
measure induced by the moral metric g. Set H\_α = L²(S\_α,
dμ\textbar\_\{S\_α\}): these are the subspaces of states confined to
individual strata. The boundary strata ∂S\_α have dimension \textless{}
dim M, so μ(∂S\_α) = 0 in the ambient measure; they contribute trivially
to H via dμ. However, each boundary stratum B carries its own intrinsic
measure dμ\_B (the volume form of the induced metric on B). Define H\_∂
= ⨁\_B L²(B, dμ\_B): these are the ``edge modes'' localized on stratum
boundaries. The total Hilbert space is then H = ⨁\_α H\_α ⊕ H\_∂, where
the direct sum is orthogonal by construction (the strata are disjoint,
and the boundary states are supported on measure-zero sets with respect
to the bulk measure). □}\(\mathcal{M}L^{2}L^{2}\mu\mathcal{H▫}\)

Measure-theoretic clarification. Since H = L²(M, dμ\_g) and the strata
partition M, the decomposition H = ⊕ H\_α (orthogonal direct sum over
open strata) follows from the additivity of the Lebesgue integral. The
boundary strata ∂S\_α have codimension ≥ 1 and hence ambient measure
zero; they do not contribute to H = L²(M, dμ\_g). Boundary states ---
wave functions supported on stratum boundaries --- are distributional
objects belonging to the rigged Hilbert space (Gelfand triple) Φ ⊂ H ⊂
Φ*, not to H itself. The term H\_boundary in the decomposition above
should therefore be understood as living in the dual space Φ*,
representing edge modes that couple adjacent strata. This distinction is
immaterial for the physical predictions (which involve inner products in
H) but is required for mathematical precision.

\textbf{Proposition 13.2 (Conservation of Probability Under Moral
Evolution).} \emph{The moral Schrödinger equation conserves the total
probability:}

\[\frac{d}{dt}\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 0\]

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Since \(\widehat{H}\) is self-adjoint,
\(\frac{d}{dt}\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = \langle\dot{\psi}|\psi\rangle + \langle\psi|\dot{\psi}\rangle = i\langle\widehat{H}\psi|\psi\rangle - i\langle\psi|\widehat{H}\psi\rangle = 0\)
by self-adjointness. \(▫\)

Proof expansion. Write ψ(t) = e\^{}\{−iHt\}ψ(0). Then d/dt⟨ψ\textbar ψ⟩
= ⟨∂\_tψ\textbar ψ⟩ + ⟨ψ\textbar∂\_tψ⟩ = ⟨−iHψ\textbar ψ⟩ +
⟨ψ\textbar−iHψ⟩ = i⟨Hψ\textbar ψ⟩ − i⟨ψ\textbar Hψ⟩. Since H is
self-adjoint, ⟨Hψ\textbar ψ⟩ = ⟨ψ\textbar Hψ⟩, so d/dt⟨ψ\textbar ψ⟩ = 0.
Hence \textbar\textbar ψ(t)\textbar\textbar² =
\textbar\textbar ψ(0)\textbar\textbar² for all t, and the evolution
operator U(t) = e\^{}\{−iHt\} is unitary.

\textbf{Proposition 13.3 (Tunneling Rate Through a Moral Barrier).}
\emph{For a one-dimensional rectangular potential barrier of height}
\(V_{0}\) \emph{and width} \(d\)\emph{, with an incident state of
energy} \(E < V_{0}\)\emph{, the tunneling probability is:}

\[T \approx \frac{16\, E(V_{0} - E)}{V_{0}^{2}}\,\exp\left( - 2d\sqrt{2(V_{0} - E)} \right)\]

\emph{In the moral context,} \(V_{0}\) \emph{represents the moral cost
of occupying the intermediate (boundary) region,} \(E\) \emph{is the
current ``moral energy'' of the state, and} \(d\) \emph{is the ``width''
of the moral barrier in the metric-induced distance on}
\(\mathcal{M}\)\emph{. High barriers and wide barriers yield
exponentially suppressed tunneling --- consistent with the rarity of
sudden moral conversions.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Consider the time-independent Schrödinger
equation −½ψ\textquotesingle\textquotesingle{} + V(x)ψ = Eψ with V(x) =
V₀ for 0 ≤ x ≤ d and V(x) = 0 otherwise. In the barrier region, ψ ∝ e±κx
where κ = √(2(V₀ − E)). Matching ψ and ψ\textquotesingle{} at x = 0 and
x = d yields the transmission coefficient: T = {[}1 + V₀² sinh²(κd) /
(4E(V₀ − E)){]}⁻¹. For κd ≫ 1, sinh(κd) ≈ ½e\^{}\{κd\}, giving T ≈
16E(V₀ − E)/V₀² · e\^{}\{−2κd\} = 16E(V₀ − E)/V₀² · exp(−2d√(2(V₀ −
E))). □

\textbf{Proposition 13.4 (Entanglement Entropy of Correlative States).}
\emph{For the partially entangled Hohfeldian state}

\[\rho_{AB} = (1 - \epsilon)\,|\Psi^{+}\rangle\langle\Psi^{+}| + \epsilon\,\frac{I}{4}\]

\emph{with}
\(|\Psi^{+}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|O,C\rangle + |L,N\rangle)\)
\emph{and noise parameter} \(\epsilon \in \lbrack 0,1\rbrack\)\emph{,
the entanglement entropy of the reduced state}
\(\rho_{A} = \text{Tr}_{B}(\rho_{AB})\) \emph{is:}

\[S(\rho_{A}) = - \text{Tr}(\rho_{A}\ln\rho_{A}) = ln2\quad\text{for all }\epsilon\]

\emph{The reduced state of each agent is maximally uncertain regardless
of the noise level --- the correlative structure ensures that individual
Hohfeldian positions are uniformly indeterminate whenever the collective
state has any entanglement.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Compute the reduced state ρ\_A =
Tr\_B(ρ\_\{AB\}). The maximally mixed component contributes Tr\_B(I/4) =
I\_A/2. For the pure component, expand \textbar Ψ⁺⟩ =
(1/√2)(\textbar O⟩\textbar C⟩ + \textbar L⟩\textbar N⟩); the partial
trace over B yields Tr\_B(\textbar Ψ⁺⟩⟨Ψ⁺\textbar) =
½(\textbar O⟩⟨O\textbar{} + \textbar L⟩⟨L\textbar) = I\_A/2, since
⟨C\textbar C⟩ = ⟨N\textbar N⟩ = 1 and ⟨C\textbar N⟩ = 0. Hence ρ\_A =
(1−ε)(I\_A/2) + ε(I\_A/2) = I\_A/2 for all ε. The entropy is S(ρ\_A) =
−Tr((I\_A/2) ln(I\_A/2)) = −2 · ½ ln(½) = ln 2. □

❖

\emph{The moral world admits superposition. Before a decision is made,
the situation is not in one definite state but in many --- framings
coexisting, interfering, reinforcing and canceling in patterns that no
classical mixture can reproduce.}

\emph{This is not a metaphor. It is a mathematical structure: the
Hilbert space of square-integrable functions on the moral manifold,
equipped with observables, a Hamiltonian, and the Schrödinger equation.
The structure makes predictions --- interference patterns, tunneling
rates, entanglement correlations --- that are testable, at least in
principle, against the empirical record of moral deliberation.}

\emph{The quantum extension does not replace the classical framework. It
contains it. Classical geometric ethics is the limit of quantum
normative dynamics when coherence is lost, barriers are high, and the
moral state is sharply localized. Most everyday moral reasoning operates
in this limit --- and the classical theory of Chapters 5--11 is
adequate.}

\emph{But for the hard cases --- genuine dilemmas, sudden conversions,
collective obligations that resist decomposition, deliberations where
the order of consideration matters --- the quantum framework reveals
structure that the classical theory cannot see. The moral world is
richer than any classical description can capture.}

\emph{Quantum Normative Dynamics is the mathematics of that richness.}

\section{Part IV: Agents and
Collectives}\label{part-iv-agents-and-collectives}

\section{Chapter 14: Collective Moral Agency --- Aggregation, Emergence,
and the Tensors of Shared
Obligation}\label{chapter-14-collective-moral-agency-aggregation-emergence-and-the-tensors-of-shared-obligation}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya cannot fix this alone. HealthBridge's collective agency
tensor involves: Priya (designed the system), Dr. Osei (flagged the
problem), Sarah Chen, VP of Product (controls deployment), the Board
(sets priorities), the FDA (regulates trial access), and the patients
(who have no voice in the process). The structure tensor is nonzero:
HealthBridge's decisions are not the sum of individual choices. The
company has emergent obligations that no single employee carries. Priya
can document the bias, but only Sarah can change deployment. Sarah can
authorize a fix, but only the Board can allocate budget. The
responsibility remainder---for patients already harmed---does not vanish
with organizational restructuring. Mrs. Voss's melanoma did not wait for
the org chart to sort itself out.}
\end{quote}

\subsection{14.1 Beyond Individual
Agents}\label{beyond-individual-agents}

The geometric framework developed in Parts II and III has focused on a
single moral situation evaluated from various perspectives. The
obligation vector \(O^{\mu}\) at a point \(p\mathcal{\in M}\) represents
what is owed; the interest covector \(I_{\mu}\) represents what matters;
the satisfaction \(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\) measures how well duty meets
need. Even the multi-agent evaluation tensor \(E_{\nu a}^{\mu}\) of
Chapter 6 (§6.6) --- which indexes by agent \(a\) --- treats each
agent's moral position as independently specifiable.

But much of moral life involves \emph{collective} agents: corporations,
states, committees, families, professions, religious communities, and
(increasingly) human-AI teams. These collectives pose questions that the
individual-agent framework cannot answer:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Does a corporation have moral obligations distinct from those of its
  employees?
\item
  When a committee decides wrongly, who bears responsibility --- and
  does the sum of individual responsibilities exhaust the collective
  responsibility?
\item
  Can an institution be unjust even if every individual within it acts
  permissibly?
\item
  How do individual moral tensors combine into collective structures ---
  and does the combination introduce \emph{new} moral content not
  present in the components?
\end{itemize}

This chapter develops the geometry of collective moral agency. The
central result is that collective moral tensors have \emph{emergent
components} --- terms that cannot be expressed as sums or products of
individual tensors. The collective is not merely the aggregate of its
members. It is a higher-rank moral entity with its own geometric
structure.

\subsection{14.2 The Aggregation Problem}\label{the-aggregation-problem}

\subsubsection{Why Simple Addition
Fails}\label{why-simple-addition-fails}

The most natural first attempt at collective moral structure is
\emph{summation}: the collective obligation is the sum of individual
obligations.

\[O_{\text{collective}}^{\mu}\overset{?}{=}\sum_{a \in A}^{}O_{a}^{\mu}\]

where \(A\) is the set of members and \(O_{a}^{\mu}\) is member \(a\)'s
obligation.

This fails for at least three reasons.

\textbf{1. Collectives have obligations that no member has.} A
corporation has an obligation to pay its debts. No individual employee
has this obligation in their personal capacity. A nation has an
obligation to provide for its citizens' basic needs. No individual
citizen has this obligation singly. The collective obligation is not a
sum of individual obligations --- it has no individual summands.

\textbf{2. Individual obligations may cancel or reinforce in
non-additive ways.} If Alice has an obligation to keep a secret and Bob
has an obligation to reveal it, the group's obligation is not the zero
vector (cancel) nor the sum of the two vectors (reinforce). It depends
on the \emph{structure} of the group --- who has authority, what the
group's purpose is, what decision procedure is in force. The combination
is mediated by structure, not by addition.

\textbf{3. The dimensionality may change.} An individual agent operates
in the nine-dimensional moral manifold of Chapter 5. A collective agent
may operate in a higher-dimensional space --- the nine ethical
dimensions, plus structural dimensions encoding the collective's
internal organization. The collective obligation vector may have
components along dimensions that have no individual analogue.

\subsubsection{The Tensor Product
Approach}\label{the-tensor-product-approach}

A better starting point is the \emph{tensor product} of individual moral
spaces. If agent \(a\) operates in a moral tangent space
\(T_{a} \cong \mathbb{R}^{9}\), the collective of \(n\) agents operates
in:

\[T_{\text{collective}} = T_{1} \otimes T_{2} \otimes \cdots \otimes T_{n}\]

This space has dimension \(9^{n}\) --- far larger than the \(9n\)
dimensions of the direct sum
\(T_{1} \oplus T_{2} \oplus \cdots \oplus T_{n}\) that simple addition
would use.

\textbf{Why the tensor product?} Because it captures
\emph{correlations}. A state in the direct sum \(T_{1} \oplus T_{2}\)
specifies agent 1's obligation and agent 2's obligation independently
--- \(9 + 9 = 18\) components. A state in the tensor product
\(T_{1} \otimes T_{2}\) specifies the full joint obligation, including
all correlations between the agents' moral positions ---
\(9 \times 9 = 81\) components. The additional \(81 - 18 = 63\)
components encode the \emph{relational structure} of the collective: how
the agents' obligations interact, constrain, and depend on one another.

This is precisely the structure encountered in Chapter 13 (§13.8) in the
quantum context. The tensor product of individual Hilbert spaces
contains entangled states --- joint states that cannot be decomposed
into individual states. The classical analogue is the same: the tensor
product of individual moral spaces contains \emph{collective moral
states} that cannot be decomposed into individual obligations.

\subsection{14.3 The Collective Agency
Tensor}\label{the-collective-agency-tensor}

\subsubsection{Definition}\label{definition-5}

\textbf{Definition 14.1 (Collective Agency Tensor).} \emph{Let}
\(A = \{ 1,\ldots,n\}\) \emph{be a set of agents. The collective agency
tensor is a multilinear map:}

Convention. All agents in A evaluate the same base point p ∈ M; the
collective agency tensor T is defined at p and transforms as a tensor
under coordinate changes on the moral manifold. The agent index a ∈ A is
a label, not a tensor index: T does not transform covariantly or
contravariantly in the agent label. Formally, T is a family of
(1,1)-tensors on T\_pM, one for each coalition subset, parametrized by
the agent set A.

\[\mathcal{C:}T_{1}^{*} \otimes \cdots \otimes T_{n}^{*} \rightarrow T_{\mathcal{M}}\]

\emph{that takes one interest covector from each agent's cotangent space
and produces an obligation vector in the moral tangent space. In
components:}

\[O_{\text{collective}}^{\mu} = \mathcal{C}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}\cdots\nu_{n}}^{\mu}\, I_{\nu_{1}}^{(1)}\, I_{\nu_{2}}^{(2)}\,\cdots\, I_{\nu_{n}}^{(n)}\]

The collective agency tensor \(\mathcal{C}\) is a \((1,n)\)-tensor ---
one upper index (the output obligation dimension) and \(n\) lower
indices (one for each agent's interest dimension). Its rank increases
with the number of agents: a two-agent collective has a rank-3 tensor
(\(9 \times 9 \times 9 = 729\) components), a three-agent collective has
a rank-4 tensor (\(9^{4} = 6561\) components), and so on.

\subsubsection{Decomposition: Individual and Emergent
Components}\label{decomposition-individual-and-emergent-components}

The collective agency tensor can be decomposed into components
attributable to individual agents and a \emph{remainder} that is
genuinely collective.

\textbf{Definition 14.2 (Decomposition of Collective Agency).} \emph{For
a two-agent collective, the agency tensor decomposes as:}

\[\mathcal{C}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}}^{\mu} = \mathcal{C}_{(\nu_{1})}^{\mu}\delta_{\nu_{2}} + \delta_{\nu_{1}}\mathcal{C}_{(\nu_{2})}^{\mu} + \mathcal{E}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}}^{\mu}\]

\emph{where:} - \(\mathcal{C}_{(\nu_{1})}^{\mu}\delta_{\nu_{2}}\)
\emph{is agent 1's individual contribution (independent of agent 2's
interests)} - \(\delta_{\nu_{1}}\mathcal{C}_{(\nu_{2})}^{\mu}\) \emph{is
agent 2's individual contribution} -
\(\mathcal{E}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}}^{\mu}\) \emph{is the emergent component
--- the part of collective agency not attributable to either individual}

The emergent component \(\mathcal{E}\) vanishes if and only if the
collective obligation is the sum of individual obligations. When
\(\mathcal{E \neq}0\), the collective has moral agency that exceeds the
combination of its members.

\subsubsection{Measuring Emergence}\label{measuring-emergence}

The \emph{degree of emergence} of a collective can be quantified:

\[\eta = \frac{\mathcal{\parallel E \parallel}}{\mathcal{\parallel C \parallel}}\]

where the norms are computed using the induced metric on the tensor
spaces. When \(\eta = 0\), the collective is fully reducible to its
members. When \(\eta = 1\), the collective agency is entirely emergent
--- none of it can be attributed to individual members. Intermediate
values indicate partial emergence.

\textbf{Claim.} For most real collectives of interest (corporations,
states, institutions), \(\eta > 0\). The emergent component is nonzero
because the collective's structure --- its decision procedures, role
differentiation, and institutional purpose --- generates obligations
that no individual member bears.

\textbf{Computational implementation (February 2026).} The DEME V3
reference implementation realizes the collective agency tensor as a
rank-5 \texttt{MoralTensor} with coalition indices. The decomposition
into individual and emergent components is computed explicitly, and the
degree of emergence \(\eta\) is a reportable diagnostic. V3 also
implements Shapley value credit assignment for fair allocation of
collective moral responsibility:

\[\phi_{i}(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq N\backslash\{ i\}}^{}\frac{|S|!\,(|N| - |S| - 1)!}{|N|!}\left\lbrack v(S \cup \{ i\}) - v(S) \right\rbrack\]

where \(v\) is the coalition value function derived from the collective
agency tensor. This enables quantitative answers to the question ``how
much of the collective obligation is attributable to agent \(i\)?''---a
question that Chapter 18 identifies as critical for human-AI
responsibility allocation. Nash equilibrium and correlated equilibrium
computations provide multi-agent coordination under norm constraints,
extending the Norm-Constrained Stochastic Game formalism of Chapter 19
to strategic settings.

\textbf{ErisML references}: demo\_game\_theory.py, demo\_shapley.py. The
Nash equilibrium computation is demonstrated in demo\_game\_theory.py,
which implements the StrategicLayer for a Prisoner's Dilemma payoff
matrix --- the canonical example of multi-agent moral coordination where
individually rational choices produce collectively suboptimal outcomes.
The Shapley value computation is demonstrated in demo\_shapley.py, which
applies the CooperativeLayer to an airport runway cost allocation
problem: each airline's fair share of the collective infrastructure cost
is computed as its marginal contribution averaged over all possible
coalition orderings, exactly implementing the formula φᵢ(v) described
above.

\subsection{14.4 The Structure Tensor}\label{the-structure-tensor}

\subsubsection{What Makes a Collective More Than a
Set}\label{what-makes-a-collective-more-than-a-set}

A collective agent is not merely a set of individuals. It has
\emph{structure}: decision procedures, role assignments, authority
hierarchies, persistence conditions, and representational conventions.
This structure mediates the relationship between individual and
collective moral positions.

\textbf{Definition 14.3 (Structure Tensor).} \emph{The structure tensor
of a collective} \(A\) \emph{is a} \((0,2)\)\emph{-tensor}
\(\Sigma_{ab}\) \emph{on the space of members, encoding the structural
relationships between them:}

Clarification. The ``space of members'' on which the structure tensor is
defined is the real vector space ℝ\^{}\textbar A\textbar, where
\textbar A\textbar{} is the cardinality of the agent set A. Each basis
vector e\_a corresponds to one agent a ∈ A. The component S\^{}a\_\{bc\}
measures the degree to which the relationship between agents b and c
structurally influences agent a's moral standing.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \(\Sigma_{ab} > 0\): \emph{agents} \(a\) \emph{and} \(b\) \emph{have
  aligned roles (authority flows in the same direction)}
\item
  \(\Sigma_{ab} < 0\): \emph{agents} \(a\) \emph{and} \(b\) \emph{have
  opposing roles (checks and balances)}
\item
  \(\Sigma_{ab} = 0\): \emph{agents} \(a\) \emph{and} \(b\) \emph{are
  structurally independent}
\end{itemize}

The structure tensor is generally \emph{asymmetric}:
\(\Sigma_{ab} \neq \Sigma_{ba}\) when the relationship between \(a\) and
\(b\) is directional (a supervisor-subordinate relationship, for
instance). The symmetric part
\(\Sigma_{(ab)} = \frac{1}{2}(\Sigma_{ab} + \Sigma_{ba})\) encodes the
\emph{mutual} structural relationship. The antisymmetric part
\(\Sigma_{\lbrack ab\rbrack} = \frac{1}{2}(\Sigma_{ab} - \Sigma_{ba})\)
encodes the \emph{power differential} --- the extent to which the
structural relationship favors one party over the other.

This connects to the power tensor \(P_{\mu\nu}\) introduced in Chapter 6
(§6.9): the antisymmetric part of the structural tensor is the
member-indexed version of the structural inequity tensor.

\subsubsection{How Structure Shapes Collective
Obligation}\label{how-structure-shapes-collective-obligation}

The collective agency tensor is related to the individual agency tensors
\emph{through} the structure tensor:

\[\mathcal{C}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}}^{\mu} = \sum_{a,b}^{}\Sigma_{ab}\, E_{\nu_{a}}^{\mu}(a) \otimes E_{\nu_{b}}^{\mu}(b) + \mathcal{E}_{\nu_{1}\nu_{2}}^{\mu}\]

The first term says: the collective obligation depends on the individual
evaluation tensors \(E_{\nu}^{\mu}(a)\) of each member, weighted and
combined according to the structure tensor \(\Sigma\). The second term
is the emergent remainder --- the part of collective agency that cannot
be expressed even through structure-mediated combination of individual
agencies.

\textbf{Example: A two-person partnership.} Alice and Bob form a law
firm. The structure tensor has \(\Sigma_{AB} = \Sigma_{BA} = 0.5\)
(equal partnership, symmetric). The collective agency tensor is
approximately the average of individual agency tensors, with a small
emergent component arising from the firm's identity as a legal entity
(the firm's obligation to clients exists independently of either
partner's individual obligations).

\textbf{Example: A corporate hierarchy.} A CEO and an employee have
\(\Sigma_{CE} = 0.8\) (the CEO's position strongly shapes the
collective's action) and \(\Sigma_{EC} = 0.2\) (the employee's position
weakly shapes it). The antisymmetric part
\(\Sigma_{\lbrack CE\rbrack} = 0.3\) encodes the power differential. The
collective obligation is dominated by the CEO's evaluation tensor, with
the employee's contribution attenuated by the structural asymmetry.

\subsection{14.5 Emergent Obligations}\label{emergent-obligations}

\subsubsection{Obligations No Member
Bears}\label{obligations-no-member-bears}

The emergent component \(\mathcal{E}\) of the collective agency tensor
represents obligations that exist at the collective level but not at the
individual level. These obligations are not mysterious or metaphysical
--- they arise from the \emph{structure} of the collective, just as the
properties of a molecule arise from the arrangement of its atoms, not
from the atoms individually.

\textbf{Example 1: Corporate debt.} A corporation borrows money. The
obligation to repay belongs to the corporation. No individual employee,
officer, or shareholder has this obligation in their personal capacity
(this is the legal content of limited liability). The obligation is an
emergent component of the corporate agency tensor --- it appears only in
\(\mathcal{E}\), not in any individual evaluation tensor
\(E_{\nu}^{\mu}(a)\).

\textbf{Example 2: National defense.} A nation has an obligation to
defend its citizens. No individual citizen has this obligation singly.
The obligation emerges from the collective structure --- the existence
of a polity with a government, a territory, and a citizenry. A random
collection of individuals in the same territory, without political
structure, would have no collective defense obligation. The obligation
requires the structure tensor \(\Sigma\) to be nonzero in specific ways
(political authority, territorial jurisdiction, citizen-state
relationships).

\textbf{Example 3: Institutional injustice.} A hiring system may be
structurally unjust --- systematically disadvantaging a particular group
--- even if every individual decision-maker acts in good faith according
to their individual obligations. The injustice is an emergent property
of the collective structure: it appears in \(\mathcal{E}\), not in any
individual \(E_{\nu}^{\mu}(a)\). This is the geometric content of the
concept of \emph{structural injustice} (Young 2011): injustice that no
individual perpetrates but that the collective generates through its
structure.

\subsubsection{Formal Criterion for
Emergence}\label{formal-criterion-for-emergence}

\textbf{Proposition 14.1 (Criterion for Emergent Obligation).} \emph{An
obligation} \(O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu}\) \emph{is emergent if and only if
there exists no set of individual obligations}
\(\{ O_{a}^{\mu}\}_{a \in A}\) \emph{such that:}

\[O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu} = \sum_{a \in A}^{}f_{a}(O_{a}^{\mu})\]

\emph{for any set of functions} \(\{ f_{a}\}\)\emph{. Equivalently,}
\(O_{\text{coll}}\) \emph{is emergent if and only if the emergent
component} \(\mathcal{E}_{\nu_{1}\cdots\nu_{n}}^{\mu}\) \emph{has
nonzero contraction with the collective interest:}

\[\mathcal{E}_{\nu_{1}\cdots\nu_{n}}^{\mu}\, I_{\nu_{1}}^{(1)}\,\cdots\, I_{\nu_{n}}^{(n)} \neq 0\]

The criterion is testable: an obligation is emergent when it cannot be
attributed to any individual member, no matter how we weight the
individual contributions. The irreducibility is structural, not merely
epistemic.

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} (⇒) Suppose O\_collᵘ is emergent: no functions
\{f\_a\} satisfy O\_collᵘ = Σ\_a f\_a(O\_aᵘ). By the Decomposition
Theorem (Prop. 14.3), Cᵘ\_\{ν₁⋯ν\_n\} = Σ\_k C\_(k)ᵘ ⊗ ⋯ +
Eᵘ\_\{ν₁⋯ν\_n\}. The collective obligation is O\_collᵘ = Cᵘ\_\{ν₁⋯ν\_n\}
I⁽¹⁾\_\{ν₁\}⋯I⁽ⁿ⁾\_\{ν\_n\}. If Eᵘ\_\{ν₁⋯ν\_n\}
I⁽¹⁾\_\{ν₁\}⋯I⁽ⁿ⁾\_\{ν\_n\} = 0, then O\_collᵘ decomposes into
individual and pairwise terms, which has the form Σ\_a f\_a(O\_aᵘ) ---
contradicting emergence. Hence the contraction is nonzero. (⇐) Suppose
the emergent contraction is nonzero. Then O\_collᵘ contains a component
from E that, by definition of the fully connected term, cannot be
expressed as any function of individual obligations alone. Hence
O\_collᵘ is emergent. □

\textbf{Proof supplement (orthogonality).} The projections P\_k (onto
the k-agent interaction subspace) are orthogonal because the
inclusion-exclusion decomposition partitions tensor components by their
interaction order. Formally, if C = Σ\_k C\_k where C\_k involves
exactly k-agent interactions, then ⟨C\_j, C\_k⟩ = 0 for j ≠ k. This
follows from Möbius inversion on the subset lattice: the decomposition
C\_k = Σ\_\{\textbar S\textbar=k\} μ(S) C\_S (where μ is the Möbius
function) produces orthogonal components because the Möbius function of
a Boolean lattice satisfies the orthogonality relation Σ\_\{S:
\textbar S\textbar=k\} μ(S)μ(S') = 0 for \textbar S'\textbar{} ≠ k.

\subsection{14.6 Distributed
Responsibility}\label{distributed-responsibility}

\subsubsection{The Responsibility
Tensor}\label{the-responsibility-tensor}

When a collective acts --- and especially when it acts wrongly --- the
question of responsibility arises. Who bears the moral weight of the
collective's action?

\textbf{Definition 14.4 (Responsibility Tensor).} \emph{The
responsibility tensor of a collective} \(A\) \emph{for an outcome}
\(\omega\) \emph{is a} \((0,1)\)\emph{-tensor (covector) on the member
space:}

\[R_{a}(\omega) \in \lbrack 0,1\rbrack\]

\emph{assigning to each member} \(a\) \emph{a degree of responsibility
for} \(\omega\)\emph{. The responsibility tensor is a moral covector: it
weights the members, just as the interest covector weights the
dimensions of moral space.}

The responsibility tensor depends on:

\textbf{Causal contribution.} How much did member \(a\)'s actions
contribute to \(\omega\)? This is the causal component of responsibility
--- the counterfactual question of whether \(\omega\) would have
occurred without \(a\)'s contribution.

\textbf{Epistemic position.} Did member \(a\) know, or should they have
known, that \(\omega\) was a likely consequence? Culpable ignorance
(failure to know what one should have known) generates responsibility;
blameless ignorance mitigates it.

\textbf{Role authority.} Did member \(a\) have the authority and
capacity to prevent \(\omega\)? A supervisor with the power to stop a
harmful action bears more responsibility than a subordinate who lacked
the power to intervene.

\textbf{Degree of endorsement.} Did member \(a\) endorse the collective
action, or were they coerced, overruled, or absent? Active endorsement
amplifies responsibility; coerced participation mitigates it.

\subsubsection{Is Responsibility
Conserved?}\label{is-responsibility-conserved}

A fundamental question: does the sum of individual responsibilities
equal the collective responsibility?

\[\sum_{a \in A}^{}R_{a}(\omega)\overset{?}{=}R_{\text{collective}}(\omega)\]

If the answer is yes, responsibility is \emph{conserved}: it can be
fully distributed to individuals, with no remainder. If the answer is
no, there is a \emph{responsibility gap} (if the sum is less) or a
\emph{responsibility surplus} (if the sum is greater).

\textbf{Claim.} In the presence of emergent obligations
(\(\mathcal{E \neq}0\)), responsibility is generically \emph{not}
conserved. The gap:

\[\Delta R = R_{\text{collective}} - \sum_{a \in A}^{}R_{a}\]

is the \emph{collective responsibility remainder} --- the responsibility
that belongs to the collective as a structured entity, not to any
individual member. This remainder is the moral analogue of the emergent
component \(\mathcal{E}\): it exists because the collective has moral
agency that exceeds the sum of its members' agencies.

\textbf{Example.} A committee votes unanimously to implement a policy
that turns out to be harmful. Each member bears responsibility for their
vote (\(R_{a} > 0\) for all \(a\)). But the committee also bears
collective responsibility for the policy as a whole --- responsibility
that includes the failures of the committee's deliberative process, its
information-gathering procedures, and its institutional design. This
collective responsibility may exceed the sum of individual
responsibilities: \(\Delta R > 0\).

\textbf{Example.} A mob commits violence. Each individual participant
bears responsibility. But the mob --- as a collective agent with
emergent dynamics (escalation, deindividuation, collective action) ---
bears additional responsibility for creating the conditions under which
individual members acted as they did. The responsibility surplus
\(\Delta R > 0\) captures the moral content of incitement and collective
frenzy.

\subsubsection{The Complicity Gradient}\label{the-complicity-gradient}

In many collective situations, responsibility is not binary
(guilty/innocent) but continuously graded. The \emph{complicity
gradient} is the gradient of the responsibility function across the
member space:

\[\nabla_{a}R = \frac{\partial R}{\partial(\text{member }a\text{’s position})}\]

This gradient measures how much more (or less) responsible a member
becomes as their position in the collective changes --- as they move
from periphery to center, from subordinate to authority, from observer
to participant.

The complicity gradient is a moral covector on the member space: it
weights members by their marginal contribution to collective
responsibility. It provides a geometric decomposition of collective
responsibility that goes beyond the binary question of ``who is
responsible?'' to the structural question of ``how does responsibility
vary with position in the collective?''

\subsection{14.7 The Geometry of Institutional
Structure}\label{the-geometry-of-institutional-structure}

\subsubsection{Institutions as Moral
Manifolds}\label{institutions-as-moral-manifolds}

An institution is a collective agent with sufficient structure and
persistence to constitute a moral entity in its own right. Corporations,
states, universities, hospitals, professional associations, and
international bodies are all institutions in this sense.

The geometric framework suggests treating the institution itself as a
\emph{moral manifold} --- a space whose points are the possible
configurations of the institution (its policies, practices, norms, and
distributions of authority), and whose tangent vectors are possible
institutional changes.

\textbf{Definition 14.5 (Institutional Manifold).} \emph{The
institutional manifold} \(\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\) \emph{of an
institution} \(\mathcal{I}\) \emph{is the space of the institution's
possible configurations. A point} \(q \in \mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\)
\emph{specifies:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \emph{The institution's policies and rules}
\item
  \emph{The distribution of authority among members}
\item
  \emph{The decision procedures in force}
\item
  \emph{The institution's relationship to external stakeholders}
\end{enumerate}

The moral manifold \(\mathcal{M}\) from Chapter 5 and the institutional
manifold \(\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\) are distinct but related. They
are coupled by a \emph{projection}:

\[\pi:\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\mathcal{\rightarrow M}\]

that maps each institutional configuration to the moral situation it
generates. Different institutional configurations (different policies,
different authority distributions) may generate the same moral situation
--- or different ones. The fiber \(\pi^{- 1}(p)\) over a moral point
\(p\) consists of all institutional configurations that produce
situation \(p\).

\subsubsection{Institutional Metrics}\label{institutional-metrics}

The institutional manifold carries its own metric --- the
\emph{institutional metric} \(h_{ij}\) --- that measures the ``cost'' of
institutional change:

\[ds_{\text{inst}}^{2} = h_{ij}\, dq^{i}\, dq^{j}\]

This metric encodes how difficult it is to change the institution along
various dimensions. Some institutional changes are easy (adjusting a
minor policy): low cost, small \(h_{ij}\). Others are hard
(restructuring the authority hierarchy): high cost, large \(h_{ij}\).
Some are nearly impossible (changing the institution's fundamental
purpose): the metric approaches infinity.

The institutional metric is related to the moral metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\)
through the projection \(\pi\):

\[g_{\mu\nu} = \frac{\partial q^{i}}{\partial x^{\mu}}\frac{\partial q^{j}}{\partial x^{\nu}}\, h_{ij}\]

The moral trade-off structure is the \emph{pullback} of the
institutional metric through the projection --- the moral costs of
change are determined, in part, by the institutional costs of the
changes required to effect them.

\subsubsection{Institutional Design as Geometric
Optimization}\label{institutional-design-as-geometric-optimization}

If the institutional manifold \(\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\) is equipped
with a metric (the cost structure of change) and a potential (the
``moral energy'' of each configuration), then \emph{institutional
design} is a problem in Riemannian optimization: find the configuration
\(q^{*} \in \mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\) that minimizes the moral
potential, subject to the institutional constraints.

This is the geometric analogue of \emph{mechanism design} in economics:
designing institutions to achieve desired outcomes, given that the
institution's internal structure mediates between individual actions and
collective results.

The geometric framework adds precision: the institutional metric
determines which changes are feasible, the curvature of
\(\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\) determines whether optimization is
well-behaved (convex) or pathological (multiple local minima, saddle
points), and the projection \(\pi\) determines how institutional changes
translate into moral outcomes.

\subsection{14.8 Collective Moral
Dynamics}\label{collective-moral-dynamics}

\subsubsection{Parallel Transport of Collective
Obligations}\label{parallel-transport-of-collective-obligations}

Chapter 10 developed the parallel transport of individual obligations
across changing circumstances. The same formalism extends to collective
obligations, but with additional structure arising from the collective's
internal dynamics.

When a collective obligation is parallel-transported along a path in
moral space, the \emph{internal structure of the collective may also
change}. The obligation to defend citizens is parallel-transported from
peacetime to wartime --- but the internal structure of the state changes
simultaneously (emergency powers, reallocation of authority,
mobilization of resources). The transport of the obligation and the
transformation of the structure are coupled.

\textbf{Definition 14.6 (Collective Parallel Transport).} \emph{The
parallel transport of a collective obligation} \(O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu}\)
\emph{along a path} \(\gamma\) \emph{in} \(\mathcal{M}\) \emph{is
governed by:}

\[\nabla_{\dot{\gamma}}O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu} + \Gamma_{\nu\rho}^{\mu}\,{\dot{\gamma}}^{\nu}\, O_{\text{coll}}^{\rho} + \Lambda_{a}^{\mu}\,{\dot{\Sigma}}_{a} = 0\]

\emph{where} \(\Gamma_{\nu\rho}^{\mu}\) \emph{is the Levi-Civita
connection (as in Chapter 10),} \(\Lambda_{a}^{\mu}\) \emph{is the
structural coupling --- encoding how changes in the structure tensor}
\(\Sigma\) \emph{affect the collective obligation --- and}
\({\dot{\Sigma}}_{a}\) \emph{is the rate of change of the structure.}

The additional term \(\Lambda_{a}^{\mu}{\dot{\Sigma}}_{a}\) is the key
difference between individual and collective parallel transport. When
the collective's internal structure is stable (\(\dot{\Sigma} = 0\)),
collective transport reduces to individual transport. When the structure
is changing, the collective obligation is pulled in directions
determined by the structural coupling \(\Lambda\) --- directions that
may not align with the individual transport.

\subsubsection{Collective Holonomy}\label{collective-holonomy}

The holonomy of a collective obligation around a closed loop may include
contributions from the structural coupling:

\[H_{\gamma}^{\text{coll}} = H_{\gamma}^{\text{ind}} + \oint\Lambda_{a}^{\mu}\, d\Sigma_{a}\]

The second term is the \emph{structural holonomy} --- the contribution
of internal reorganization to the collective obligation's rotation
around the loop. A collective that reorganizes as it traverses a moral
circuit (e.g., a corporation that restructures during a crisis) may
acquire collective holonomy even if the individual-level holonomy is
zero.

\textbf{Example.} A university faces a controversy, reorganizes its
governance in response, and returns to ``normal operations.'' The
structural holonomy measures how the governance reorganization has
permanently altered the university's moral orientation --- even though
the explicit policies may look the same as before. The collective
obligation has been rotated by the structural journey.

\subsection{14.9 Collective Agents in the Nine
Dimensions}\label{collective-agents-in-the-nine-dimensions}

\subsubsection{The Collective Scope}\label{the-collective-scope}

Chapter 5 (§5.2) organized the nine moral dimensions via a 3 × 3 matrix:
three ethical scopes (Individual, Relational, Collective) × three
epistemic modes (What Matters, Who Decides, What We Know). The
``Collective'' row --- Dimensions 3, 8, 6 (Justice/Fairness, Procedural
Legitimacy, Societal/Environmental Impact) --- is precisely the row that
collective agents operate in most naturally.

Individual agents primarily generate obligations along the Individual
and Relational rows. Collective agents primarily generate obligations
along the Collective row --- and they do so with a strength and
specificity that individual agents cannot match. The obligation of a
state to provide justice (Dimension 3) is stronger, more specific, and
more structurally grounded than any individual citizen's obligation to
promote justice. The obligation of a corporation to ensure procedural
legitimacy (Dimension 8) is an obligation of the institution, not of any
individual officer.

\subsubsection{The Collective-Individual
Coupling}\label{the-collective-individual-coupling}

The relationship between collective and individual obligations is not
one-directional. Collective obligations \emph{feed back} into individual
obligations through the structure tensor.

\textbf{Downward causation.} A collective obligation
\(O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu}\) generates individual obligations through the
structure tensor:

\[O_{a}^{\mu} = \Sigma_{ab}\,\mathcal{C}_{\nu}^{- 1\mu}\, O_{\text{coll}}^{\nu}\]

Each member acquires an individual obligation that is their ``share'' of
the collective obligation, weighted by their structural position. A
firefighter's individual obligation to rescue (Dimension 1) is generated
by the fire department's collective obligation to protect, mediated by
the firefighter's role in the department's structure.

\textbf{Upward emergence.} Individual moral actions aggregate (through
the collective agency tensor) into collective moral properties:

\[O_{\text{coll}}^{\mu} = \mathcal{C}_{\nu_{1}\cdots\nu_{n}}^{\mu}\, O_{\nu_{1}}^{(1)}\,\cdots\, O_{\nu_{n}}^{(n)}\]

The collective is constituted by individual actions but not reducible to
them. The interplay between downward causation and upward emergence
creates a feedback loop: collective obligations shape individual ones,
which in turn constitute the collective. This circularity is not vicious
--- it is the structural content of the sociological fact that
institutions and individuals mutually constitute each other.

\subsection{14.10 Emergent Moral
Properties}\label{emergent-moral-properties}

\subsubsection{Properties That Exist Only at the Collective
Level}\label{properties-that-exist-only-at-the-collective-level}

Some moral properties have no individual analogue. They exist only at
the collective level and resist decomposition.

\textbf{Justice.} Justice is a property of social arrangements --- of
how benefits and burdens are distributed across a population. An
individual can \emph{act justly} (treating each person fairly in
individual interactions), but justice as a \emph{structural property}
--- the fairness of the overall distribution --- is a collective
attribute. In geometric terms, justice is a function on
\(\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\), not on the individual moral manifold.

\textbf{Legitimacy.} Political legitimacy --- the right of an
institution to make binding decisions --- is a collective property that
no individual possesses. A judge has authority by virtue of the judicial
system's legitimacy, not by individual qualities. Legitimacy is an
emergent property of the institution's structure tensor and its
relationship to the governed.

\textbf{Democratic representation.} The property of a decision's being
democratically legitimate requires a \emph{process} involving multiple
agents, structured by rules of participation, deliberation, and voting.
No individual action can be ``democratic'' --- democracy is irreducibly
collective.

\subsubsection{The Emergent Properties
Criterion}\label{the-emergent-properties-criterion}

\textbf{Proposition 14.2 (Criterion for Emergent Moral Property).}
\emph{A moral property} \(P\) \emph{is emergent at the collective level
if and only if:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \(P\) \emph{is well-defined on collective moral states:}
  \(P:\mathcal{M}_{\text{inst}}\mathbb{\rightarrow R}\)
\item
  \(P\) \emph{is not expressible as a function of individual moral
  properties alone:} \(P \neq f(P_{1},P_{2},\ldots,P_{n})\) \emph{for
  any function} \(f\) \emph{and any set of individual-level properties}
  \(\{ P_{a}\}\)
\item
  \(P\) \emph{depends on the structure tensor:}
  \(\partial P/\partial\Sigma_{ab} \neq 0\) \emph{for some} \(a,b\)
\end{enumerate}

Condition (3) is the geometric marker of emergence: the property depends
on the relationships between members, not just on the members
themselves.

\emph{Proof.} Necessity. (1) If P is not well-defined on M\_inst, it
cannot be a property of collectives at all. (2) If P = f(P₁, \ldots,
P\_n) for some function f and individual-level properties \{P\_a\}, then
P is reducible to individual properties and is not emergent by
definition. (3) If ∂P/∂Σ\_\{ab\} = 0 for all a, b, then P does not
depend on the structure tensor and hence depends only on properties of
the individual members, reducing to case (2). Sufficiency. Suppose all
three conditions hold. By (1), P is a well-defined collective property.
By (2), P is not expressible as a function of individual properties
alone. By (3), the irreducibility is structural --- P depends on the
relationships between members, not just their individual states. Hence P
is emergent at the collective level. □

\subsection{14.11 AI Systems as Collective
Agents}\label{ai-systems-as-collective-agents}

\subsubsection{Human-AI Collectives}\label{human-ai-collectives}

As AI systems take on morally significant roles, the question of
collective agency becomes urgent. A human-AI team --- a physician using
a diagnostic AI, a judge using a sentencing algorithm, a pilot using an
autopilot --- is a collective agent whose moral structure requires
analysis.

The collective agency tensor of a human-AI team has the form:

\[\mathcal{C}_{\nu_{H}\nu_{AI}}^{\mu}\]

where \(\nu_{H}\) indexes the human's interest dimensions and
\(\nu_{AI}\) indexes the AI's ``interest'' dimensions (more precisely,
the objective-function dimensions that the AI is designed to optimize).

The emergent component \(\mathcal{E}\) of this tensor is particularly
important for AI alignment. If \(\mathcal{E \neq}0\), the human-AI team
generates collective obligations that neither the human nor the AI bears
individually. These emergent obligations may be \emph{desirable} (the
team can achieve outcomes neither could alone) or \emph{dangerous} (the
team generates harms that neither would generate alone).

\subsubsection{Responsibility Distribution in Human-AI
Teams}\label{responsibility-distribution-in-human-ai-teams}

When a human-AI team produces a harmful outcome, the responsibility
tensor \(R_{a}(\omega)\) must be defined for both the human member and
the AI member. But the AI's ``responsibility'' is different in character
from the human's:

The AI has no moral autonomy --- its actions are determined by its
training, its design, and its deployment. Attributing responsibility to
the AI is a shorthand for attributing responsibility to the \emph{chain
of human decisions} that created, trained, deployed, and maintained the
AI. The responsibility tensor for the AI component decomposes into:

\[R_{AI}(\omega) = R_{\text{designer}} + R_{\text{trainer}} + R_{\text{deployer}} + R_{\text{operator}} + R_{\text{emergent}}\]

where \(R_{\text{emergent}}\) is the residual responsibility that cannot
be attributed to any individual in the chain. This residual is the
