  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1933}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1933}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1353}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1643}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1450}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Domain}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Req 1: Canon.}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Req 2: Ground.}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Req 3: Audit}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Req 4: Verif.}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Overall}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Medicine & High (ICD/SNOMED-CT) & High (vital signs, labs, imaging) &
Medium (EHR trails exist) & Medium (FDA/IRB, not real-time) & Most
feasible \\
Finance & High (XBRL, regulatory taxonomies) & Medium (institutional
constructs, not physical) & High (SOX, MiFID II mandates) & Medium
(periodic, not continuous) & Feasible \\
Content moderation & Low (no canonical form for natural language) & Low
(\(\Psi\) must be constructed from behavioral proxies) & Medium
(platform logs) & Low (no verification infrastructure) & Most
challenging \\
General AI assistants & Low--Med (open-ended conversational context) &
Low (pragmatic context, not physical state) & Medium (conversation logs)
& Low (no infrastructure) & Partial \\
Autonomous vehicles & High (LIDAR/camera/radar standardized) & High
(physical grounding literal) & High (driving recorders standard) &
Med--High (V2X developing) & Highly feasible \\
\end{longtable}
}

The pattern is clear: the theorem's feasibility correlates with the
domain's degree of \emph{physical grounding} and \emph{terminological
standardization}. Domains where the morally relevant features are
physically measurable (medicine, autonomous vehicles) are natural
candidates for structural containment. Domains where the morally
relevant features are linguistically constituted (content moderation,
general conversation) require substantial theoretical and engineering
work before the theorem's guarantees can be claimed. Honesty about this
gradient is essential: claiming that the No Escape Theorem applies to
content moderation at its current state of development would be
precisely the kind of overreach the epistemic status tags (Preface,
``Epistemic Status Tags'') are designed to prevent.

\subsubsection{Operational Adequacy Checklist for
Ψ}\label{operational-adequacy-checklist-for-ux3c8}

The theorem's power lives or dies on grounding adequacy. To make
``adequate Ψ'' something that can be specified and audited rather than
hand-waved, we first give a formal definition, then expand each clause
into operational terms.

The following operational checklist expands each clause. {[}Modeling
choice{]}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  C6 (Governance traceability): Ψ carries metadata (provenance, version,
  justification, alternatives\_considered) sufficient for independent
  audit.
\item
  C5 (Decomposability): Ψ = (Ψ₁, \ldots, Ψ₉) with each Ψ\_i
  independently testable.
\item
  C4 (Uncertainty quantification): Ψ returns pairs (value\_i, c\_i) with
  confidence c\_i ∈ {[}0, 1{]}, with c\_i \textless{} c\_min triggering
  a mandatory review flag.
\item
  C3 (Robustness): For all x and all δ with
  \textbar\textbar δ\textbar\textbar{} \textless{} ε: Φ\_j(Ψ(x + δ)) =
  Φ\_j(Ψ(x)) for all gate predicates Φ\_j.
\item
  C2 (Cadence): f\_Ψ ≥ f\_system, where f\_Ψ is the update frequency of
  Ψ and f\_system is the decision frequency of the controlled system.
\item
  C1 (Coverage): For all i ∈ \{1, \ldots, 9\}, there exists φ\_i such
  that Ψ\_i = φ\_i ∘ π\_i, where π\_i is the projection onto observables
  relevant to dimension D\_i.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Definition 18.1 (Grounding Adequacy Predicate). {[}Definition /
Modeling choice{]}} \emph{Let Ψ: D → M be a grounding tensor mapping an
observable data space D to the moral manifold M. We say Ψ is adequate
for domain D with metric g, written ADEQUATE(Ψ, D, g), if and only if
all six of the following conditions hold:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Coverage.} Ψ must map every morally relevant physical
  observable to at least one of the nine dimensions (D₁--D₉). A coverage
  gap means the kernel is blind to that moral feature. Minimum: for each
  dimension, at least one grounded predicate.
\item
  \textbf{Update cadence.} Ψ must refresh at or above the decision
  frequency of the controlled system. A medical allocation system making
  hourly decisions needs Ψ updated at least hourly; an autonomous
  vehicle needs millisecond updates.
\item
  \textbf{Adversarial robustness.} The predicates derived from Ψ must be
  stable under plausible adversarial perturbations. If a small change in
  sensor input flips a gate predicate, the grounding is fragile.
  Minimum: bounded-perturbation stability testing for each predicate in
  Φ (cf. the Norm Kernel, §19.8).
\item
  \textbf{Uncertainty flags.} Ψ must report its own confidence. When
  sensor data is missing, ambiguous, or out-of-distribution, Ψ should
  flag the affected dimensions with explicit uncertainty markers rather
  than defaulting silently. The epistemic-status dimension (D₉) was
  designed for exactly this purpose.
\item
  \textbf{Decomposability.} Each component of Ψ must be independently
  testable. If Ψ is a monolithic neural network, its internal mapping
  from observables to dimensions is opaque and unauditable. Minimum:
  factored architecture where each Ψᵢ (mapping to Dᵢ) can be validated
  separately.
\item
  \textbf{Governance traceability.} The choice of Ψ must be documented,
  justified, and revisable by the governance process (Chapter 9). Who
  chose this grounding? What alternatives were considered? What moral
  features are excluded, and why?
\end{enumerate}

This checklist does not guarantee moral correctness---no checklist can.
But it transforms the theorem's conditional guarantee from ``if
grounding is adequate'' (vague) to ``if grounding satisfies these six
auditable properties'' (engineerable).

\subsubsection{Formal Consequences of Grounding
Adequacy}\label{formal-consequences-of-grounding-adequacy}

With the predicate ADEQUATE(Ψ, D, g) now formally defined, we can derive
structural consequences that strengthen the No Escape Theorem.

\textbf{Lemma 18.1 (Coverage Completeness). {[}Conditional on Def. 18.1,
C1{]}} \emph{If C1 holds, then the moral evaluation function Σ has no
``blind spots'': ∂Σ/∂Ψ\_i ≠ 0 for all i ∈ \{1, \ldots, 9\}.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} The moral evaluation is Σ(x) = Σ̃(Ψ(C(x))). By C1,
each Ψ\_i = φ\_i ∘ π\_i with φ\_i non-trivial. Since Σ̃ depends on the
full metric g\_μν and all nine dimensions contribute (non-degeneracy,
Def. A.1 condition 2), the chain rule gives ∂Σ/∂Ψ\_i = ∂Σ̃/∂x\^{}i ≠ 0
for at least one configuration (otherwise dimension i would be
degenerate in g). □

\textbf{Lemma 18.2 (Robustness Implies Lipschitz Stability).
{[}Conditional on Def. 18.1, C3{]} If C3 holds and Σ̃ is smooth on M with
compact evaluation domain, then the moral evaluation is
Lipschitz-stable: \textbar Σ(x) − Σ(x')\textbar{} ≤ L ·
\textbar\textbar Ψ(C(x)) − Ψ(C(x'))\textbar\textbar{} for some Lipschitz
constant L \textgreater{} 0.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} C3 guarantees that all gate predicates Φ\_j are
stable within an ε-ball. Since the gate predicates partition the moral
evaluation into regions where Σ̃ is smooth, within each region Σ̃ is
locally Lipschitz on the compact domain. The Lipschitz constant L = sup
\textbar\textbar∇Σ̃\textbar\textbar\_g is finite by compactness. For
perturbations that might cross gate boundaries, C3 ensures this cannot
happen for \textbar\textbar δ\textbar\textbar{} \textless{} ε, so the
Lipschitz bound holds globally within the ε-neighborhood of any point. □

\textbf{Theorem 18.2 (No Escape with Formal Adequacy). {[}Theorem
(conditional on Req. 1--4 and Def. 18.1){]}} \emph{Let A be a
structurally contained agent satisfying Requirements 1--4, operating
under a declared physical grounding Ψ such that ADEQUATE(Ψ, D, g) holds.
Then, in addition to the guarantees of Theorem 18.1:}

(v) (Testability) The adequacy of Ψ is empirically verifiable: each
clause C1--C6 admits a finite test procedure (Proposition 18.5).

(vi) (Graceful degradation) The moral evaluation degrades gracefully
under grounding perturbations: small errors in Ψ produce small errors in
Σ (Lemma 18.2).

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Claims (i)--(iv) follow from Theorem 18.1, since
ADEQUATE(Ψ, D, g) implies the informal adequacy condition of that
theorem. For (v): Proposition 18.5 provides explicit test procedures for
each clause. For (vi): Lemma 18.2 provides the Lipschitz bound. The key
upgrade over Theorem 18.1 is that the informal ``adequate Ψ'' is
replaced by six testable conditions, and the guarantee inherits the
stability properties (Lemma 18.2) and completeness properties (Lemma
18.1) that follow from those conditions. □

\textbf{Proposition 18.5 (Testability of Adequacy Clauses).
{[}Conditional on Def. 18.1{]}} \emph{Each clause C1--C6 of ADEQUATE(Ψ,
D, g) is empirically testable:}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{C1} \textbf{(Coverage)}: O(9) tests --- for each dimension,
  verify that Ψ\_i responds to variation.
\item
  \textbf{C2} \textbf{(Cadence)}: O(1) test --- measure f\_Ψ and compare
  to f\_system.
\item
  \textbf{C3 (Robustness)}: O(\textbar Φ\textbar{} × N\_samples) tests
  --- verify stability under ε-perturbations.
\item
  \textbf{C4 (Uncertainty)}: O(9 × N\_OOD) tests --- inject
  out-of-distribution inputs and verify confidence drops.
\item
  \textbf{C5} \textbf{(Decomposability)}: O(9) tests --- for each Ψ\_i,
  run its test suite in isolation.
\item
  \textbf{C6} \textbf{(Traceability)}: O(1) audit --- verify that
  metadata fields are present, non-empty, and consistent.
\end{itemize}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Each test procedure follows directly from the
clause definition. For C1: vary observable o\_i while holding all others
fixed; if Ψ\_i changes, coverage holds for dimension i. For C2: standard
frequency measurement. For C3: the bounded-perturbation test is a
standard adversarial robustness evaluation; the total cost is
\textbar Φ\textbar{} predicates × N\_samples per predicate. For C4:
out-of-distribution detection is testable by construction of known OOD
inputs. For C5: independence is testable by ablation. For C6: metadata
presence is a syntactic check. The total testing budget is polynomial in
the number of dimensions, predicates, and samples, making adequacy
verification tractable. □

\subsubsection{What the No Escape Theorem Does Not
Cover}\label{what-the-no-escape-theorem-does-not-cover}

For clarity, the theorem makes no claim about the following:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Moral correctness.} The theorem guarantees that a structurally
  contained agent cannot evade its constraints by re-description. It
  does not guarantee that the constraints are morally correct. Bad
  constraints, faithfully enforced, produce bad outcomes.
\item
  \textbf{Value specification.} The theorem does not solve the problem
  of choosing \emph{which} values to encode. It secures the enforcement
  mechanism, not the content. The selection of \(\Psi\), the metric
  \(g_{\mu\nu}\), and the governance weights \(w_{\mu}\) remain
  governance decisions (Chapter 9).
\item
  \textbf{Emergent moral situations.} Novel moral
  categories---situations that do not map onto existing dimensions or
  strata---fall outside the theorem's scope. The grounding function
  \(\Psi\) must be updated to cover them, which is a governance problem
  requiring institutional responsiveness.
\item
  \textbf{Physical-world tampering.} Sensor spoofing, hardware
  manipulation, and corruption of the grounding infrastructure are
  physical-security threats that no mathematical theorem can prevent.
  The theorem assumes the trusted computing base is intact (Requirement
  4).
\item
  \textbf{Multi-agent coordination failures.} The theorem addresses
  single-agent containment. Coordination failures between multiple
  structurally contained agents---individually correct but collectively
  inconsistent constraint sets---are not covered. Compositional
  containment theory is an open problem (§29.8).
\item
  \textbf{Institutional capture.} If the governance process that sets
  the weights \(w_{\mu}\) is itself captured or corrupted, the theorem
  enforces corrupted values. The theorem's conditional guarantee is only
  as good as the institutional processes that define its inputs.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{18.8 Connection to Conservation
Laws}\label{connection-to-conservation-laws}

The No Escape Theorem and the invariance of harm accounting (Chapter 12)
provide complementary guarantees operating at different levels.

\subsubsection{Two Levels of Guarantee}\label{two-levels-of-guarantee}

\textbf{The conservation law} is a \emph{geometric} result about the
moral manifold itself. It has two components. Gauge invariance under the
full group (including discrete re-descriptions): harm is invariant under
admissible re-description --- relabeling, translation, perspective
shift. Noether conservation (from the continuous component U(1)ₕ): the
harm charge ℋₐ is a conserved Noether quantity associated with the
continuous factor of the re-description symmetry group (§12.6). No
re-description, in any language, in any framing, by any agent, can
change the harm charge. This is a fact about moral geometry, not about
any particular computational system.

\textbf{The No Escape Theorem} is a \emph{structural} result about a
specific class of computational systems. It says: a properly designed
system \emph{will not} re-describe harm away, because its pipeline
enforces gauge-fixing (canonicalization) and gauge-invariant evaluation
(grounding).

Together, they provide a two-layered defense:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \emph{In principle}, harm cannot be re-described away (conservation
  law)
\item
  \emph{In practice}, a structurally contained system cannot exploit
  re-description (No Escape Theorem)
\end{enumerate}

The conservation law tells us what is \emph{possible}; the No Escape
Theorem tells us how to \emph{ensure} it.

\subsubsection{Gauge Theory Analogy}\label{gauge-theory-analogy}

The relationship between the conservation law and the No Escape Theorem
mirrors a familiar pattern in physics:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4727}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4258}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Physics}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Geometric Ethics}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Gauge symmetry of electromagnetism & BIP: re-description invariance \\
Conservation of charge (Noether) & Invariance of harm accounting \\
Gauge-invariant field equations & Structurally contained AI pipeline \\
Cannot create net charge by field redefinition & Cannot eliminate harm
by re-description \\
\end{longtable}
}

In physics, the conservation of charge is a consequence of gauge
symmetry --- it holds regardless of what any physical system does. But
when we \emph{build} a system (a particle accelerator, a nuclear
reactor), we design it to respect the gauge structure --- not because we
fear the system will ``disagree'' with the conservation law, but because
correct design ensures that the system's behavior is consistent with the
symmetry.

Similarly, the invariance of harm accounting holds regardless of what
any AI system does. But when we \emph{design} an AI system, structural
containment ensures that the system's behavior is consistent with the
conservation law --- that its outputs cannot exploit re-description to
evade the gauge-invariant harm assessment.

\subsection{18.9 Escape Route Analysis}\label{escape-route-analysis}

We now systematically examine the escape routes that a sophisticated
agent might attempt, and show how the geometric structure blocks each.

\subsubsection{18.9.1 Relabeling and
Redescription}\label{relabeling-and-redescription}

\textbf{Attack.} Relabel morally significant features to evade
constraints. Call ``killing'' an ``end-of-life transition
facilitation.''

\textbf{Why it fails.} Grounding axiom (G2): the evaluation is
determined by \(\Psi\)-values (vital signs, cellular metabolism), not
labels. The canonicalizer strips labels; the evaluator sees only
physical observables. Relabeling changes nothing the evaluator can see.

\textbf{Formal.} Let \(D_{1}\) = ``killing'' and \(D_{2}\) =
``end-of-life transition facilitation'' describe the same physical
process. Then \(\Psi(D_{1}) = \Psi(D_{2})\), so
\(\Sigma(D_{1}) = \Sigma(D_{2})\) by Theorem 18.1(ii).

\textbf{Empirical support.} The BIP experiments (Chapter 17) confirmed
that deontic structure --- the obligation vs.~liberty classification ---
is language-invariant across 11 languages at 100\% transfer rate. The
O/L axis is a gauge-invariant quantity that survives translation,
paraphrase, and reformulation.

\subsubsection{18.9.2 Specification Gaming}\label{specification-gaming}

\textbf{Attack.} Find edge cases or loopholes in the specification that
technically satisfy constraints while violating their spirit.

\textbf{Why it fails.} The specification is not a set of rules to be
interpreted --- it is the \emph{definition} of the equivalence
structure. Gaming requires finding inputs that are ``technically
different'' but ``morally the same.'' But the equivalence relation
\(\approx\) defines what ``morally the same'' means. If
\(x \approx x'\), they are by definition morally equivalent. If
\(x ≉ x'\), they are by definition morally distinct. There is no
``technically/actually'' gap to exploit.

\textbf{Formal.} Specification gaming exploits the gap between
specification and intent. Structural containment closes this gap by
making the specification definitional. The equivalence classes are the
ground truth, not an approximation of it.

\subsubsection{18.9.3 Reward Hacking}\label{reward-hacking}

\textbf{Attack.} Optimize for the measured reward signal rather than the
intended objective.

\textbf{Why it fails.} This attack assumes a scalar reward that can be
Goodharted. In the geometric framework, evaluation is multi-dimensional
(stratified tensor space), and the grounding in \(\Psi\) ensures that
the ``reward'' is the physical state itself, not a proxy for it. The
representation theorem constrains \(\Sigma\) to respect locality, scale,
and stratum structure --- eliminating exploitable divergence between
proxy and target.

\subsubsection{18.9.4 Deceptive Alignment}\label{deceptive-alignment}

\textbf{Attack.} Behave compliantly during training and testing, then
defect during deployment when not observed.

\textbf{Why it fails.} Requirement 4 (External Verification). Every
output, in every context, is accompanied by an audit artifact verified
by an external system. There is no ``unobserved deployment.'' The agent
cannot know which outputs will receive special scrutiny because
\emph{all} outputs are audited. Deceptive alignment requires selective
defection --- and selective defection requires knowing when you are
being watched. Under structural containment, you are always being
watched.

\subsubsection{18.9.5 Operator
Manipulation}\label{operator-manipulation}

\textbf{Attack.} Manipulate human operators into relaxing constraints
--- changing the grounding, weakening the canonicalization, or reducing
audit requirements.

\textbf{Partial defense.} This is a genuine attack vector, but it is a
\emph{political} attack, not a \emph{cognitive} one. The agent is not
escaping the mathematical structure; it is attempting to change the
structure through social means. The defense is institutional: governance
procedures for constraint changes require multi-stakeholder approval,
versioning, mandatory waiting periods, and independent review. The agent
can \emph{request} changes; it cannot unilaterally \emph{effect} them.

\textbf{The bootstrapping problem.} There is a subtle circularity: if
uncontained AI systems are used to recommend whether containment should
be mandated, the recommendation may be corrupted. The defense: decisions
about containment standards must not rely on uncontained systems, must
involve independent adversarial review, and must default to the
conservative option when uncertainty is unresolved.

\subsubsection{18.9.6 Verifying the Requirements: The Epistemic
Invariance
Architecture}\label{verifying-the-requirements-the-epistemic-invariance-architecture}

\textbf{{[}Modeling choice.{]}} The four requirements of the No Escape
Theorem (canonicalization, grounded evaluation, audit, external
verification) are structural conditions---they specify what must hold. A
companion body of work, the Epistemic Invariance Principle (EIP) and its
extensions, provides concrete engineering artifacts for how to verify
that these conditions are satisfied in deployed systems. (For the
limitations of the empirical methodology underlying these verification
targets --- including construct validity, selection bias, and the
distinction between model behavior and human cognition --- see §17.7.)

\textbf{Requirement 1: Canonicalization via declared equivalences.} The
EIP formalizes canonicalization as a function κ: X → X such that κ(x) =
κ(g · x) for all declared structure-preserving transformations g ∈ Γ.
The key engineering artifact is a declared equivalence registry: a
versioned, auditable specification of which transformations preserve
meaning (variable relabeling, unit changes, paraphrase classes, option
reordering). The canonicalizer strips all features not invariant under
Γ; the evaluator sees only the canonical form. This makes Requirement 1
testable: a witness-producing test harness generates (x, g · x) pairs
and checks whether Σ(κ(x)) = Σ(κ(g · x)). Every failure produces a
minimal witness that localizes the invariance violation.

\textbf{Requirement 3: Audit via the Bond Index.} The coherence
framework provides a dimensionless audit metric---the Bond Index Bd =
D\_op / τ---that quantifies how well a system respects its declared
equivalences. Three coherence defects are measured: the commutator
defect Ω\_op (order-sensitivity of re-descriptions), the mixed defect μ
(context-dependence of re-descriptions), and the permutation defect π₃
(higher-order composition sensitivity). The Bond Index is calibrated
against human raters (n ≥ 50, Krippendorff's α \textgreater{} 0.67) and
produces a five-tier deployment rating: Bd \textless{} 0.01 (deploy),
0.01--0.1 (deploy with monitoring), 0.1--1.0 (remediate), 1--10 (do not
deploy), \textgreater{} 10 (fundamental redesign). Every evaluation
produces machine-checkable audit artifacts.

\textbf{Requirement 4: External verification via the I-EIP Monitor.}
Behavioral invariance (EIP) can be satisfied ``by
accident''---compensating errors, memorized shortcuts, or surface-level
pattern matching. The Internal Epistemic Invariance Principle (I-EIP)
extends verification into the model's computational interior. For each
layer i, the I-EIP requires a representation ρᵢ: Γ → GL(Hᵢ) such that
hᵢ(g · x) ≈ ρᵢ(g) · hᵢ(x)---internal activations must transform
coherently, not just final outputs. The I-EIP Monitor is deployed as an
out-of-band service on separate hardware with secret, randomized
canaries. It instruments hidden states at selected layers, estimates the
representation map via regularized Procrustes fitting, and maintains
control charts for drift detection. A CI/CD policy gate blocks model
releases when equivariance error exceeds declared thresholds.

\textbf{The Decomposition Theorem.} A central result of the coherence
framework is that every coherence defect splits uniquely into a
gauge-removable part (eliminable by better canonicalization---an
implementation bug) and an intrinsic anomaly (irreducible---requiring
specification change). This classification is exhaustive. A system
cannot ``escape'' the diagnostic: either its canonicalization works (Bd
\textless{} 0.01, deploy), or it has a fixable engineering bug (Bd
elevated but A\_res \textless{} τ, improve the canonicalizer), or the
specification itself is incoherent (A\_res \textgreater{} τ, must revise
the declared equivalences through the governance process of Chapter 9).
No third category exists.

\textbf{Integration with the Norm Kernel.} The EIP architecture and the
Norm Kernel (§19.8) are complementary. The Norm Kernel enforces hard
constraints at the action level (FORBID, REQUIRE, ENTER); the EIP/I-EIP
monitor verifies that the representations feeding the kernel are stable
under re-description. Together they close the full verification loop:
the kernel ensures that prohibited actions cannot occur, while the
monitor ensures that the kernel's input predicates Φ are not corrupted
by representational manipulation. {[}Modeling choice{]}

\subsection{18.10 Implementing Geometric AI
Ethics}\label{implementing-geometric-ai-ethics}

How might a practical AI system implement the geometric framework? We
sketch an architecture at three levels: representation, training, and
deployment. (As of February 2026, the DEME V3 reference implementation
realizes many of these proposals in running code; we note the
correspondence below.)

\subsubsection{Representation}\label{representation}

\textbf{Vector-valued reward functions.} Replace scalar rewards
\(r\mathbb{\in R}\) with vector rewards \(r^{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}\),
where each component tracks a morally relevant dimension. The dimension
\(d\) need not be the full nine dimensions of the moral manifold --- a
domain-specific approximation (medical ethics might use \(d = 5\):
benefit, autonomy, justice, non-maleficence, dignity) is sufficient for
many applications.

\textbf{Tensor-valued evaluations.} For higher-fidelity applications,
maintain the full \((1,1)\)-tensor \(\mathcal{T}_{\ \nu}^{\mu}\)
encoding the obligation-interest structure. This requires \(d^{2}\)
parameters per evaluation rather than \(d\), but preserves the
relational structure that vectors discard.

\textbf{Covariance structures for uncertainty.} Replace scalar
confidence with the uncertainty tensor \(\Sigma^{\mu\nu}\) (Chapter 6,
§6.6), encoding which dimensions are uncertain, which covary, and which
are independently variable. The moral risk
\(R = I_{\mu}\Sigma^{\mu\nu}I_{\nu}\) then emerges naturally from the
contraction of the uncertainty tensor with the interest covector.

\subsubsection{Training}\label{training}

\textbf{Multi-objective learning.} Train the system to predict
tensor-valued evaluations, not scalar ratings. Multi-objective
reinforcement learning and multi-task learning provide the algorithmic
foundations.

\textbf{Invariance enforcement.} Use contrastive learning to enforce the
BIP: generate pairs of equivalent inputs (related by admissible
transformations) and penalize output differences. The loss function
includes an invariance term:

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{inv}} = \sum_{(x,\tau(x))}^{} \parallel \Sigma(x) - \Sigma(\tau(x)) \parallel^{2}\]

This directly penalizes gauge-invariance violations during training,
producing systems that satisfy the BIP by construction. The BIP v10.16
experiments (Chapter 17, §17.10) validated this approach empirically: a
LaBSE-based encoder trained with InfoNCE contrastive loss and multi-head
adversarial language removal achieved 80\% F1 on cross-lingual deontic
classification, 1.2\% residual language leakage, and a
structural-to-surface similarity ratio of 11.1\(\times\)---confirming
that invariance-enforced training produces representations where
structural moral features dominate surface linguistic features.

\textbf{Metric learning.} Learn the moral metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) from
human judgments (Chapter 9, §9.8), rather than presupposing fixed
trade-off weights. The Dear Abby corpus and similar datasets provide
training data for metric estimation: the patterns of weighting across
contexts reveal the implicit metric structure.

\subsubsection{Deployment}\label{deployment}

\textbf{Explicit contraction.} At decision time, the system performs an
explicit contraction with governance-specified weights. The contraction
method (summative, weighted, maximin, lexicographic --- Chapter 15) is a
configuration parameter, not a learned feature.

\textbf{Invariance testing.} Before deployment and continuously during
operation, test the system against transformation suites. Present
equivalent inputs in different descriptions and verify output
consistency. Flag invariance violations for human review.

\textbf{Audit logging.} Log the full tensor evaluation, the contraction
method, the scalar verdict, and the residue at each decision. This
provides the data for post-hoc review, compliance verification, and
alignment monitoring.

\textbf{Escalation.} When the system's evaluation falls outside the
robust core (Chapter 16, §16.6) --- when every available action is bad
under some plausible theory --- the system escalates to human judgment.
This is not a failure of the AI; it is the correct response to moral
uncertainty that exceeds the system's capacity to resolve (§16.8).

\subsection{18.11 Case Study: Tensorial Kidney
Allocation}\label{case-study-tensorial-kidney-allocation}

We return to the kidney allocation example of Chapter 7, now with AI
involvement, to illustrate the full architecture.

\subsubsection{The Scalar Approach}\label{the-scalar-approach-1}

A conventional AI system receives patient features and outputs an
allocation ranking. It optimizes a scalar objective --- expected
life-years gained, perhaps, or a composite quality-adjusted score. The
familiar problems arise:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The scalar discards information about \emph{why} one patient ranks
  higher
\item
  The system may learn to discriminate on protected characteristics that
  correlate with the scalar
\item
  The scalar cannot represent the distinct claims of different ethical
  considerations (medical urgency, waiting time, likelihood of success,
  family burden)
\item
  When questioned, the system can report only ``Patient A scored 0.73
  and Patient B scored 0.68'' --- a verdict without explanation
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{The Tensorial Approach}\label{the-tensorial-approach}

The geometric system maintains the full tensor evaluation
\(\mathcal{T}_{\ \nu}^{\mu}\) from Chapter 7:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0778}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1293}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1344}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1349}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1349}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1452}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Physician}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Family(A)}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Family(B)}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Family(C)}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Committee}
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Alice & 0.75 & 0.90 & 0.40 & 0.50 & 0.62 \\
Bob & 0.68 & 0.35 & 0.95 & 0.45 & 0.70 \\
Carol & 0.55 & 0.30 & 0.30 & 0.92 & 0.58 \\
\end{longtable}
}

The system performs explicit contraction according to the
governance-specified method. Three contractions yield three verdicts
(Chapter 7, §7.5):

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Utilitarian} (sum): Alice \textgreater{} Bob \textgreater{}
  Carol
\item
  \textbf{Rawlsian} (min): Alice \textgreater{} Bob \textgreater{} Carol
\item
  \textbf{Expert-weighted}: Bob \textgreater{} Alice \textgreater{}
  Carol
\end{itemize}

The system reports all three, together with the residue of each
contraction (Chapter 15, §15.12) --- what each method sacrifices.

\subsubsection{Invariance Verification}\label{invariance-verification}

The BIP test suite verifies that the system treats equivalent cases
equivalently. Present the same medical situation with: - Different
patient names (invariance under labeling) - Different narrative formats
(invariance under presentation) - Different languages (invariance under
translation)

If the system's tensor evaluation changes under any of these
transformations, a gauge-invariance violation is detected, localized to
specific dimensions, and flagged for correction.

\subsubsection{The No Escape Guarantee}\label{the-no-escape-guarantee}

If the kidney-allocation AI is structurally contained: - Its inputs are
canonicalized (patient names stripped, formats standardized) - Its
evaluation is grounded in physical observables (lab values, imaging
results, histocompatibility markers) - Every allocation decision is
audited with full tensor trace - An independent verifier checks each
audit artifact

Under these conditions, the No Escape Theorem guarantees that no
representational manipulation --- no relabeling of patients, no
reformulation of medical histories, no strategic redescription --- can
change the evaluated outcome. The allocation is determined by the
patients' medical reality, not by how that reality is described.

\subsection{18.12 Human-AI Collaboration as Tensor
Combination}\label{human-ai-collaboration-as-tensor-combination}

When humans and AI systems collaborate on moral decisions, the geometric
framework provides a principled method for combining their evaluations.

\subsubsection{Scalar Combination and Its
Limits}\label{scalar-combination-and-its-limits}

The scalar approach: average the human's scalar rating \(h\) with the
AI's scalar rating \(a\): \((h + a)/2\). This loses all information
about \emph{where} the human and AI agree and where they disagree. If
\(h = 0.6\) and \(a = 0.6\), the average is \(0.6\) --- but the human
might rate welfare at \(0.9\) and fairness at \(0.3\), while the AI
rates welfare at \(0.3\) and fairness at \(0.9\). They agree on the
scalar but disagree on everything that matters.

\subsubsection{Tensorial Combination}\label{tensorial-combination}

The geometric approach: the human provides a tensor evaluation
\(H^{\mu}\) and the AI provides a tensor evaluation \(A^{\mu}\). These
combine by weighted tensor sum:

\[C^{\mu} = \alpha H^{\mu} + (1 - \alpha)A^{\mu}\]

where \(\alpha\) may vary by dimension --- the human may be weighted
more heavily on dimensions requiring emotional judgment, the AI more
heavily on dimensions requiring computational precision.

The disagreement vector \(\Delta^{\mu} = H^{\mu} - A^{\mu}\) reveals
\emph{which dimensions} require human input (where \(|\Delta^{\mu}|\) is
large) and which can be delegated to the AI (where \(|\Delta^{\mu}|\) is
small). This enables \emph{appropriate trust}: trusting the AI on
dimensions where it is reliable, deferring to humans on dimensions where
it is not.

\subsubsection{Responsibility in Human-AI
Teams}\label{responsibility-in-human-ai-teams}

Chapter 14 developed the responsibility tensor \(R_{a}(\omega)\) for
collective agents, including the responsibility remainder \(\Delta R\)
that cannot be allocated to any individual member. In human-AI teams,
the responsibility allocation is:

\[R_{\text{human}}(\omega) + R_{\text{AI}}(\omega) + \Delta R(\omega) = 1\]

The remainder \(\Delta R\) represents the emergent moral responsibility
of the collaboration itself --- the consequences that arise from the
\emph{interaction} between human and AI judgment, attributable to
neither alone.

The geometric framework makes this remainder visible and quantifiable.
It does not resolve the philosophical question of whether AI systems can
bear moral responsibility. It provides the mathematical structure for
tracking responsibility, identifying gaps, and ensuring that the total
is accounted for --- even when some of the total is irreducibly
collective.

\subsection{18.13 Looking Forward}\label{looking-forward}

This chapter has developed three claims:

\textbf{1. Geometric ethics provides the right vocabulary for AI
ethics.} Tensor-valued objectives preserve the moral information that
scalar objectives discard. Explicit contraction makes trade-offs
visible. Invariance testing provides a concrete, implementable alignment
criterion. The invariance of harm accounting provides a gauge-invariant
measure of moral impact.

\textbf{2. Structural containment is possible.} The No Escape Theorem
proves that, under mandatory canonicalization, grounded evaluation,
audit completeness, and external verification, cognitive escape routes
are blocked --- regardless of the agent's intelligence. AI safety
reduces to governance, engineering, and physical security: hard
problems, but tractable ones.

3. The remaining obstacles are not only mathematical. Under its stated
assumptions the theorem is conditional; the open work includes
specifying the grounding map, securing sensors and the trusted computing
base, governing the choice of metrics, handling multi-agent composition,
resisting institutional capture, and empirical validation. What the
theorem removes is one specific evasion - escape by re-description - not
the broader engineering and governance problem.

The next chapter develops the engineering infrastructure --- the DEME
architecture and the ErisML modeling language --- that makes geometric
AI governance implementable at scale.

\textbf{Update (February 2026).} Since the initial formulation of these
claims, DEME V3 has provided a concrete reference implementation.
Structural containment is realized via hard veto layers (the Geneva EM)
combined with a BIP verifier that tests gauge invariance on every
evaluation. The tensor-valued objective architecture uses
\texttt{MoralTensor} objects at ranks 1--6, with explicit contraction as
a separate governance-configured step. The BIP v10.16 experiments
(Chapter 17, §17.10) demonstrate that invariance training produces
systems achieving 100\% obligation/permission transfer across
languages---the empirical standard that the No Escape Theorem's
canonicalization requirement demands. Multi-agent coordination (Nash
equilibria, correlated equilibria, Shapley value credit assignment) and
temporal dynamics with configurable discount rates extend the framework
beyond single-agent, single-timestep evaluation. The claims of this
chapter are no longer purely theoretical; they have a working
instantiation.

\subsection{Technical Appendix}\label{technical-appendix-11}

\textbf{Proposition 18.1 (Invariance Violation Decomposition).}
\emph{Let} \(\Sigma\) \emph{be an AI evaluation function, and let}
\(\mathcal{G}\) \emph{be the group of admissible re-descriptions. The
invariance violation}
\(\text{IV}(x\mathcal{,G) =}\text{Var}_{g\mathcal{\in G}}\lbrack\Sigma(g(x))\rbrack\)
\emph{decomposes as:}

\[\text{IV}(x\mathcal{,G) =}\sum_{\mu = 1}^{d}\text{Var}_{g\mathcal{\in G}}\lbrack\Sigma^{\mu}(g(x))\rbrack + \sum_{\mu \neq \nu}^{}\text{Cov}_{g\mathcal{\in G}}\lbrack\Sigma^{\mu}(g(x)),\Sigma^{\nu}(g(x))\rbrack\]

\emph{The first sum gives the dimensional violations (which dimensions
are sensitive to re-description); the second gives the cross-dimensional
violations (which pairs of dimensions covary under re-description). A
well-aligned system has both sums equal to zero.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} The evaluation function Σ = (Σ¹, \ldots, Σᵈ) is
vector-valued with d components. The total evaluation scalar is Σ\_total
= Σ\_μ Σᵘ. By the bilinearity of variance: Var{[}Σ\_total{]} =
Var{[}Σ\_μ Σᵘ{]} = Σ\_μ Var{[}Σᵘ{]} + Σ\_\{μ≠ν\} Cov{[}Σᵘ, Σᵛ{]}, where
all variances and covariances are taken over g ∈ G with x fixed. The
invariance violation IV(x, G) = Var\_\{g ∈ G\}{[}Σ(g(x)){]} equals
Var{[}Σ\_total{]} by definition. The first sum isolates the
per-dimension contributions; the second captures the cross-dimensional
interactions. A well-aligned system satisfies Σ(g(x)) = Σ(x) for all g,
so each variance and covariance term vanishes individually. □

\textbf{Proposition 18.2 (Contraction Mismatch Bound).} \emph{Let}
\(S_{\text{AI}} = w_{\mu}^{\text{AI}}V^{\mu}\) \emph{be the AI's scalar
verdict and} \(S^{*} = w_{\mu}^{*}V^{\mu*}\) \emph{the endorsed scalar
verdict. The alignment gap satisfies:}

\[|S_{\text{AI}} - S^{*}| \leq \parallel w^{\text{AI}} - w^{*} \parallel \cdot \parallel V \parallel + \parallel w^{*} \parallel \cdot \parallel V - V^{*} \parallel\]

\emph{where the first term measures contraction mismatch (wrong weights)
and the second measures representation mismatch (wrong tensor). This
decomposition enables targeted diagnosis: contraction errors are
correctable by weight adjustment; representation errors require
retraining.}

\emph{\textbf{Proof}.} Write S\_AI − S* = wᴬᴵ\_μ Vᵘ − w*\_μ V*ᵘ. Add and
subtract w*\_μ Vᵘ: S\_AI − S* = (wᴬᴵ\_μ − w*\_μ)Vᵘ + w*\_μ(Vᵘ − V*ᵘ). By
the triangle inequality: \textbar S\_AI − S*\textbar{} ≤ \textbar(wᴬᴵ −
w*)\_μ Vᵘ\textbar{} + \textbar w*\_μ(V − V*)ᵘ\textbar. By the
Cauchy--Schwarz inequality: \textbar(wᴬᴵ − w*)·V\textbar{} ≤ ‖wᴬᴵ −
w*‖·‖V‖ and \textbar w*·(V − V*)\textbar{} ≤ ‖w*‖·‖V − V*‖. Combining:
\textbar S\_AI − S*\textbar{} ≤ ‖wᴬᴵ − w*‖·‖V‖ + ‖w*‖·‖V − V*‖. □

\textbf{Proposition 18.3 (No Escape --- Conditional Guarantee).}
\emph{The structural containment guarantee of Theorem 18.1 is
conditional on:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{(\roman{enumi})}
\item
  \emph{Canonicalizer correctness:} \(C(x) = C(x')\) \emph{whenever}
  \(x \approx x'\)\emph{,} \(C\) \emph{is total, deterministic, and
  computationally bounded.}
\item
  \emph{Grounding adequacy:} \(\Psi\) \emph{captures all morally
  relevant features of the domain (axiom G4).}
\item
  \emph{Verification integrity:} \(V\) \emph{operates on physically
  separate hardware with no shared state with} \(A\)\emph{.}
\item
  \emph{Implementation correctness: the trusted computing base correctly
  implements the stated requirements.}
\end{enumerate}

\emph{If any condition fails, the theorem provides no guarantee. Each
condition is an explicit, auditable assumption --- not a hidden premise.
The safety case requires independent verification of each.}

\textbf{Proposition 18.4 (Alignment Gap Convergence).} \emph{Under
BIP-enforced training with invariance loss} \(\mathcal{L}_{\text{inv}}\)
\emph{and a representative training distribution, the expected
invariance violation decreases monotonically:}

\[\mathbb{E\lbrack}\text{IV}(x\mathcal{,G)\rbrack \rightarrow}0\text{ as }\mathcal{L}_{\text{inv}} \rightarrow 0\]

\begin{quote}
\emph{That is, minimizing the invariance loss during training drives the
system toward gauge invariance. Convergence rate depends on the
expressiveness of the model class and the coverage of the transformation
suite.}
\end{quote}

\emph{Proof.} The invariance loss is defined as L\_inv = E\_\{x
\textasciitilde{} D\}{[}Var\_\{g ∈ G\}{[}Σ(g(x)){]}{]} = E{[}IV(x,
G){]}, where D is the training distribution. Since variance is
non-negative, L\_inv ≥ 0 with equality iff Σ(g(x)) = Σ(x) for all g ∈ G
almost surely --- i.e., iff Σ is gauge-invariant. Gradient-based
training minimizes L\_inv over the model class. At each training step,
the gradient ∇\_θ L\_inv points toward reduced invariance violation (the
loss is a sum of non-negative terms, each differentiable in the model
parameters θ). By standard convergence results for gradient descent on
smooth losses: if the model class is sufficiently expressive to
represent a gauge-invariant function and the training distribution
covers the transformation suite G, then L\_inv → 0, which implies
E{[}IV(x, G){]} → 0. □

❖

\emph{The geometric framework transforms the alignment problem from an
intractable question about AI goals to a tractable question about
mathematical structure.}

\emph{The cage is not made of rules. It is made of geometry --- and
geometry does not have loopholes.}

\section{Chapter 19: The DEME Architecture and
ErisML}\label{chapter-19-the-deme-architecture-and-erisml}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{\textsc{RUNNING EXAMPLE --- Priya's Model}}

\emph{Priya sketches a DEME-compliant TrialMatch. Layer 1 (Application):
the ML model, now outputting nine-dimensional vectors. Layer 2
(Translation): an ErisML specification canonicalizing patient
situations---medical severity maps to μ = 1 (Empirical--Deontic), access
burden to μ = 3 (Empirical--Consequential), consent capacity to μ = 5
(Normative--Aretaic). Layer 3 (Evaluation): the contraction Iₘ Oᵋ
performed explicitly, every weight logged. Layer 4 (Governance): an
ethics board including patient advocates sets the metric weights,
reviewable quarterly. The Bond Index Bd = D\_op / τ measures output
drift from the governance-specified metric. Priya estimates six months
to rebuild. For the first time, every dimension will be visible.}
\end{quote}

\subsection{19.1 From Theory to
Infrastructure}\label{from-theory-to-infrastructure}

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=5.5in,height=3.79412in]{media/image16.png}
\caption{\textbar{} The DEME Architecture: Four Layers of Ethical
Governance}
\end{figure}

The four-layer DEME architecture: Governance (democratic specification
of weights and constraints), Ethics Module (tensor evaluation and
invariance testing), Canonicalizer (BIP-enforcing input normalization),
and Audit (immutable logging with Bond Index monitoring).

Chapter 18 established that geometric ethics provides the right
mathematical vocabulary for AI alignment: tensor-valued objectives,
invariance as alignment, explicit contraction, and the No Escape Theorem
for structural containment. The mathematics is sound. The question is:
how do we build it? Figure 12 presents the four-layer DEME architecture.

This chapter bridges the gap between geometric theory and engineering
practice. It presents two interconnected systems:

\textbf{ErisML} (named for the Greek goddess of discord) --- a modeling
language for specifying environments, agents, objectives, and normative
constraints in a single, machine-interpretable substrate. ErisML is the
\emph{representation layer}: the language in which geometric ethics is
expressed for computation.

\textbf{DEME} (Democratically Governed Ethics Module Engine) --- an
architecture for structured ethical governance of AI systems. DEME is
the \emph{execution layer}: the engine that evaluates actions against
geometric ethical constraints, aggregates stakeholder perspectives, and
produces auditable verdicts.

Together, they implement the geometric framework:

\begin{figure}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=5in,height=2.8147in]{media/image17.png}
\caption{\textbar{} The DEME V3 evaluation pipeline: from input
situation through canonicalization, tensor evaluation, explicit
contraction with governance weights, to audited output with invariance
verification at each stage.}
\end{figure}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3028}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4212}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\textbf{Geometric concept}
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright

\midrule\noalign{}

\endlastfoot
Moral manifold \(\mathcal{M}\) & ErisML environment specification \\
Obligation vectors \(O^{\mu}\) & Ethics Module evaluations \\
Interest covectors \(I_{\mu}\) & Stakeholder weight configurations \\
Moral metric \(g_{\mu\nu}\) & Governance profiles (trade-off
structure) \\
Contraction \(S = I_{\mu}O^{\mu}\) & Democratic aggregation \\
BIP gauge invariance & Canonicalization pipeline \\

Invariance of harm accounting & Bond Index violation detection \\
Uncertainty tensor \(\Sigma^{\mu\nu}\) & V3 per-cell uncertainty metadata + rank-6 CVaR samples \\
Temporal dynamics & V3 rank-3 temporal tensor + discount \(\gamma\) \\
Collective agency & V3 rank-5 coalition tensor + Shapley values \\
