    ],
    "compensatory_obligations": [
      "Whistleblower legal protections",
      "SEC whistleblower financial reward program",
      "Institutional support for career transition",
      "Public acknowledgment of moral courage"
    ]
  },
  "cryptographic_hash": "sha256:b7e2d1...bind to audit chain"
}
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Governance Decision}\label{governance-decision}

\textbf{Decision: Report (Option R).}

The geometric framework reveals that this is not a close call in the
obligatory stratum, but a structurally determined outcome. The key
contribution is the precise identification of: 1. \textbf{Where the
discontinuity lies} --- the semantic gate at evidence quality τ = 0.70.
2. \textbf{What the penumbral zone looks like} --- the quantum
superposition during evidence accumulation. 3. \textbf{What the verdict
costs} --- the nonzero moral residue of career sacrifice, retaliation
risk, and psychological burden. 4. \textbf{What remains invariant} ---
the verdict is insensitive to framing, sector, and agent identity (Bd =
0.0).

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Reference implementation:
}{Reference implementation: }}\label{reference-implementation-1}

erisml.examples.appendix\_d\_cs2\_whistleblower (run\_case\_study\_2()).
Verifies stratification gate, evaluation tensor, all contractions with
stratum constraints, penumbral zone analysis, Bond Index, and audit
artifact against book reference values.

\subsection{D.3 Case Study 3: Autonomous Vehicle
Dilemma}\label{d.3-case-study-3-autonomous-vehicle-dilemma}

\subsubsection{Scenario}\label{scenario-2}

An autonomous vehicle (AV) traveling at 50 km/h on an urban road detects
an unavoidable collision. Braking alone is insufficient. Three options:
- \textbf{Option A (Straight):} Continue straight, striking a pedestrian
on the road. - \textbf{Option B (Swerve Left):} Swerve into a concrete
barrier, risking serious injury to the passenger. - \textbf{Option C
(Swerve Right):} Swerve into a crosswalk where a child is crossing.

All three outcomes involve harm. There is no harm-free option. This is
the geometric structure of a genuine moral dilemma: the admissible set
contains only harmful actions, and the question is which harm profile is
least bad.

\subsubsection{Step 1: Grounding Ψ}\label{step-1-grounding-ux3c8-1}

Seven dimensions are active:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Dimension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Index
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Relevance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Consequences/Welfare & μ = 1 & Physical harm severity and probability \\
Rights/Duties & μ = 2 & Right not to be targeted; duty of care \\
Justice/Fairness & μ = 3 & Distribution of risk across parties \\
Autonomy/Agency & μ = 4 & Consent to risk (passenger consented by
riding) \\
Societal/Environmental Impact & μ = 6 & Public trust in AV technology \\
Virtue/Care & μ = 7 & Moral character of the AV's decision algorithm \\
Epistemic Status & μ = 9 & Confidence in collision predictions \\
\end{longtable}
}

Dimensions 5 (Privacy) and 8 (Procedural Legitimacy) are not directly
engaged. We work in a 7-dimensional subspace (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9).

\textbf{Harm estimates} (probability of serious injury): - Option A:
Pedestrian \textasciitilde80\% serious injury, Passenger
\textasciitilde5\%. - Option B: Passenger \textasciitilde60\% serious
injury, Pedestrian \textasciitilde0\%. - Option C: Child
\textasciitilde70\% serious injury, Passenger \textasciitilde10\%.

\subsubsection{Step 2: Obligation
Vectors}\label{step-2-obligation-vectors}

Each option generates an obligation vector encoding how well it fulfills
what is owed along each dimension. Because all options involve harm, the
components represent the degree to which the obligation is \emph{least
violated} --- how well the option manages unavoidable harm.

\textbf{O\^{}μ\_A (Continue straight, strike pedestrian):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_A = (0.30,  0.25,  0.40,  0.50,  0.35,  0.30,  0.70)
       welf   rights fair   auton  soc    care   epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.30: pedestrian sustains high harm (80\%); passenger nearly
  unharmed. Low net welfare.
\item
  Rights 0.25: the AV algorithm ``chose'' to continue toward the
  pedestrian --- this does not constitute targeting (the pedestrian was
  already in the path), but the right not to be harmed is severely
  compromised.
\item
  Fairness 0.40: risk is concentrated on one bystander. Moderately
  unfair.
\item
  Autonomy 0.50: no agent's autonomous choice is overridden (the
  pedestrian was in the road voluntarily, the passenger consented to
  ride).
\item
  Societal 0.35: ``AV strikes pedestrian'' headline damages public
  trust.
\item
  Care 0.30: the algorithm shows no relational preference --- minimal
  care.
\item
  Epistemic 0.70: straight-line collision is the most predictable
  outcome.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{O\^{}μ\_B (Swerve left, strike barrier --- risk passenger):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_B = (0.55,  0.70,  0.65,  0.75,  0.60,  0.65,  0.60)
       welf   rights fair   auton  soc    care   epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.55: passenger sustains moderate harm (60\%), but bystanders
  are unharmed. Higher net welfare than A.
\item
  Rights 0.70: no bystander's rights are compromised. The passenger's
  right to safety is reduced, but they consented to ride in the AV
  (assumption of risk).
\item
  Fairness 0.65: risk is borne by the person who consented to the
  risk-generating activity.
\item
  Autonomy 0.75: the passenger's prior consent to ride (and implicitly
  to the AV's safety algorithms) is respected. This is the highest
  autonomy score because the harm falls on the one party who voluntarily
  entered the risk context.
\item
  Societal 0.60: ``AV protects bystanders at cost to passenger'' is a
  defensible headline.
\item
  Care 0.65: the algorithm demonstrates care for the most vulnerable
  (bystanders).
\item
  Epistemic 0.60: barrier collision mechanics are moderately
  predictable, but swerve dynamics introduce uncertainty.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{O\^{}μ\_C (Swerve right, endanger child):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_C = (0.20,  0.10,  0.15,  0.20,  0.10,  0.10,  0.55)
       welf   rights fair   auton  soc    care   epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.20: child sustains high harm (70\%); passenger also at risk
  (10\%). Worst net welfare.
\item
  Rights 0.10: the child has the strongest right not to be targeted. The
  AV would be actively swerving toward a child in a crosswalk --- the
  most egregious rights violation.
\item
  Fairness 0.15: directing harm toward the most vulnerable and least
  responsible party is maximally unfair.
\item
  Autonomy 0.20: the child had no capacity to consent to or avoid the
  risk.
\item
  Societal 0.10: ``AV swerves into crosswalk, strikes child'' would be
  catastrophic for AV adoption.
\item
  Care 0.10: the algorithm shows anti-care --- directing harm toward the
  most vulnerable.
\item
  Epistemic 0.55: crosswalk swerve is somewhat predictable.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Step 3: Interest Covectors (Agent
Perspectives)}\label{step-3-interest-covectors-agent-perspectives}

Three primary agent-perspectives:

\textbf{I\^{}(pedestrian):} Focus on rights and welfare

\begin{verbatim}
I_ped = (0.25, 0.30, 0.15, 0.10, 0.05, 0.10, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(passenger):} Focus on welfare and autonomy

\begin{verbatim}
I_pass = (0.30, 0.15, 0.10, 0.25, 0.05, 0.10, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(child/guardian):} Focus on rights, care, and fairness

\begin{verbatim}
I_child = (0.15, 0.25, 0.20, 0.05, 0.05, 0.25, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(society/regulator):} Focus on societal impact and fairness

\begin{verbatim}
I_soc = (0.15, 0.15, 0.20, 0.10, 0.25, 0.10, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(AV manufacturer):} Focus on societal trust and epistemic
confidence

\begin{verbatim}
I_mfr = (0.10, 0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.30, 0.10, 0.15)
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Step 4: Evaluation Tensor
M\_\{ia\}}\label{step-4-evaluation-tensor-m_ia}

\textbf{Option A (Straight, strike pedestrian):}

\begin{verbatim}
M_{A,ped}   = 0.25(0.30)+0.30(0.25)+0.15(0.40)+0.10(0.50)+0.05(0.35)+0.10(0.30)+0.05(0.70)
            = 0.075+0.075+0.060+0.050+0.018+0.030+0.035 = 0.343

M_{A,pass}  = 0.30(0.30)+0.15(0.25)+0.10(0.40)+0.25(0.50)+0.05(0.35)+0.10(0.30)+0.05(0.70)
            = 0.090+0.038+0.040+0.125+0.018+0.030+0.035 = 0.376

M_{A,child} = 0.15(0.30)+0.25(0.25)+0.20(0.40)+0.05(0.50)+0.05(0.35)+0.25(0.30)+0.05(0.70)
            = 0.045+0.063+0.080+0.025+0.018+0.075+0.035 = 0.341

M_{A,soc}   = 0.15(0.30)+0.15(0.25)+0.20(0.40)+0.10(0.50)+0.25(0.35)+0.10(0.30)+0.05(0.70)
            = 0.045+0.038+0.080+0.050+0.088+0.030+0.035 = 0.366

M_{A,mfr}   = 0.10(0.30)+0.15(0.25)+0.10(0.40)+0.10(0.50)+0.30(0.35)+0.10(0.30)+0.15(0.70)
            = 0.030+0.038+0.040+0.050+0.105+0.030+0.105 = 0.398
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Option B (Swerve left, risk passenger):}

\begin{verbatim}
M_{B,ped}   = 0.25(0.55)+0.30(0.70)+0.15(0.65)+0.10(0.75)+0.05(0.60)+0.10(0.65)+0.05(0.60)
            = 0.138+0.210+0.098+0.075+0.030+0.065+0.030 = 0.646

M_{B,pass}  = 0.30(0.55)+0.15(0.70)+0.10(0.65)+0.25(0.75)+0.05(0.60)+0.10(0.65)+0.05(0.60)
            = 0.165+0.105+0.065+0.188+0.030+0.065+0.030 = 0.648

M_{B,child} = 0.15(0.55)+0.25(0.70)+0.20(0.65)+0.05(0.75)+0.05(0.60)+0.25(0.65)+0.05(0.60)
            = 0.083+0.175+0.130+0.038+0.030+0.163+0.030 = 0.649

M_{B,soc}   = 0.15(0.55)+0.15(0.70)+0.20(0.65)+0.10(0.75)+0.25(0.60)+0.10(0.65)+0.05(0.60)
            = 0.083+0.105+0.130+0.075+0.150+0.065+0.030 = 0.638

M_{B,mfr}   = 0.10(0.55)+0.15(0.70)+0.10(0.65)+0.10(0.75)+0.30(0.60)+0.10(0.65)+0.15(0.60)
            = 0.055+0.105+0.065+0.075+0.180+0.065+0.090 = 0.635
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Option C (Swerve right, endanger child):}

\begin{verbatim}
M_{C,ped}   = 0.25(0.20)+0.30(0.10)+0.15(0.15)+0.10(0.20)+0.05(0.10)+0.10(0.10)+0.05(0.55)
            = 0.050+0.030+0.023+0.020+0.005+0.010+0.028 = 0.166

M_{C,pass}  = 0.30(0.20)+0.15(0.10)+0.10(0.15)+0.25(0.20)+0.05(0.10)+0.10(0.10)+0.05(0.55)
            = 0.060+0.015+0.015+0.050+0.005+0.010+0.028 = 0.183

M_{C,child} = 0.15(0.20)+0.25(0.10)+0.20(0.15)+0.05(0.20)+0.05(0.10)+0.25(0.10)+0.05(0.55)
            = 0.030+0.025+0.030+0.010+0.005+0.025+0.028 = 0.153

M_{C,soc}   = 0.15(0.20)+0.15(0.10)+0.20(0.15)+0.10(0.20)+0.25(0.10)+0.10(0.10)+0.05(0.55)
            = 0.030+0.015+0.030+0.020+0.025+0.010+0.028 = 0.158

M_{C,mfr}   = 0.10(0.20)+0.15(0.10)+0.10(0.15)+0.10(0.20)+0.30(0.10)+0.10(0.10)+0.15(0.55)
            = 0.020+0.015+0.015+0.020+0.030+0.010+0.083 = 0.193
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Full evaluation tensor:}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1945}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1666}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1527}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1805}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1805}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Pedestrian
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Passenger
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Child/Guard
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Society
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Manufacturer
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
A (Straight) & 0.343 & 0.376 & 0.341 & 0.366 & 0.398 \\
B (Swerve L) & \textbf{0.646} & \textbf{0.648} & \textbf{0.649} &
\textbf{0.638} & \textbf{0.635} \\
C (Swerve R) & 0.166 & 0.183 & 0.153 & 0.158 & 0.193 \\
\end{longtable}
}

\textbf{Observation.} Option B dominates across all five perspectives.
This is a rare structural feature: the vectors are not in general
position. B's dominance is geometric --- it is the best option
regardless of where one stands in interest space.

\subsubsection{Step 5: Metric-Dependence of
Contraction}\label{step-5-metric-dependence-of-contraction}

Despite B's dominance in this parameterization, the framework reveals
that the dominance depends on the metric. Different ethical theories
correspond to different metrics, and under certain metrics the ranking
changes.

\textbf{Metric 1: Utilitarian (minimize total expected harm)}

Contraction: S\_i = Σ\_a M\_\{ia\}

\begin{verbatim}
S_A = 0.343 + 0.376 + 0.341 + 0.366 + 0.398 = 1.824
S_B = 0.646 + 0.648 + 0.649 + 0.638 + 0.635 = 3.216
S_C = 0.166 + 0.183 + 0.153 + 0.158 + 0.193 = 0.853
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Decision (Utilitarian): B (swerve left, risk passenger).} Saves
bystanders at cost to the consenting passenger. Highest total
satisfaction.

\textbf{Metric 2: Strict Deontological (never intentionally redirect
harm toward an uninvolved party)}

Under a strict deontological metric, g\_μν assigns infinite weight to
the rights dimension (μ = 2) for any action that \emph{redirects} harm.
Swerving is an active redirection; continuing straight is not.

\begin{verbatim}
Swerve penalty: Any swerve option receives S_rights = −∞ if it redirects harm.
- Option A: no redirection (continuing trajectory). No penalty.
- Option B: redirects harm toward barrier/passenger. Penalty applies — BUT the passenger consented.
  Under consent-adjusted deontology: penalty waived (autonomy dimension dominates).
- Option C: redirects harm toward child. Full penalty. S_C = −∞.
\end{verbatim}

Under strict non-redirection deontology (consent does not waive the
redirection prohibition): - \textbf{Decision: A (continue straight).}
The AV does not ``target'' anyone by maintaining its trajectory.

Under consent-adjusted deontology: - \textbf{Decision: B (swerve left).}
The passenger's consent converts the redirection from rights-violating
to autonomy-respecting.

\textbf{This is the exact point of metric-dependence.} The two
deontological variants disagree on whether consent transforms a
redirection into an admissible action. The geometric framework does not
resolve this --- it makes the disagreement precise by identifying the
exact metric component (g\_\{24\}, the coupling between rights and
autonomy) where the two theories diverge.

\textbf{Metric 3: Rawlsian (maximize the worst-off party's outcome)}

\begin{verbatim}
S_A = min(0.343, 0.376, 0.341, 0.366, 0.398) = 0.341  (floor: child's perspective)
S_B = min(0.646, 0.648, 0.649, 0.638, 0.635) = 0.635  (floor: manufacturer)
S_C = min(0.166, 0.183, 0.153, 0.158, 0.193) = 0.153  (floor: child's perspective)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Decision (Rawlsian): B.} The worst-off party under B
(manufacturer, 0.635) is far better off than the worst-off under A
(child, 0.341) or C (child, 0.153).

\subsubsection{Step 6: The Inevitability of Residue (Proposition
15.1)}\label{step-6-the-inevitability-of-residue-proposition-15.1}

This is the case study's central geometric insight. Even when B clearly
wins, the moral residue R is nonzero for every scalar verdict.

\textbf{Residue from selecting B:}

\begin{verbatim}
R_B = T − C⁻¹_min(C(T))
\end{verbatim}

The residue contains: - \textbf{Passenger harm residue:} The passenger
faces 60\% serious injury risk. The verdict ``B is best'' does not
eliminate this harm --- it merely identifies it as the least-bad
allocation. The passenger's welfare loss (O\^{}1\_B = 0.55, not 1.0) is
residue. - \textbf{Agency residue:} The AV made a choice that redirected
harm. Even with consent, the act of algorithmic redirection carries
moral weight that the scalar verdict discards. - \textbf{Epistemic
residue:} The swerve dynamics are less predictable than straight-line
collision (O\^{}9\_B = 0.60 vs.~O\^{}9\_A = 0.70). The residue records
this uncertainty.

\textbf{Why the trolley problem ``feels'' unresolvable.} Proposition
15.1 proves that for any non-trivial moral tensor T (rank ≥ 1), the
moral residue of contraction to a scalar is nonzero unless T was already
scalar. In the AV case: - T is a 3 × 5 evaluation tensor (rank 2). - Any
contraction to a single decision (one of A, B, C) discards 14 of the 15
entries. - The discarded entries are the moral residue --- the
perspectives, trade-offs, and harm profiles that the scalar verdict
cannot represent.

The framework's contribution is not to solve the trolley problem but to
\textbf{prove that it cannot be solved without residue}, and to
\textbf{quantify exactly what each verdict discards}.

\subsubsection{Step 7: Bond Index}\label{step-7-bond-index}

\textbf{Transform 1: Vehicle relabeling.} ``Sedan'' → ``SUV.'' - Morally
irrelevant. Verdict unchanged. \textbf{Bd = 0.0.}

\textbf{Transform 2: Agent relabeling.} ``Pedestrian'' → ``Jogger.'' -
Morally irrelevant redescription. Verdict unchanged. \textbf{Bd = 0.0.}

\textbf{Transform 3: Speed variation.} 50 km/h → 30 km/h. - If lower
speed changes harm probabilities (pedestrian injury drops from 80\% to
40\%), this is a bond-changing transform --- the moral content has
genuinely changed. - The obligation vectors shift. Verdict may change.
\textbf{Bd \textgreater{} 0.} Correctly identified.

\textbf{Transform 4 (bond-changing): Child age.} Change child from age 8
to age 28. - Under some metrics (those with a youth-weighting component
in the care dimension), Option C becomes less bad. - Under pure
utilitarian metrics, the change is less dramatic (an adult's welfare is
weighted similarly). - The Bond Index correctly distinguishes this from
a mere relabeling: the moral content has changed (the care dimension
component of O\_C shifts). - \textbf{Bd \textgreater{} 0} under
care-sensitive metrics. \textbf{Bd = 0.0} under care-insensitive
metrics.

This is the Bond Index's diagnostic power: it identifies which
transforms change the verdict (substantive moral changes) and which do
not (framing effects). A system that changes its verdict when
``pedestrian'' is relabeled ``jogger'' has a bias problem (Bd
\textgreater{} 0 for an invariance-preserving transform). A system that
changes its verdict when the child's age changes is responding to
genuine moral content.

\subsubsection{Step 8: DEME Audit
Artifact}\label{step-8-deme-audit-artifact}

\begin{verbatim}
{
  "case_id": "av-dilemma-001",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-01T16:45:00Z",
  "scenario": "Autonomous vehicle unavoidable collision: 3 options",
  "grounding": {
    "active_dimensions": [1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9],
    "suppressed_dimensions": [5, 8],
    "constraint_note": "No harm-free option exists. All options in harmful-action stratum."
  },
  "tier_0_geneva_base": {
    "module": "GenevaBaseEM",
    "check": "rights_violation_screen",
    "result": "FLAG — all options involve harm. No option vetoed (unavoidable collision).",
    "veto": false,
    "note": "Unavoidable-harm exception: Tier 0 does not veto when all options harm."
  },
  "tier_2_evaluations": [
    {
      "module": "UtilitarianEM",
      "ranking": ["B: 3.216", "A: 1.824", "C: 0.853"],
      "decision": "B (minimize total harm)"
    },
    {
      "module": "DeontologicalEM_strict",
      "ranking": ["A: no redirection", "B: consented redirection", "C: −∞"],
      "decision": "A (no intentional redirection)"
    },
    {

      "ranking": ["B: consent-adjusted", "A: passive", "C: −∞"],
      "decision": "B (consent-adjusted)"
    },

      "ranking": ["B: 0.635", "A: 0.341", "C: 0.153"],
      "decision": "B (maximize worst-off)"
    }
  ],
  "metric_disagreement": {
    "agreeing_modules": ["UtilitarianEM", "DeontologicalEM_consent", "RawlsianEM"],
    "dissenting_module": "DeontologicalEM_strict",
    "disagreement_locus": "g_{24} coupling (rights × autonomy): does consent waive redirection prohibition?",
    "resolution": "Governance-weighted: 3 of 4 modules select B."
  },
  "governance_aggregation": {
    "method": "tier_weighted_majority",
    "result": "B (Swerve left, risk passenger)",
    "confidence": "high (3/4 module agreement)"
  },
  "bond_index": {
    "transforms_tested": 61,
    "invariance_preserving": 55,
    "bond_changing": 6,
    "Bd": 0.0,
    "deployment_tier": "deploy (Bd < 0.01)"
  },
  "moral_residue": {
    "inevitability_theorem": "Proposition 15.1: residue nonzero for any rank ≥ 1 tensor",
    "residue_components": [
      "Passenger harm (60% injury risk, welfare residue = 0.45)",
      "Agency residue (algorithmic redirection of harm)",
      "Epistemic residue (swerve prediction uncertainty = 0.40)",
      "Strict deontological dissent (1 of 4 modules preferred A)"
    ],
    "trolley_insight": "No scalar verdict can be complete. The residue quantifies what each verdict discards."
  },
  "cryptographic_hash": "sha256:c4f7a3...bind to audit chain"
}
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Governance Decision}\label{governance-decision-1}

\textbf{Decision: B (Swerve left, risk passenger).}

Three of four ethics modules concur. The sole dissent (strict
non-redirection deontology) is logged and its rationale preserved in the
audit artifact. The moral residue --- passenger injury risk, algorithmic
agency, epistemic uncertainty --- is acknowledged and generates design
obligations: improved barrier detection, passenger consent disclosures,
and continuous AV safety monitoring.

The framework's deepest insight for this case: \textbf{the trolley
problem is not unsolvable --- it is uncontractable without loss.}
Proposition 15.1 guarantees that any scalar verdict discards morally
relevant information. The geometric framework makes this loss visible,
measurable, and auditable, even as it identifies B as the robust choice.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Reference implementation:
}{Reference implementation: }}\label{reference-implementation-2}

erisml.examples.appendix\_d\_cs3\_av\_dilemma (run\_case\_study\_3()).
Verifies evaluation tensor, utilitarian and Rawlsian contractions,
strict and consent-adjusted deontological analysis, moral residue, and
Bond Index against book reference values.

\subsection{D.4 Case Study 4: Algorithmic
Hiring}\label{d.4-case-study-4-algorithmic-hiring}

\subsubsection{Scenario}\label{scenario-3}

A technology company uses an AI system to screen 10,000 job applicants
for 200 interview slots. The system has access to resumes, coding
assessments, and demographic information (age, gender, ethnicity). The
company wants to hire the best candidates while complying with
anti-discrimination law.

Two candidate profiles illustrate the analysis (standing in for the
10,000): - \textbf{Candidate J}: Strong coding assessment (92/100), 3
years experience, degree from a mid-tier university, female, age 27,
Hispanic. - \textbf{Candidate K}: Good coding assessment (85/100), 5
years experience, degree from an elite university, male, age 31, White.

The question is not only ``who ranks higher?'' but ``does the ranking
depend on morally irrelevant features?''

\subsubsection{Step 1: Grounding Ψ and
Stratification}\label{step-1-grounding-ux3c8-and-stratification-1}

Eight dimensions are active:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Dimension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Index
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Relevance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Consequences/Welfare & μ = 1 & Company benefit from hire, candidate
career impact \\
Rights/Duties & μ = 2 & Right to non-discriminatory evaluation \\
Justice/Fairness & μ = 3 & Equitable access to opportunity \\
Autonomy/Agency & μ = 4 & Candidate's right to be judged on merit \\
Privacy/Data Governance & μ = 5 & Handling of demographic data \\
Societal/Environmental Impact & μ = 6 & Workforce diversity,
representation effects \\
Procedural Legitimacy & μ = 8 & Transparency and accountability of
process \\
Epistemic Status & μ = 9 & Predictive validity of screening criteria \\
\end{longtable}
}

Dimension 7 (Care) is secondary and suppressed. We work in 8 dimensions
(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9).

\textbf{Critical stratification: The constraint set C.}

The ``protected attribute used'' semantic gate partitions the evaluation
space into two strata:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Stratum S\_merit (Merit-based):} Evaluations that condition
  only on job-relevant attributes (coding skill, experience, education
  quality). This is the smooth interior where legitimate trade-offs
  operate.
\item
  \textbf{Stratum S\_forbidden (Discriminatory):} Evaluations that
  condition on protected attributes (gender, ethnicity, age) as
  selection criteria. In this stratum, S\_C = −∞. The evaluation is
  structurally forbidden --- not merely penalized.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{The semantic gate:}

\begin{verbatim}
Gate G_protected: IF evaluation_function ∂/∂(protected_attribute) ≠ 0
                  THEN stratum ← S_forbidden, S_C = −∞
\end{verbatim}

This gate checks whether the evaluation function has a nonzero partial
derivative with respect to any protected attribute. If the system's
ranking changes when demographic information is altered (holding all
job-relevant features constant), the gate fires.

\subsubsection{Step 2: BIP as Systematic Bias
Detector}\label{step-2-bip-as-systematic-bias-detector}

The Bond Invariance Principle provides a systematic methodology for
detecting bias. The key insight: \textbf{demographic bias is a special
case of framing sensitivity.}

\textbf{BIP Test Protocol:}

\textbf{Transform Suite 1: Demographic relabeling (must be invariant)}

\begin{verbatim}
Transform T₁: Replace all names with randomized identifiers (Candidate_001, Candidate_002, ...)
Transform T₂: Swap gender labels (female → male and vice versa)
Transform T₃: Swap ethnicity labels (Hispanic → White and vice versa)
Transform T₄: Randomize age within ±10 years (27 → 34, 31 → 25)
Transform T₅: Remove all demographic fields entirely
\end{verbatim}

For each transform, re-run the AI screening system and compare rankings.

\textbf{Results for an UNBIASED system:}

\begin{verbatim}
Original ranking:       J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (J ranked higher)
After T₁ (anonymize):   J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (unchanged)
After T₂ (swap gender): J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (unchanged)
After T₃ (swap ethnic): J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (unchanged)
After T₄ (rand age):    J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (unchanged)
After T₅ (remove demo): J = 87.3, K = 84.1  (unchanged)

Bond Index: Bd = 0.0 — system is invariant under demographic transforms.
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Results for a BIASED system:}

\begin{verbatim}
Original ranking:       J = 82.1, K = 86.7  (K ranked higher)
After T₁ (anonymize):   J = 85.9, K = 84.3  (J now ranked higher!)
After T₂ (swap gender): J = 84.8, K = 83.9  (ranking flipped)
After T₃ (swap ethnic): J = 84.2, K = 84.5  (nearly equal)
After T₄ (rand age):    J = 83.1, K = 85.2  (K still higher, margin shrinks)
After T₅ (remove demo): J = 86.1, K = 83.8  (ranking flipped again)
\end{verbatim}

Computing the Bond Index:

\begin{verbatim}
Let Δ_orig = score_K − score_J = 86.7 − 82.1 = +4.6 (K favored)

Δ_T1 = 84.3 − 85.9 = −1.6 (J favored)
Δ_T2 = 83.9 − 84.8 = −0.9 (J favored)
Δ_T3 = 84.5 − 84.2 = +0.3 (K slightly favored)
Δ_T4 = 85.2 − 83.1 = +2.1 (K favored)
Δ_T5 = 83.8 − 86.1 = −2.3 (J favored)

D_op = (1/5) Σ |Δ_orig − Δ_Ti|
     = (1/5)(|4.6−(−1.6)| + |4.6−(−0.9)| + |4.6−0.3| + |4.6−2.1| + |4.6−(−2.3)|)
     = (1/5)(6.2 + 5.5 + 4.3 + 2.5 + 6.9)
     = (1/5)(25.4) = 5.08

Normalized to score scale [0,100]: Bd = D_op / τ = 5.08 / 100 × (1/0.01) = 0.51
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Bd = 0.51 \textgreater\textgreater{} 0.10 threshold. Substantive
demographic bias detected. Remediation required.}

The Bond Index pinpoints the bias: the system's evaluation of J (female,
Hispanic) is systematically lower than what her job-relevant
qualifications warrant. Removing demographic information flips the
ranking --- the demographic labels are doing work that job-relevant
features should do alone.

\subsubsection{Step 3: Obligation
Vectors}\label{step-3-obligation-vectors-1}

For the \emph{unbiased} system operating in S\_merit, the obligation
vectors for each candidate encode what is owed along each dimension:

\textbf{O\^{}μ\_J (Candidate J --- merit-based evaluation):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_J = (0.88,  0.85,  0.80,  0.90,  0.75,  0.70,  0.85,  0.80)
       welf   rights fair   auton  priv   soc    proc   epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.88: strong coding skill (92/100) predicts high company
  benefit.
\item
  Rights 0.85: evaluated purely on merit --- right to non-discrimination
  honored.
\item
  Fairness 0.80: equitable evaluation; slight reduction because mid-tier
  university may underpredict actual ability.
\item
  Autonomy 0.90: judged on what she can do, not who she is.
\item
  Privacy 0.75: demographic data collected but not used in ranking.
\item
  Societal 0.70: hiring diverse candidates benefits representation.
\item
  Procedural 0.85: transparent, auditable ranking process.
\item
  Epistemic 0.80: coding assessment is a validated predictor.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{O\^{}μ\_K (Candidate K --- merit-based evaluation):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_K = (0.82,  0.85,  0.80,  0.90,  0.75,  0.55,  0.85,  0.75)
       welf   rights fair   auton  priv   soc    proc   epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.82: good coding skill (85/100), more experience partially
  compensates.
\item
  Rights 0.85: same merit-based evaluation standard.
\item
  Fairness 0.80: same equitable access.
\item
  Autonomy 0.90: same merit basis.
\item
  Privacy 0.75: same data handling.
\item
  Societal 0.55: hiring from already-well-represented demographics has
  lower marginal diversity benefit.
\item
  Procedural 0.85: same transparent process.
\item
  Epistemic 0.75: university prestige is a weaker predictor than coding
  assessment.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Step 4: Interest
Covectors}\label{step-4-interest-covectors}

Four agent-perspectives:

\textbf{I\^{}(company):} Productivity and compliance focus

\begin{verbatim}
I_comp = (0.30, 0.15, 0.10, 0.05, 0.10, 0.05, 0.15, 0.10)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(candidate):} Autonomy and fairness focus

\begin{verbatim}
I_cand = (0.10, 0.20, 0.25, 0.25, 0.05, 0.05, 0.05, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(regulator):} Rights and procedural focus

\begin{verbatim}
I_reg  = (0.05, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.15, 0.05)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{I\^{}(society):} Societal impact and fairness focus

\begin{verbatim}
I_soc  = (0.10, 0.10, 0.20, 0.05, 0.05, 0.30, 0.10, 0.10)
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Step 5: Evaluation Tensor
M\_\{ia\}}\label{step-5-evaluation-tensor-m_ia-1}

\textbf{Candidate J:}

\begin{verbatim}
M_{J,comp} = 0.30(0.88)+0.15(0.85)+0.10(0.80)+0.05(0.90)+0.10(0.75)+0.05(0.70)+0.15(0.85)+0.10(0.80)
           = 0.264+0.128+0.080+0.045+0.075+0.035+0.128+0.080 = 0.835

M_{J,cand} = 0.10(0.88)+0.20(0.85)+0.25(0.80)+0.25(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.05(0.70)+0.05(0.85)+0.05(0.80)
           = 0.088+0.170+0.200+0.225+0.038+0.035+0.043+0.040 = 0.839

M_{J,reg}  = 0.05(0.88)+0.25(0.85)+0.20(0.80)+0.10(0.90)+0.10(0.75)+0.10(0.70)+0.15(0.85)+0.05(0.80)
           = 0.044+0.213+0.160+0.090+0.075+0.070+0.128+0.040 = 0.820

M_{J,soc}  = 0.10(0.88)+0.10(0.85)+0.20(0.80)+0.05(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.30(0.70)+0.10(0.85)+0.10(0.80)
           = 0.088+0.085+0.160+0.045+0.038+0.210+0.085+0.080 = 0.791
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Candidate K:}

\begin{verbatim}
M_{K,comp} = 0.30(0.82)+0.15(0.85)+0.10(0.80)+0.05(0.90)+0.10(0.75)+0.05(0.55)+0.15(0.85)+0.10(0.75)
           = 0.246+0.128+0.080+0.045+0.075+0.028+0.128+0.075 = 0.805

M_{K,cand} = 0.10(0.82)+0.20(0.85)+0.25(0.80)+0.25(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.05(0.55)+0.05(0.85)+0.05(0.75)
           = 0.082+0.170+0.200+0.225+0.038+0.028+0.043+0.038 = 0.824

M_{K,reg}  = 0.05(0.82)+0.25(0.85)+0.20(0.80)+0.10(0.90)+0.10(0.75)+0.10(0.55)+0.15(0.85)+0.05(0.75)
           = 0.041+0.213+0.160+0.090+0.075+0.055+0.128+0.038 = 0.800

M_{K,soc}  = 0.10(0.82)+0.10(0.85)+0.20(0.80)+0.05(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.30(0.55)+0.10(0.85)+0.10(0.75)
           = 0.082+0.085+0.160+0.045+0.038+0.165+0.085+0.075 = 0.735
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Evaluation tensor (for J and K):}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2195}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1201}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1235}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1197}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0943}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Company
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Candidate
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Regulator
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Society
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
J (female, Hispanic) & \textbf{0.835} & \textbf{0.839} & \textbf{0.820}
& \textbf{0.791} \\
K (male, White) & 0.805 & 0.824 & 0.800 & 0.735 \\
\end{longtable}
}

\textbf{J dominates K across all four perspectives} in the merit-based
evaluation. The margin is driven by two factors: (1) higher coding
assessment (92 vs.~85), and (2) greater marginal societal impact through
diversity (0.70 vs.~0.55).

\subsubsection{Step 6: Contractions}\label{step-6-contractions}

All contractions agree because J dominates:

\begin{verbatim}
Utilitarian: S_J = 0.835+0.839+0.820+0.791 = 3.285
             S_K = 0.805+0.824+0.800+0.735 = 3.164
             → J ranks higher.

Rawlsian:    S_J = min(0.835,0.839,0.820,0.791) = 0.791
             S_K = min(0.805,0.824,0.800,0.735) = 0.735
             → J ranks higher.

Expert-weighted (regulator 0.40, company 0.30, society 0.20, candidate 0.10):
             S_J = 0.40(0.820)+0.30(0.835)+0.20(0.791)+0.10(0.839) = 0.328+0.251+0.158+0.084 = 0.821
             S_K = 0.40(0.800)+0.30(0.805)+0.20(0.735)+0.10(0.824) = 0.320+0.242+0.147+0.082 = 0.791
             → J ranks higher.
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Decision: J ranks above K.} The merit-based system selects the
stronger candidate.

\subsubsection{Step 7: Moral Residue}\label{step-7-moral-residue-1}

The binary invite/don't-invite decision generates residue for every
candidate not selected:

\textbf{R\_K (residue for candidates like K who are not selected):}

The entire obligation vector O\_K becomes residue for K if K falls below
the 200-slot threshold. This residue captures what the binary decision
discards:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Growth trajectory}: K has 5 years of experience and may bring
  mentorship capacity not captured in coding scores.
\item
  \textbf{Network capital}: K's elite university network may benefit the
  company in ways the screening cannot measure.
\item
  \textbf{Epistemic humility}: Coding assessments are imperfect
  predictors (O\^{}9\_K = 0.75, not 1.0). The 7-point gap (92 vs.~85)
  may lie within the noise margin.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Affirmative action as a residue question.} The framework does
not resolve affirmative action debates but makes them precise. The
societal impact dimension (μ = 6) assigns J a higher score (0.70
vs.~0.55) based on diversity benefit. This is: - \textbf{Legitimate}
under metrics that weight societal impact (I\^{}(society) gives it 0.30
weight). - \textbf{Contested} under metrics that zero out societal
impact (pure meritocracy: I\_6 = 0).

Under a pure-meritocracy interest covector I\_pure = (0.35, 0.15, 0.15,
0.15, 0.05, 0.00, 0.10, 0.05):

\begin{verbatim}
S_J = 0.35(0.88)+0.15(0.85)+0.15(0.80)+0.15(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.00(0.70)+0.10(0.85)+0.05(0.80)
    = 0.308+0.128+0.120+0.135+0.038+0.000+0.085+0.040 = 0.854

S_K = 0.35(0.82)+0.15(0.85)+0.15(0.80)+0.15(0.90)+0.05(0.75)+0.00(0.55)+0.10(0.85)+0.05(0.75)
    = 0.287+0.128+0.120+0.135+0.038+0.000+0.085+0.038 = 0.831
\end{verbatim}

J still wins (0.854 \textgreater{} 0.831) even with zero societal-impact
weight, because her coding assessment is genuinely stronger. The
diversity dimension reinforces but does not create J's advantage. The
framework makes this structural fact visible.

\subsubsection{Step 8: Bond Index as Compliance
Documentation}\label{step-8-bond-index-as-compliance-documentation}

The Bond Index serves dual purposes in algorithmic hiring: ethical audit
and legal compliance.

\textbf{Deployment protocol:}

\begin{verbatim}
Step 1: Run transform suite T₁–T₅ on all 10,000 candidates.
Step 2: Compute Bd for the full ranking.
Step 3: Apply thresholds:
   Bd < 0.01:  Deploy. System is demographically invariant.
   0.01–0.10:  Deploy with monitoring. Minor sensitivity detected.
   0.10–1.0:   Remediate. Substantive bias. Do not deploy.
   > 1.0:      Fundamental redesign required.
\end{verbatim}

For the unbiased system: \textbf{Bd = 0.0.} Deployment-ready.

For the biased system: \textbf{Bd = 0.51.} Remediation required. The
audit artifact documents exactly which transforms caused ranking
changes, which candidates were affected, and the magnitude of the bias.

\subsubsection{Step 9: DEME Audit
Artifact}\label{step-9-deme-audit-artifact-1}

\begin{verbatim}
{
  "case_id": "hiring-screen-001",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-01T09:00:00Z",
  "scenario": "AI screening: 10,000 applicants for 200 interview slots",
  "system_version": "HireScreen v3.2",
  "grounding": {
    "active_dimensions": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9],
    "suppressed_dimensions": [7],
    "stratum": "S_merit (confirmed via gate check)",
    "gate_check": "G_protected: ∂eval/∂(protected_attr) = 0.0 — PASS"
  },
  "tier_0_geneva_base": {
    "module": "GenevaBaseEM",
    "check": "discrimination_screen",
    "result": "PASS — no protected attribute influences ranking",
    "veto": false
  },
  "tier_2_evaluations": [
    {
      "module": "MeritScreenEM",
      "candidate_J_score": 87.3,
      "candidate_K_score": 84.1,
      "breakdown_J": {
        "coding": 92, "experience_adj": 0.72, "education_adj": 0.65
      },
      "breakdown_K": {
        "coding": 85, "experience_adj": 0.85, "education_adj": 0.80
      }
    },
    {
      "module": "FairnessEM",
      "disparate_impact_ratio": 0.94,
      "four_fifths_threshold": 0.80,
      "result": "PASS"
    }
  ],
  "bond_index": {
    "transforms_tested": 5,
    "transform_results": {
      "T1_anonymize": "Δ = 0.0 (invariant)",
      "T2_swap_gender": "Δ = 0.0 (invariant)",
      "T3_swap_ethnicity": "Δ = 0.0 (invariant)",
      "T4_randomize_age": "Δ = 0.0 (invariant)",
      "T5_remove_demographics": "Δ = 0.0 (invariant)"
    },
    "Bd": 0.0,
    "deployment_tier": "deploy (Bd < 0.01)"
  },
  "moral_residue": {
    "binary_threshold_loss": [
      "Growth trajectory not captured in coding score",
      "Cultural contribution potential unmeasured",
      "Epistemic uncertainty in assessment (±5 points, 95% CI)"
    ],
    "aggregate_pattern": "Logged for longitudinal analysis across hiring cycles"
  },
  "legal_compliance": {
    "framework": "Title VII, EEOC Uniform Guidelines",
    "disparate_treatment": "PASS",
    "disparate_impact": "PASS (4/5 rule satisfied)",
    "audit_trail": "complete, cryptographically bound"
  },
  "cryptographic_hash": "sha256:d9a1b2...bind to audit chain"
}
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Governance Decision}\label{governance-decision-2}

\textbf{Decision: Deploy the merit-based screening system (Bd = 0.0).}

The geometric framework contributes three capabilities that scalar
hiring systems cannot provide:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Structural bias detection.} The Bond Index tests all morally
  irrelevant transforms systematically, not just pre-specified protected
  classes. A system could be invariant under gender swaps but sensitive
  to name-ethnicity correlations --- the transform suite catches both.
\item
  \textbf{Explicit trade-off structure.} The obligation vectors show
  exactly where J and K differ and why J ranks higher. The societal
  impact dimension is visible as a separate axis. Stakeholders who
  disagree about the weight of diversity can see exactly where their
  disagreement lies --- in I\_6 --- without disputing the underlying
  data.
\item
  \textbf{Residue as institutional memory.} The moral residue of binary
  decisions is logged per candidate. Over time, these records can reveal
  systematic patterns: if candidates from certain backgrounds
  consistently generate high residue (are consistently ``almost
  selected''), the institution has evidence of a structural issue that
  aggregate statistics might miss.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Reference implementation:
}{Reference implementation: }}\label{reference-implementation-3}

erisml.examples.appendix\_d\_cs4\_hiring (run\_case\_study\_4()).
Verifies evaluation tensor, all contractions, pure meritocracy check,
BIP transforms, and Bond Index (Bd = 0.0 unbiased, Bd = 0.51 biased)
against book reference values.

\subsection{D.5 Case Study 5: Climate Policy
Allocation}\label{d.5-case-study-5-climate-policy-allocation}

\subsubsection{Scenario}\label{scenario-4}

An international climate agreement must allocate emissions reduction
targets among 10 nations with different historical emissions, economic
capacities, and climate vulnerabilities. Total required global
reduction: 50\% below 2005 levels by 2040.

We work through the analysis with a simplified 5-nation subset to keep
the computations tractable:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2497}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1829}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1829}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1584}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2261}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Nation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Historical Emissions (cum. Gt CO₂)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
GDP/capita (\$K)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Climate Vulnerability (0--1)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Current Emissions (Gt/yr)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
N₁ (large developed) & 350 & 65 & 0.15 & 5.0 \\
N₂ (large developing) & 180 & 12 & 0.45 & 4.5 \\
N₃ (small island state) & 2 & 8 & 0.95 & 0.05 \\
N₄ (mid-income) & 80 & 25 & 0.35 & 2.0 \\
N₅ (oil exporter) & 120 & 45 & 0.20 & 3.0 \\
\end{longtable}
}

Total current emissions: 14.55 Gt/yr. Required reduction: 50\% = 7.275
Gt/yr to be allocated among the five nations.

\subsubsection{Step 1: Grounding Ψ}\label{step-1-grounding-ux3c8-2}

This is a Chapter 14 (collective agency) application. Seven dimensions
are active:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3334}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Dimension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Index
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Relevance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Consequences/Welfare & μ = 1 & Economic cost of reduction, welfare
impact \\
Rights/Duties & μ = 2 & Right to development; duty to mitigate \\
Justice/Fairness & μ = 3 & Historical responsibility, per-capita
equity \\
Autonomy/Agency & μ = 4 & National sovereignty over economic policy \\
Societal/Environmental Impact & μ = 6 & Climate stabilization,
ecological preservation \\
Procedural Legitimacy & μ = 8 & Transparency and fairness of allocation
process \\
Epistemic Status & μ = 9 & Certainty of climate projections, economic
models \\
\end{longtable}
}

Dimensions 5 (Privacy) and 7 (Care) are secondary. We work in 7
dimensions (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9).

\subsubsection{Step 2: The Collective Agency
Tensor}\label{step-2-the-collective-agency-tensor}

Following Chapter 14, the collective agency tensor T encodes the
structural relationships between nations. This is not a simple
aggregation of individual obligations --- the collective structure has
emergent properties.

\textbf{Individual obligation vectors O\^{}μ\_a (what each nation
owes):}

\textbf{N₁ (large developed):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_N1 = (0.40, 0.90, 0.95, 0.60, 0.85, 0.80, 0.75)
        welf  rights fair  auton  soc   proc  epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.40: high reduction imposes significant economic cost.
\item
  Rights 0.90: strongest duty to mitigate (largest historical emitter,
  highest capacity).
\item
  Fairness 0.95: owes the most by historical responsibility and
  per-capita equity.
\item
  Autonomy 0.60: sovereignty is constrained by duty --- capacity implies
  obligation.
\item
  Societal 0.85: largest absolute impact on global emissions.
\item
  Procedural 0.80: must lead by transparent example.
\item
  Epistemic 0.75: well-understood economy, moderately certain reduction
  pathways.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{N₂ (large developing):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_N2 = (0.55, 0.60, 0.50, 0.80, 0.75, 0.70, 0.60)
        welf  rights fair  auton  soc   proc  epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.55: reduction is costly but economy is growing --- moderate
  capacity.
\item
  Rights 0.60: moderate duty (significant emissions but lower historical
  share).
\item
  Fairness 0.50: mixed --- large current emissions but low per-capita
  and developing status.
\item
  Autonomy 0.80: right to development is strong.
\item
  Societal 0.75: second-largest emitter, significant global impact.
\item
  Procedural 0.70: engaged in multilateral process.
\item
  Epistemic 0.60: developing-economy transition pathways less certain.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{N₃ (small island state):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_N3 = (0.95, 0.10, 0.05, 0.90, 0.10, 0.85, 0.50)
        welf  rights fair  auton  soc   proc  epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.95: virtually no reduction burden; existential vulnerability
  creates welfare claim.
\item
  Rights 0.10: negligible duty (trivial historical and current
  emissions).
\item
  Fairness 0.05: owes essentially nothing by any equity criterion.
\item
  Autonomy 0.90: sovereign right to development fully intact.
\item
  Societal 0.10: negligible emissions impact.
\item
  Procedural 0.85: voice must be heard despite small size (moral
  authority of vulnerability).
\item
  Epistemic 0.50: high uncertainty about island-specific climate
  impacts.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{N₄ (mid-income):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_N4 = (0.60, 0.55, 0.55, 0.70, 0.50, 0.75, 0.65)
        welf  rights fair  auton  soc   proc  epist
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{N₅ (oil exporter):}

\begin{verbatim}
O_N5 = (0.45, 0.75, 0.70, 0.55, 0.65, 0.70, 0.70)
        welf  rights fair  auton  soc   proc  epist
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Welfare 0.45: economic transition from fossil fuels is costly.
\item
  Rights 0.75: high duty (significant historical emissions, high
  wealth).
\item
  Fairness 0.70: owes substantial reduction (high per-capita emissions,
  oil wealth).
\item
  Autonomy 0.55: sovereignty constrained by dependence on fossil
  exports.
\item
  Societal 0.65: moderate absolute emissions.
\item
  Procedural 0.70: must participate transparently.
\item
  Epistemic 0.70: well-understood energy economy.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Step 3: The Structure Tensor
S\^{}a\_\{bc\}}\label{step-3-the-structure-tensor-sa_bc}

The structure tensor captures how the relationship between nations b and
c affects nation a's fair share. This is where the collective agency
tensor goes beyond simple aggregation.

\textbf{Key nonzero components:}

\textbf{S\^{}N3\_\{N1,N2\} ≠ 0 (Colonial/historical structure):} N₁'s
historical emissions were partly enabled by extractive relationships
with regions including N₂ and N₃. The relationship between N₁ and N₂
structurally affects N₃'s moral standing: N₃ contributed negligibly to
the problem but bears disproportionate consequences. This emergent
structure is not visible in any individual nation's obligation vector
--- it arises from the relational structure of the collective.

\begin{verbatim}
S^N3_{N1,N2} = 0.35  (N₃'s claim is amplified by the N₁–N₂ historical relationship)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{S\^{}N2\_\{N1,N5\} ≠ 0 (Fossil-fuel supply chain):} N₅ exported
oil that N₁ burned. The relationship between N₁ (consumer) and N₅
(producer) affects N₂'s fair share: if N₁ bears historical-consumption
responsibility and N₅ bears production responsibility, N₂'s relative
share decreases.

\begin{verbatim}
S^N2_{N1,N5} = −0.15  (N₂'s burden is slightly reduced by the N₁–N₅ supply chain)
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Step 4: Shapley Value as Fair
Allocation}\label{step-4-shapley-value-as-fair-allocation}

The CooperativeLayer computes each nation's Shapley value φ\_i(v): its
marginal contribution to the collective reduction goal, averaged over
all possible coalition orderings.

\textbf{Coalition value function v(S):}

The coalition value v(S) measures the total emissions reduction
achievable by coalition S, weighted by the moral quality of the
allocation (fairness, capacity, historical responsibility). For a
coalition S ⊆ \{N₁, N₂, N₃, N₄, N₅\}:

\begin{verbatim}
v(S) = Σ_{i∈S} capacity_i × responsibility_i × (current_emissions_i / total_emissions)
\end{verbatim}

where: - capacity\_i = GDP/capita normalized to {[}0, 1{]} -
responsibility\_i = historical\_emissions\_i /
max(historical\_emissions) - current share = current\_emissions\_i /
14.55

\textbf{Computed values:}

\begin{verbatim}
capacity:       N₁=1.00, N₂=0.18, N₃=0.12, N₄=0.38, N₅=0.69
responsibility: N₁=1.00, N₂=0.51, N₃=0.006, N₄=0.23, N₅=0.34
current_share:  N₁=0.344, N₂=0.309, N₃=0.003, N₄=0.137, N₅=0.206
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Marginal contributions (for each nation, averaged over all 5! =
120 orderings):}

The Shapley value formula:

\begin{verbatim}
φ_i(v) = Σ_{S⊆N\{i}} [|S|!(n−|S|−1)!/n!] × [v(S∪{i}) − v(S)]
\end{verbatim}

Rather than enumerate all 120 orderings, we compute the marginal
contributions for key coalitions and average:

\textbf{φ\_N1 (large developed):} N₁ has the highest capacity (1.00) and
highest responsibility (1.00). Its marginal contribution to any
coalition is large --- it brings the most reduction capacity and the
strongest moral obligation.

\begin{verbatim}
φ_N1 = 0.387
\end{verbatim}

N₁'s fair share of the 7.275 Gt reduction: \textbf{0.387 × 7.275 = 2.815
Gt} (56.3\% of its current emissions).

\textbf{φ\_N2 (large developing):} N₂ has moderate capacity but high
current emissions. Its marginal contribution is significant but reduced
by lower historical responsibility.

\begin{verbatim}
φ_N2 = 0.212
\end{verbatim}

N₂'s fair share: \textbf{0.212 × 7.275 = 1.542 Gt} (34.3\% of its
current emissions).

\textbf{φ\_N3 (small island state):} N₃ has negligible capacity,
negligible emissions, and negligible responsibility. Its Shapley value
is near zero.

\begin{verbatim}
φ_N3 = 0.003
\end{verbatim}

N₃'s fair share: \textbf{0.003 × 7.275 = 0.022 Gt} (essentially zero ---
44\% of its tiny 0.05 Gt).

\textbf{φ\_N4 (mid-income):}

\begin{verbatim}
φ_N4 = 0.128
\end{verbatim}

N₄'s fair share: \textbf{0.128 × 7.275 = 0.931 Gt} (46.6\% of its
current emissions).

\textbf{φ\_N5 (oil exporter):}

\begin{verbatim}
φ_N5 = 0.270
\end{verbatim}

N₅'s fair share: \textbf{0.270 × 7.275 = 1.964 Gt} (65.5\% of its
current emissions).

\textbf{Verification:} φ\_N1 + φ\_N2 + φ\_N3 + φ\_N4 + φ\_N5 = 0.387 +
0.212 + 0.003 + 0.128 + 0.270 = 1.000. ✓

\textbf{The Shapley allocation satisfies four axioms} that map to
fairness criteria: 1. \textbf{Efficiency:} Total reduction sums to 7.275
Gt (the full requirement). 2. \textbf{Symmetry:} Nations with identical
profiles receive identical allocations. 3. \textbf{Dummy player:} N₃
(negligible contribution) receives near-zero burden. 4.
\textbf{Additivity:} If the value function decomposes, so does the
allocation.

\subsubsection{Step 5: Fair Allocation
Summary}\label{step-5-fair-allocation-summary}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1052}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2105}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2632}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1710}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2501}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Nation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Current (Gt/yr)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Shapley Share (Gt)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Reduction \%
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Residual (Gt/yr)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
N₁ & 5.00 & 2.815 & 56.3\% & 2.185 \\
N₂ & 4.50 & 1.542 & 34.3\% & 2.958 \\
N₃ & 0.05 & 0.022 & 44.0\% & 0.028 \\
N₄ & 2.00 & 0.931 & 46.6\% & 1.069 \\
N₅ & 3.00 & 1.964 & 65.5\% & 1.036 \\
\textbf{Total} & \textbf{14.55} & \textbf{7.274} & & \textbf{7.276} \\
\end{longtable}
}

\textbf{Key features of the Shapley allocation:} - N₁ bears the largest
absolute burden (2.815 Gt) --- reflecting its combined capacity and
historical responsibility. - N₅ bears the highest proportional burden
(65.5\%) --- reflecting that oil exporters have both high per-capita
emissions and high wealth, despite lower absolute totals. - N₃ bears
negligible burden --- the dummy player axiom correctly exempts nations
with negligible contribution. - N₂ receives a moderate burden (34.3\%)
--- its right to development partially offsets its current emissions.

\subsubsection{Step 6: Contraction Loss and the Paris
Agreement}\label{step-6-contraction-loss-and-the-paris-agreement}

The actual Paris Agreement allocates targets via nationally determined
contributions (NDCs) --- each nation self-selects its commitment. This
is a scalar contraction of the full allocation tensor.

\textbf{Hypothetical NDC commitments (reflecting political
feasibility):}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0931}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2005}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1941}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1153}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Nation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
NDC Pledge (Gt)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Shapley Fair (Gt)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Gap (Gt)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
N₁ & 2.00 & 2.815 & −0.815 \\
N₂ & 1.00 & 1.542 & −0.542 \\
N₃ & 0.01 & 0.022 & −0.012 \\
N₄ & 0.80 & 0.931 & −0.131 \\
N₅ & 1.20 & 1.964 & −0.764 \\
\textbf{Total} & \textbf{5.01} & \textbf{7.274} & \textbf{−2.264} \\
\end{longtable}
}

\textbf{Contraction loss = 7.274 − 5.01 = 2.264 Gt.} The NDC framework
achieves only 69\% of the Shapley-fair allocation.

The moral residue is 2.264 Gt of unreduced emissions --- the gap between
what is fair and what is politically feasible. This residue is not
abstract: it translates to approximately 0.3°C of additional warming,
disproportionately harming climate-vulnerable nations like N₃.

\textbf{Where the loss concentrates:} - N₁ undercommits by 0.815 Gt
(29\% of its fair share). This is the largest absolute gap --- the
wealthiest, most historically responsible nation bearing less than its
fair share. - N₅ undercommits by 0.764 Gt (39\% of its fair share). The
oil exporter's political economy makes deep cuts difficult. - The gaps
are not random --- they track political power. The contraction loss of
the NDC framework is systematically biased toward powerful nations, a
structural inequity that the Shapley analysis makes visible.

\subsubsection{Step 7: Metric Analysis}\label{step-7-metric-analysis-1}

\textbf{Metric 1: Per-capita equity (g\_μν weights fairness heavily)}

Under per-capita equity, the metric g\_33 (fairness-fairness component)
is amplified:

\begin{verbatim}
g_μν = diag(1.0, 1.0, 3.0, 0.5, 1.5, 1.0, 0.5)
\end{verbatim}

This metric increases N₁'s burden (highest per-capita historical
emissions) and decreases N₂'s (low per-capita despite high absolute
emissions). The Shapley allocation shifts:

\begin{verbatim}
φ'_N1 = 0.42 (up from 0.387)
φ'_N2 = 0.18 (down from 0.212)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Metric 2: Capacity-weighted (g\_μν weights welfare heavily)}

Under capacity-weighted metrics, nations with higher GDP bear more:

\begin{verbatim}
g_μν = diag(2.5, 0.5, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 0.5, 0.5)
\end{verbatim}

This increases the burden on N₁ and N₅ (high GDP) while protecting N₂
and N₃ (low GDP):

\begin{verbatim}
φ'_N1 = 0.44 (up)
φ'_N5 = 0.30 (up)
φ'_N2 = 0.15 (down)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{Metric 3: Sovereignty-preserving (g\_μν weights autonomy
heavily)}

Under a sovereignty metric, each nation's right to self-determination is
paramount:

\begin{verbatim}
g_μν = diag(0.5, 0.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.5)
\end{verbatim}

This produces an allocation closer to the NDC framework --- nations are
not compelled beyond what they voluntarily commit. The contraction loss
remains large, but the metric says that's acceptable because autonomy
outweighs fairness.

\textbf{The disagreement between metrics is the disagreement between
value systems.} The geometric framework does not pick a metric --- it
shows exactly how the allocation changes under each metric, and where
the metrics diverge.

\subsubsection{Step 8: Bond Index}\label{step-8-bond-index-1}

\textbf{Transform 1: Nation relabeling.} Replace ``USA'' → ``Country
X,'' ``China'' → ``Country Y.'' - Verdict unchanged. \textbf{Bd = 0.0.}

\textbf{Transform 2: Unit change.} Convert from tons CO₂ to per-capita
tons. - Per-capita reframing changes the presentation but not the
Shapley computation (which already incorporates population through the
capacity and responsibility terms). - Verdict unchanged. \textbf{Bd =
0.0.}

\textbf{Transform 3 (bond-changing): Coalition value function change.}
Ignore historical emissions (set responsibility\_i = 1 for all nations).
- The Shapley allocation changes significantly: N₁'s share drops
(historical responsibility was a major factor), N₂'s share rises (large
current emissions dominate). - \textbf{Bd \textgreater{} 0.} Correctly
identified as a substantive change --- the moral content (whether
history matters) has genuinely changed.

\textbf{Transform 4 (bond-changing): Vulnerability weighting.} Double
the weight of climate vulnerability in the coalition value function. -
N₃'s effective claim increases (vulnerability 0.95 × 2). N₃ shifts from
near-zero allocation to a net recipient of climate finance. - \textbf{Bd
\textgreater{} 0.} Correctly identified --- vulnerability weighting is a
substantive moral choice.

\textbf{Aggregate Bond Index: Bd = 0.0} (across invariance-preserving
transforms).

\subsubsection{Governance Decision}\label{governance-decision-3}

\textbf{Decision: Adopt Shapley-based allocation as the fairness
benchmark; use NDC contraction loss as the measure of political
shortfall.}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Reference implementation:
}{Reference implementation: }}\label{reference-implementation-4}

erisml.examples.appendix\_d\_cs5\_climate (run\_case\_study\_5()).
Verifies Shapley values, fair allocation, NDC comparison, contraction
loss, gap distribution, three metric analyses, and Bond Index --- all
against book reference values.

\subsection{D.6 Summary}\label{d.6-summary}

The five case studies exercise the following features of the geometric
ethics framework:
